# Thread: How much morning sun for U1a1 material haplogroup living at 41 latitude?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-morning-sun-for-u1a1-material-haplogroup-living-at-41-latitude.31012/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nickhae — 2025-05-29T23:41:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-morning-sun-for-u1a1-material-haplogroup-living-at-41-latitude.31012/post-353540>

Hi! Title says it all. Curious is there is a recommendation for how much morning sun to get with U1a1 haplogroup currently living at 41 degrees latitude? From what I can tell, this haplogroup lived at 21 to 40 degrees 10,000 years ago. My mother, grandmother, and great-grandmother are all from northern Sweden. Mother is alive and relatively well at 71 years old (spent a lot of time in SE Asia, South Africa, and currently lives in Brisbane, Australia). Grandmother died from Alzheimer's at just under 90 and great-grandmother lived well into her 90s (both lived in Sweden).

Anything else needed to determine my morning sun needs? I am in what I think is very good health, but would like to go from very good to exceptional. Already do CT, get plenty of sun when I can, and do my best to mitigate nnEMF and blue light (but can and will do better on that), and I am starting to add in morning light now.

Appreciate any help / insight!

### Jack Kruse — 2025-05-30T00:16:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-morning-sun-for-u1a1-material-haplogroup-living-at-41-latitude.31012/post-353543>

The U1a1 haplotype is a subclade of the mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) haplogroup U1, which itself is a branch ofthe broader haplogroup U. Haplogroup U is one of the oldest and most widespread mtDNA haplogroups inEurasia, originating from haplogroup R approximately 43,000–50,000 years ago during the early Upper Paleolithic period. The U1 subclade, including U1a1, is estimated to have arisen between 26,000 and 37,000 years ago and is characterized by low frequencies across Europe, with higher prevalence in Eastern Europe,Anatolia, the Near East, and parts of South Asia, such as the Kerala region in India.

Bayesian Skyline Plots and phylogenetic analyses indicate that U1a1’s expansions were distinct from other U subclades, with no clear evidence of shared demographic histories with haplogroups like M1 or U6. Its presence in ancient populations and low modern frequency suggest it may have been more widespread in the past but was subject to genetic drift or population bottlenecks.

Mitochondrial DNA influences energy metabolism and ROS production, key sources of biophotons. U1a1, as a subclade of haplogroup U, may have specific polymorphisms affecting mitochondrial complex I or cytochrome c oxidase activity, potentially altering ROS levels.
Studies (e.g., on haplogroup U) suggest U subclades may confer adaptations to colder climates or dietary stress, possibly increasing mitochondrial ROS production under oxidative stress. However, no specific data links U1a1 to unique ROS or biophoton profiles.
Probability Impact: U1a1 likely contributes to a baseline biophoton emission rate similar to other U subclades, with a slight increase (10–20%) in ROS-related emissions due to potential metabolic adaptations to historical environments (e.g., Southwest Asia or Eastern Europe).

So if they have diseases they likely need 2 hours of AM sunrise and one hour before sunset.

Environmental factors at 41°N suggest a moderate biophoton emission rate, with a 60–70% probability of emissions falling within the standard range for human cells (10–100 photons/s/cm²), adjusted downward by antioxidant intake by a local mediterrean diet and upward by urban light stress and nnEMF footrpint. If you are in Sweden you are not at 41N latitude.

### nickhae — 2025-05-30T16:02:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-morning-sun-for-u1a1-material-haplogroup-living-at-41-latitude.31012/post-353574>

Thanks Uncle Jack! I am in coastal Connecticut, no longer in Sweden. If I have no diseases, what does the 2hr sunrise / 1hr sunset change to? Trying to find the bookends and then optimize within those bounds. Many thanks!

### Jack Kruse — 2025-05-30T18:47:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-morning-sun-for-u1a1-material-haplogroup-living-at-41-latitude.31012/post-353576>

Coastal CT is terrible due to Groton Seabase which has some of the worse power densities I have ever tested. Always between 10,000 - 1000,000 milli Watts per meter squared.

### Mightygumshoe — 2025-07-24T18:12:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-successfully-reverse-parkinson%E2%80%99s.31013/post-356903>

I just "skimmed" a "non peer reviewed piece about Parkinson's Disease (PD) and Deuterium

**Title: Mitochondrial Dysfunction in Parkinson’s Disease: Is Impaired Deuterium-depleted Nutrient Supply by Gut Microbes a Primary Factor?

Link:** [**https://www.preprints.org/manuscript/202504.1079/v1**](https://www.preprints.org/manuscript/202504.1079/v1)

"It is likely beneficial to take deuterium depleted water (DDW) on a regular basis, both to prevent and to treat PD. The PC12 tumor cell line has become a popular choice for research in PD, because these cells synthesize, store and release dopamine. An in vitro study on PC12 cells evaluating the potential for DDW to protect from PD showed that it attenuated apoptosis induced by H2O2 exposure, reduced reactive oxygen species, and increased the activity of the protective enzymes catalase and CuZn-superoxide dismutase \[[137](https://www.preprints.org/manuscript/202504.1079/v1#B137-preprints-155823)\].

Regular aerobic exercise has the potential to prevent prodromal conditions that can lead to development of PD \[[138](https://www.preprints.org/manuscript/202504.1079/v1#B138-preprints-155823)\]. Animal studies have shown that, during Parkinsonism, exercise helps to evoke adaptive neuroplasticity in basal ganglia \[[139](https://www.preprints.org/manuscript/202504.1079/v1#B139-preprints-155823)\]. **Vitamin D has been shown to play a significant role in mitochondrial health \[[140](https://www.preprints.org/manuscript/202504.1079/v1#B140-preprints-155823)\]. Spending more time outdoors in the sunlight to promote vitamin D synthesis in the skin decreases the risk of PD \[[141](https://www.preprints.org/manuscript/202504.1079/v1#B141-preprints-155823)\]"
....**

there's a lot more in the article. I have been reading Uncle Jack's forum, some blog posts etc. and happened on this elsewhere....was looking into DDW because we just ordered/received some, Saw, gut, Parkinson's etc....so skimmed it. I have an in-law with Parkinson's and another in-law with early symptoms.

I am not a doc....and don't pretend to be one, except once at age 4. Peace.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-08-12T15:49:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-successfully-reverse-parkinson%E2%80%99s.31013/post-358034>

This blog is about why the SUN is TINA for healing of all diseases.

[https://www.patreon.com/posts/decentralized-66-135709978](https://www.patreon.com/posts/decentralized-66-135709978)

Photorepair, also known as photoreactivation, is a DNA repair mechanism that uses visible light to reverse UV-induced DNA damage, specifically pyrimidine dimers. It's a process found in many organisms, but not in placental mammals, which rely on nucleotide excision repair. Photorepair involves enzymes called photolyases that bind to damaged DNA and, upon absorbing light energy, to break the abnormal bonds, restoring the original DNA structure.

No matter the disease you have this is your how and why.

This new blog today on the quantum biology of photorepair is remarkable. I will show you what planting of seeds in a fertile mind can do to a sock human.

Don't fear burning things down today. Today’s ashes are tomorrow’s soil. Moments are like seeds to place in that soil. Any moment’s beginning ends in a moment....and the seed transforms into some new, something different, something unique. The moral of the story of the seed is if you want something new, you have to stop doing something old.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-12T14:54:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-successfully-reverse-parkinson%E2%80%99s.31013/post-362539>

If Parkinson’s was purely genetic or bad luck, you wouldn’t see a geographic gradient like this, would you?

It's not randomly distributed either.

Observe this. It presence is very low at equator ask yourself why?

Much more prevalent the farther you get from the equator

Think about that for a moment. WHY?

There’s a clear pattern here. And it shows up in many other chronic diseases too

I can show you disease maps for MS, diabetes, obesity, autoimmune conditions showing the same geographic gradient

The pattern follows stellar light of the sun.

Parkinson’s is a melanin storage disorder due to a lack of sunlight that manifests as a dopamine disorder.

The top line of the slide shows it clear as day.

And dopamine IS NOT just a neurotransmitter. Its a light driven signal as the slide shows.

UV light builds melanin + dopamine tone
Melanin is the storage form dopamine
Circadian rhythm sets dopamine metabolism

So what does the equator have where Parkinsons rates are lowest?

\- strong UV year round = more consistent dopamine signaling and more melanin = light stability.
\- Most neurodegenerative conditions are do to a LACK OF LIGHT STABILITY.
\- consistent 12 hours days and 12 hour nights for better circadian timing

You lose both these the further you travel from the equator

And so you get lower dopamine reserve & more neurodegeneration pressure

**_I have consitently said for 20 years the best Rx is a move to the tropics._** You must get your skin and eyes in Nature's game daily, wherever you live, to give your biology a chance to operate the way its supposed to.

Hi Jack,
Firstly - I want to thank-you immensely. I am new here, but have spent tens of tens of hours and hours listening to your incredible podcasts. I have a nice close up picture, like your podcasts asked so you can see who I am. I am from Australia, living in Brisbane, and did study science at University 15 years ago, however, a lot of the knowledge is beyond my capabilities to understand, and has been giving me a very sore head, but I'm still trying. Have gone to the library, and borrowed some physics books.
My Mum has Parkinson's, is 67 years of age, was diagnosed at 59. She worked as an ICU Nurse for 40 years until she was diagnosed and had to retire. She is the most beautiful soul, kindest heart, and I can't count the number of times I have cried myself to sleep over her illness - it is the saddest thing in my life.
Can I please kindly add to this thread - will the light allow the melanin to go back into the brain if she watches the sunrise for an hour every day? How long would she do this until she sees results? If she has had Deep Brain Surgery - will this impact anything? Is there anything additional to add to the protocol for Parkinsons like Meth Blue, and if so, is it impacted by the DBS? I really really believe everything you have said about POMC and melanin, blue light and the KT event on the podcasts.
It jusst seems that every neurosurgeon I have met has said that its degenerative, i.e. you can slow it down, but you can't repair it. I really please would love to know if you've seen otherwise (I really believe everything you say, and do - it makes so much sense to me and I feel it in my soul - I just was wondering if maybe you could please add just 1 story or some tiny info / timelines?
Many thanks, and please don't stop ever doing what you do, Ella x

### Jack Kruse — 2026-04-18T17:35:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-successfully-reverse-parkinson%E2%80%99s.31013/post-371416>

**Truncal Vagotomy did show an effect on decreased PD and the reason should be obvious why it did.**

Truncal Vagotomy did show an effect on decreased PD and the reason should be obvious. There was no back load allowed of deuterium from a denervated stomach and pancrease where the 2 L of bicarb were made to flush the dueterium out in a Bristol stool number 4. This stopped the backwash into the CNS via the floor of the 4th ventricle to affect the basal ganglia. Truncal Vagotomy is effectively a "circuit breaker" for the Deuterium Backflow.

By severing the truncal connection, surgeons accidentally created an isotopic firewall. If the Braak hypothesis says the pathology starts in the gut, my thesis explains the mechanism with surgical precision: the gut is the primary "Deuterium Sink" of the body. In many podcasts I have talked about my training in General surgery before my Oral surgery and neurosurgery residency. I got stuck doing two years of this crap instead of one. It was here I learned a lot about gastric vagal function.

I am so old that Centralized medicine still was doing vagotomies for peptic ulcer disease. This fueled my interest in the vagus nerve and eventually fueled my early adoption of VNS for brain diseases. Recall that the pancreas produces roughly 2 liters of bicarbonate-rich fluid daily. This isn't just for pH; it is the vagal exhaust fluid.

**The Flush:** A healthy, vagally-driven system uses this 2L loop to fractionate and "flush" the heavy trash (2H) out of the GI tract, resulting in a perfect Bristol Stool Type 4. This is where the KIE receipt should be in your life, not in your brain!!! When you cut the nerve it stops the back wash of D+ entering the CNS through the Area Postrema, the "chemoreceptor trigger zone" on the floor of the 4th ventricle. People forget that Area Postrema has no BBB so D+ gets in no problem and then D+ concentrates in the lateral ventricles right next to the basal ganglion.

By performing a Truncal Vagotomy, the "highway" for this heavy backwash is physically removed by general surgeons. I did hundreds of these surgeries in two years.

I saw PD improve in some and it was a stunner. This is when I got the idea when VNS came to market I was going to use them on neurodegeneration patients. I believe the same mechanism is operational in ALS patients and the only difference here is the D+ is getting flushed down the obex into the central canal and the subarachoid space to create a lattice lock of their CSF there.

When you cut the vagus he "Deuterium Bomb" in the gut is no longer connected to the Cranial Isotopic Refinery.

This is why PD improved when we severed their nerves.

Even if the gut is "heavy," the Basal Ganglia are spared the isotopic influx, preserving the Topological Sovereignty of the brain's motor centers. This is why so many people post vagotomy got gall stones, dumping syndrome, and gastroparesis. These gut organs were being loaded with D+. In fact my first ever no trauma splenic rupture occured in a post vagotomy patient two weeks after we performed the procedure. This was my education at Charity hosptal. Irreplaceable.
The Failure: When the Vagal Bus collapses (due to SAA declination or nnEMF), the "exhaust" stalls. The heavy water accumulates in the gut, increasing the viscosity of the enteric fluid and "bleaching" the local melanin where deuterium has an effect on the basal ganglia. This was a comment made on this blog below.

[https://www.patreon.com/posts/decentralized-155380212](https://www.patreon.com/posts/decentralized-155380212)

# Thread: Pollack - CSF - Primovascular System: Pathways of Light in the body
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pollack-csf-primovascular-system-pathways-of-light-in-the-body.31025/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BrunoC — 2025-05-31T13:16:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pollack-csf-primovascular-system-pathways-of-light-in-the-body.31025/post-353631>

Hi, Jack.

Very briefly, I am a French MD/DO (born in Paris), after 25 years in the USA I now live in Ecuador (Vilcabamba). I became a gold member recently.

1\. If you are interested, a very interesting article about CSF, finally showing the continuity CSF / peripheral nerves comparments.
The continuity can be found only by injecting 1,9 gold nano particles in the lateral ventricles (the approximate size of what is regularly found in the CSF: albumin, glucose, neurotrophic factors) not with much larger particles (e.g.ink), as they tried before.

"Cerebrospinal fluid flow extends to peripheral nerves further unifying the nervous system. 2024 Sep 6;10(36):eadn3259. doi: 10.1126/sciadv.adn3259. Epub 2024 Sep 4."
The full article is here: [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11373606/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11373606/)

2\. I am not sure you know, Gerald pollack did some experiment with DDW, he said it also build EZ water.

I interviewed him in the past, he told me this March 1st:
<<We just finished testing DDW. It builds EZ water.
We’re now constructing the manuscript. Should be submitted for publication in a few weeks.
Best,
Jerry>>

3\. Another passion of mine: the PVS, it may add a nice piece to your amazing work.

The primo-vascular system (PVS) constitute a very unique anatomo-physiologic system, depicted as a novel circulatory system, with primo-vessels and primo-fluid, clearly set apart from those of the arterio-venous, lymphatic, and cerebrospinal systems.

It seems to carry photons, DNA/RNA fragments, has immune functions (it carries a large quantity of small adult stem cells), some endocrine functions and is found deeply involved in pathological processes including inflammation and cancer.

You can look at it on pubmed or read a peer-reviewed article I wrote a while ago: The Primo Vascular System, A Unique Human Biological System Shifting Medical Paradigm - Prospective Osteopathic Applications for Cranial, Visceral, Lymphatic, and Fascial Techniques, Chikly B, Roberts P, Quaghebeur J...

The full article in my website, for example:

### [Peer-reviewed Journal: The Primo Vascular System, A Unique Human Biological System Shifting Medical Paradigm - Prospective Osteopathic Applications for Cranial, Visceral, Lymphatic, and Fascial Techniques, Chikly B, Roberts P, Quaghebeur J | Chikly H](https://chiklyinstitute.com/index.php/Research-Articles/peer-reviewed-journal-primo-vascular-system-unique-human-biological-system)

Peer-reviewed Journal: Chikly B, Roberts P, Quaghebeur J. The Primo Vascular System: A Unique Human Biological System Shifting Medical Paradigm - Prospective Osteopathic Applications for Cranial, Visceral, Lymphatic, and Fascial Techniques. Journal of American Osteopathic Association (JAOA)...

 chiklyinstitute.com

I hope it is of some interest to you

### Jack Kruse — 2025-05-31T22:57:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pollack-csf-primovascular-system-pathways-of-light-in-the-body.31025/post-353651>

Just so you are aware, I am aware of this. And here is a passage from a future blog about it.

**The Eye as a Quantum Gateway, the PVS as a Decentralized Conduit, and the Perils of Artificial Light**
The Eye: A Quantum Gateway for Light-Driven Biology
The eye is not merely a camera but a quantum gateway that orchestrates the body’s circadian and metabolic rhythms through light. The cornea, with its UV-A-sensitive neuropsin, and the retina, with melanopsin, form a semiconducting circuit that processes sunlight (0.89–3.94 eV) to regulate the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN). The SCN, in turn, influences the leptin-melanocortin pathways (metabolic balance) and the habenular nucleus (mood regulation). Sunlight’s broad spectrum, UV-A, IR-A, and NIR—activates these photoreceptors, generating ultraweak photon emissions (UPEs, 200–800 nm) that maintain quantum coherence, as Fritz-Albert Popp described. In the retina, mitochondria use UV-A to regenerate photoreceptors, while melanin in the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) splits water, producing protons and electrons that fuel the Z-Z proton tunneling pathway in cytochrome c oxidase (CCO) for ATP synthesis. This light-driven process ensures mitochondrial health, supports circadian synchronization, and maintains systemic redox potential via the blood.

**The Primo Vascular System: A Decentralized Quantum Conduit**

The Primo Vascular System (PVS) extends the eye’s quantum signaling throughout the body, acting as a decentralized network that links the outer world to internal organs via the circulatory system. Found in intravascular and extravascular spaces, on organ surfaces, and within the central nervous system, the PVS consists of thread-like vessels and nodes that transport embryonic-like cells (P-microcells) and signaling molecules like exosomes. These vessels, rich in fluid-filled channels, are ideally suited to conduct UPEs generated by melanin in the eye. When UV-A light (3.1–3.94 eV) hits the cornea and RPE, melanin absorbs it, splitting water into H⁺, e⁻, and oxygen, while emitting biophotons. The PVS distributes these biophotons and associated proton/electron flows to distal tissues, enhancing mitochondrial function and redox potential. This aligns with our thesis’s emphasis on UPEs as coherent signals, one photon triggering 10⁹ reactions (Popp), and the Z-Z pathway’s role in ATP production, which is amplified by UV-A and IR-A light.

The PVS’s periodic fluid flow (15–30 seconds) facilitate the movement of DC electric currents, as described by Becker, which are enhanced by UV-A and IR-A. These currents, carried by protons and electrons from melanin’s water-splitting, support tissue regeneration and metabolic coupling across the body. The PVS’s presence in blood vessels underscores the circulatory system’s role as a “cosmic river,”, with red blood cells creating UPEs while performing phototaxis toward UV-A light, amplifying the eye’s light signals systemically to the surfaces of organs to awaken the power within them.

# Thread: Dr. John Ott: The Light Side of HealthBy The Mother Earth News Editors|Published on January 1, 1986
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-john-ott-the-light-side-of-healthby-the-mother-earth-news-editors-published-on-january-1-1986.31029/>
**Subforum:** The Kruse Longevity Center
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Stuart Briscoe — 2025-05-31T23:15:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-john-ott-the-light-side-of-healthby-the-mother-earth-news-editors-published-on-january-1-1986.31029/post-353654>

### [Dr. John Ott: The Light Side of Health – Mother Earth News](https://www.motherearthnews.com/sustainable-living/nature-and-environment/john-ott-zm0z86zhun/)

A Plowboy Interview with John Ott, a former banker, whose hobby of time-lapse photography lead him to study the ways in which light can, in the proper spectral balance, enhance the health of plants, animals, and even humans.

www.motherearthnews.com

The Sarasota Study, which Dr. Kruse refers to, from Dr. Ott's research:

Dr. Kruse' recent podcast where he mentions John Ott's work, the Sarasota Experiment, and Ott's books.

**PLOWBOY:** You put full spectrum lights into classrooms?

**OTT:** Yes, we did a study in the Sarasota County school system in 1973; we placed radiation-shielded full-spectrum lights in two rooms for five months and left the standard cool white fluorescent tubes in two control group classrooms. Our experiment found that in the groups under our lights, several extremely hyperactive children calmed down completely and were able to overcome their learning disabilities. Interestingly enough, the children in that classroom also developed one third fewer dental cavities than the control group during that five month period! We used time-lapse cameras in each classroom to record incidences of disruptive behavior, and the fluorescent classrooms always had more.

A similar kind of experiment was conducted in 1975 in a California public school and another in Washington State; they had similar results.

Benefit of Natural Sunshine:

**PLOWBOY:** Besides shielding the television, what can the average person — someone who maybe works in an office or spends a lot of time with computers and virtually lives indoors — do to reduce the health hazards in his or her life … short of moving to Tahiti?

**OTT:** Just get outdoors in natural light as much as possible. A minimum of six hours a day of natural daylight would be ideal. Now, I know that would be very difficult to achieve for people living in big cities and those who have indoor jobs, but there are ways to at least increase your exposure to full-spectrum light. Open windows whenever possible. And remember, being outside doesn’t necessarily mean lying in direct sunlight. You can get full benefit of natural daylight by sitting and reading under a shade tree or eating on a screened porch. Sleeping on a screened porch is wonderful, too. Outdoor sports are highly recommended when you have the time.

Some of John Ott's books: (_My Ivory Cellar_, 1958; _Health and Light_, 1973; and _Light, Radiation, and You_, 1982).

Thank you!!

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-john-ott-the-light-side-of-healthby-the-mother-earth-news-editors-published-on-january-1-1986.31029/post-357596>

What Decentralized Medicine Insights on CATARACT formation are included in this new masterpiece blog for Savage consumption?

[https://www.patreon.com/posts/decentralized-65-124070324](https://www.patreon.com/posts/decentralized-65-124070324)

Changes in light input via the lens predict epigenetic programming. Blocked sunlight disrupts histone modifications and DNA methylation, contributing to modern diseases MAHA could never solve because of their myopic focus on food.

The creeping blindness of cataract formation reflects a broader metaphor in modern life: modern comforts (e.g., technology abuse, centralized medicine) suppress the discomfort of poor thinking; poor thinking and poor outcomes should lead to fostering wisdom and identity. Avoiding this decentralized struggle (e.g., via opioids over sunlight) poisons resilience and creativity.

Melanin, Melatonin, and Vitamin D Deficiency: Hypothyroidism reduces Coulomb charge in the skin and lens due to low melanin, melatonin, an an altered VDR, acting in unison to alter how Gauss law can be used and this impairs charge collection in cells leading to disease. This process opacifies the lens, causing cataracts.

Stars require darkness to shine, just as human biology needs light-dark cycles for optimal function. An opaque lens diminishes this balance, reducing the relative power of light to drive epigenetic programming and cellular evolution. Modern artificial lighting (e.g., blue light overuse) exacerbates this, further distorting the light-dark tension essential for health.

Leptin-Melanocortin Pathway: Leptin stimulates TRH via the leptin-melanocortin pathway, boosting α-MSH and POMC, which are essential for melanin production. In hypothyroidism, this pathway is disrupted, leading to pale skin, lens opacification, and increased sunburn risk. A cataract gives you a ciliary ganglionectomy just like sunglasses do and this leads to many diseases in modern humans.

Thermoregulation and TRH: TRH acts as a neurotransmitter, inducing hyperthermia when injected intracerebroventricularly. This heat stress, combined with a diminished heat sink (e.g., in the retina and lens), heightens cataract risk because it alters the production of high signal UPEs in the eyes and central retinal pathways leading to more diseases you never saw coming.

### Chlo_e — 2025-06-02T20:35:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/universities.31037/post-353777>

Maybe a slightly different perspective (I don't have kiddos) - but gaining knowledge and experience of centralised systems is not necessarily a waste of time, as it can offer a vantage point for individuals to enact change from within, and/or allow individuals to navigate such systems as to undo corrupt practices, and/or equip individuals to offer a better model etc. JK is an example of this. As was Andrew Marino when he went to study law after being on the receiving end of corrupt, corporate-captured legal battles.

I don't know how it is in the US- but apprenticeships and entrepreneurial ventures are alternatives to university in other countries.

If you meant safety in terms of health- plenty of blogs and forum discussions to guide the decision-making process.

# Thread: Intro: Mornington Peninsula, Australia - Dad w/Metastatic Melanoma in Brain

### MayaB — 2025-06-03T03:05:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/intro-mornington-peninsula-australia-dad-w-metastatic-melanoma-in-brain.31039/post-353813>

New member saying hello. Based on the beach on the Mornington Peninsula in Victoria, Australia. Work as an archaeologist, have 2 kids.

Spent the last year fighting for my dad - he was diagnosed May24 with metastatic melanoma with brain metastasis, age 70 otherwise fit (orginally had melanoma removed from his skin when he was in his 40s). The oncologists tried immunotherapy but they had to stop after 2 rounds because it caused kidney failure, type 1 diabetes after destroying the pancreas, and chrons symptoms. They gave him 3-5 months to live.

I put him on deuterium depleted water immediately (85ppm for 3 months/65ppm for 5 months/now 45ppm). He is still here 12 months later and now has zero nuerological symptoms (the cancer originally left him unable to walk). Of the four tumours which caused the most concern and were each over 2cm - well they are now all under 1cm (approx 0.8 - 0.6cm at last scan). The oncologist has no explanantion and of course refuses to read one single paper I have sent her on DDW (I'm wasting my time I KNOW). I also have my dad obeying natures light schedule. He now has vitiligo which means the melanin is at work. But he feels uneasy about sitting in direct sunlight so that is my ongoing battle.

I love learning and I look forward to doing so in this space with Dr Jack and the rest of the community.

My Dad's cancer story is underpinned by messed up light. Escaping from Eastern Europe to Australia at 16 (compltely different light/water environment), entering the work force as a postman where shift work was the frame. Up at 3am each day, hours under fluro lights sorting mail, out in the daylight in sunglasses and full clothing to deliver mail, home by the afternoon, repeat. In later years was given an office job with the postoffice under lights and infront of a computer all day.

Australia a desert yes but most of the population lives along the coasts and east of the great dividing range (with great volcanic history) with high rainfaill. Far North QLD has the wet tropics and worlds oldest continuest rainforest (180 million years) - definitely my fav area but perhaps just shy of truly equatorial.

The true desert is in all of our living and working environments and the minds of the "doctors". The onocologist gets excited about the vitiligo my dad has developed knowing full well that the melanin is migrating to where it is needed but has no desire to join the dots any further to the story of light staring her in the face.

# Thread: Metacritique of Influential Studies Purporting COVID-19 Vaccine Suc-
cesses: Part 1 - Watson et al
Raphael Lataster1
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metacritique-of-influential-studies-purporting-covid-19-vaccine-suc-cesses-part-1-watson-et-al-raphael-lataster1.31041/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Stuart Briscoe — 2025-06-03T14:54:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metacritique-of-influential-studies-purporting-covid-19-vaccine-suc-cesses-part-1-watson-et-al-raphael-lataster1.31041/post-353825>

**Abstract**

Despite a dearth of articles critically examining ma-

jor COVID-19 vaccine studies, this review evalu-

ates the widely cited Watson et al, which claimed

that over 14 million lives were saved globally by

COVID-19 vaccines. Several issues were identified

that generally invalidate its conclusions and raise

doubts about the actual success of the global vac-

cination program. These include the use of inaccu-

rate estimates of effectiveness and safety due to in-

adequate counting windows; lack of recognition of

.

waning effectiveness and eventual negative effec-

tiveness; failure to account for confounding varia-

bles; exaggerated infection and case fatality rates;

insufficient consideration of vaccine-related risks;

and possible financial or political conflicts of inter-

est. Multiple lines of evidence are highlighted that

suggest the risks of COVID-19 vaccines may out-

weigh the benefits, particularly in young and

healthy populations.

**Keywords:** COVID-19, COVID-19 vaccines, counting windows, risk-benefit analysis, negative effectiveness

[https://journalofindependentmedicine.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/05/ima-jim-v01-n02-a07-metacritique-of-influential-studies-purporting-covid-19-vaccine-successes-part-1-watson-et-al.pdf?utm\_source=substack&utm\_medium=email](https://journalofindependentmedicine.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/05/ima-jim-v01-n02-a07-metacritique-of-influential-studies-purporting-covid-19-vaccine-successes-part-1-watson-et-al.pdf?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email)

**Conclusion**

Watson et al has been central in shaping the domi-

nant narrative that COVID-19 vaccines have saved

tens of millions of lives worldwide. However, a

closer examination reveals a web of flawed method-

ological assumptions, unsupported estimates, and

under-discussed risks—factors that substantially

undermine this conclusion.

In Part 2, I turn to Kitano et al, a major US-based

study that similarly claims significant net benefits

of vaccination. As will become clear, many of the

same methodological and ethical concerns apply—

alongside additional issues specific to the American

context. Finally, Part 3 will explore these patterns

across several other prominent studies concerning

Europe and Oceania, ultimately highlighting con-

sistent weaknesses within the literature supporting

widespread COVID-19 vaccination.

148 [https://doi.org/10.71189/JIM/2025/V01N02A07](https://doi.org/10.71189/JIM/2025/V01N02A07)

J Indep Med 2025 Vol. 1 No.

[https://www.cell.com/.../pdf/S0962-8924(17)30146-0.pdf](https://www.cell.com/trends/cell-biology/pdf/S0962-8924\(17\)30146-0.pdf?fbclid=IwZXh0bgNhZW0CMTAAYnJpZBExRVZ2QW1vMXJXUkd3R2V0NQEeNdFgwq4R1HqyFEj560v5tAidcNGLOEIfRSmW9ZtmCmozXHnZqt6tLmJKAg4_aem_4BrzG6ST0WWYTlxvGoip4g)

**Light/dark and Charge Control:** Have you ever asked yourself the of question how light and dark affecting nanotube formation. The manner in which it happens strongly suggests a photobioelectric response. A lack of daytime light (e.g., UV-A, red/NIR) or darkness at night disrupts circadian mitochondrial function, increasing ROS and charge leakage (e.g., proton gradient collapse). Nanotubes likely fuse mitochondria to increase surface area, acting as a larger capacitor to stabilize bioelectric charge. This is supported by Liu et al., who note nanotubes enhance membrane potential (ΔΨm) under stress. This is why GDF15 is used for mitoception.

**ER Failure and Lipid Loss:** in cell membranes loaded with DHA: Prolonged nanotube activity, driven by defective mitochondria (e.g., from blue light), overtax the ER, which synthesizes lipids (e.g., DHA) for mitochondrial membranes. Loss of melatonin (nighttime antioxidant made 95% in mitochondria), melanin (photoprotection), and DHA (membrane fluidity) impairs ER function, leading to protein misfolding and stress, linking to diseases like autism, neurodegeneration, and EDS (Perkins et al., 2017, Journal of Cell Science).

**Oxygen Toxicity and Capacitance:** Nanotubes reflect mitochondrial MRO2 use and are reflection of the cell acting to reduce oxygen toxicity by buffering ROS, building a larger capacitive network to control current. This aligns with the GOE lessons of evolution, early life adapted to rising oxygen via light-sensitive structures (e.g., aromatic rings in prokaryotes, but upgraded tomicrotubules in eukaryotes), a precursor to mitochondrial nanotubes. Light is driving this all, not electrical current. Electrical currents are down stream light, dark, and temperature effects. Time to wake up Martin. Ask better questions.

"Selfish DHA" > "Selfish Gene" story. DHA was created the link for living things between thermodynamics and information theory. DHA was innovated 50 million years before the Cambrian explosion in plankton via photosynthesis. It made the ocean a hard drive of information waiting to be used in eukaryotes. DHA provides massive amounts of electrons to the cell. This allowed life to use more light. More light = more information in the system to build complexity.

This situation mimics when Shannon was first at Bell Labs: they had tons of information buried in their messages but had no way to mine or collect the information to make the data useful. Shannon thought about the problem and wrote a paper in 1948 about using mathematics to data-mine. The paper was called, "A Mathematical Theory of Communication".

This paper is really what DHA does for eukaryotes and it represents the ideas buried in my Quantum thermodynamics thesis on my Patreon blog. Once Shannon connected these dots mathematically, it opened the door to signal processing, compression and converting messages into an algorithm code to transmit them digitally. It gave rise to the Cambrian revolution in computers. DHA did the same for cells.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-08-18T23:43:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood.31045/post-358441>

**Mitochondria as Quantum Battery Masters: A Photobioelectric Framework for Energy, Information, and Consciousness**

Abstract

Mitochondria are traditionally viewed as cellular powerhouses, converting food-derived energy into ATP via the electron transport chain (ETC). However, this classical perspective overlooks their role as quantum thermodynamic hubs that manage energy and information through light-driven processes. This thesis proposes a decentralized photo-bioelectric framework, positing that mitochondria function as quantum batteries, utilizing entangled proton spins, ultraweak photon emissions (UPEs), and structured water to store and process energy and information. This framework integrates quantum biology, information theory, and AMO (atomic, molecular, and optical) physics to explain how mitochondria create negative entropy, enabling eukaryotic complexity. Furthermore, it explores the biophysics of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) and microtubules (MT) in neural systems, linking UPE-mediated quantum coherence to consciousness and examining how demyelination disrupts this system.

By framing mitochondria as quantum processors and CSF as a waveguide for UPEs, this work addresses the hard problem of consciousness—how subjective experience (qualia) arises from physical processes, and proposes novel strategies for restoring quantum coherence in neurodegenerative conditions like multiple sclerosis (MS).IntroductionMitochondria, descendants of ancient bacteria, are central to cellular energy metabolism, converting substrates like glucose and fats into ATP through oxidative phosphorylation. However, recent advances in quantum biology suggest that their role extends beyond classical biochemistry. This thesis introduces a decentralized photo-bioelectric framework, arguing that mitochondria function as quantum batteries, leveraging light, proton spins, and structured water to manage energy and information. This perspective builds on Ilya Prigogine’s dissipative structures theory, which describes how far-from-equilibrium systems create order through energy dissipation (Prigogine, 1977), and integrates information theory principles from Claude Shannon and Rolf Landauer, which establish the equivalence of energy and information (Shannon, 1948; Landauer, 1961).

The framework posits that mitochondria decode solar energy embedded in food molecules, re-emitting it as UPEs, coherent biophotons with high orbital angular momentum (OAM), to drive cellular processes. These UPEs, modulated by quantum spin states of electrons and protons, encode information, enabling mitochondria to function as quantum processors. Structured water, particularly deuterium-depleted water (DDW), acts as a protective medium, shielding entangled proton spins from decoherence. This thesis extends this model to neural systems, exploring how CSF distributes UPEs to microtubules, sustaining quantum coherence and consciousness. Demyelination, a hallmark of diseases like MS, disrupts this system, impairing UPE signaling, microtubule function, and qualia. By integrating first principles, literature, and novel hypotheses, this work aims to redefine mitochondrial function and propose quantum-based interventions for neurodegenerative conditions.

Theoretical Framework

Mitochondria as Quantum Batteries

Quantum batteries, as conceptualized by Binder et al. (2015), utilize entangled qubits to store energy in excited states, achieving a quantum speedup in charging time compared to classical systems. In Binder’s model, N qubits entangle, reducing charging time from linear (O(N)) to sublinear (O(√N)) due to collective excitation. Mitochondria replicate this mechanism biologically. The mitochondrial matrix accumulates protons (H⁺) from the tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle, with dehydrogenases stripping hydrogen atoms from substrates (e.g., NADH → NAD⁺ + H⁺ + e⁻). Each proton, a ¹H nucleus with spin 1/2, acts as a qubit or “wit,” storing energy in its nuclear spin state (Nicholls & Ferguson, 2013). The Kruse for Dummies lecture exploded this idea.

### [Page title](https://optimalklubs.com/kruse-for-dummies-general/)

 optimalklubs.com

The inner mitochondrial membrane (IMM) generates a strong electric field (30 MV/m), and the F₀ subunit of ATP synthase spins at 9,000 RPM, creating a magnetic field that aligns proton spins (Mitchell, 1961). Spin-spin coupling, akin to NMR’s J-coupling, may entangle these protons, forming a quantacell where the state of one proton depends on the others (Naaman & Waldeck, 2019). Sunlight-derived electrons excite NADH, driving the ETC (Complexes I–IV) and flipping proton spins in milliseconds, enabling rapid energy transfer. This quantum speedup mirrors Binder’s model, allowing mitochondria to deliver “energy on demand” for cellular processes.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-08-18T23:43:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood.31045/post-358442>

**Structured Water as a Protective Medium**

Entangled quantum states are susceptible to decoherence from environmental noise. In mitochondria, matrix water, produced via the reaction O₂ + 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂O (approximately 0.5 L daily per human)—forms structured layers near IMM proteins, known as exclusion zone (EZ) water (Pollack, 2013; Boron & Boulpaep, 2017). EZ water, characterized by a hexagonal lattice, excludes solutes and exhibits a reduced dielectric constant (ε\_r ≈ 60 vs. 78 for bulk water), creating a proton-rich fringe that shields entangled spins (Del Giudice et al., 1988). This acts as a biological Faraday cage, protecting proton spins from thermal noise and external electromagnetic fields (EMFs).

Deuterium (D₂O), present in natural water at a 1:6,400 ratio to H₂O, disrupts this system. Deuterium’s 20% greater mass and slower proton dynamics (lower dipole moment, sluggish reorientation) impair proton tunneling in ATP synthase, reducing ETC efficiency (Kropman & Bakker, 2001; Somlyai et al., 1993). DDW, with deuterium levels below 120 ppm, enhances proton mobility, lowers the refractive index, and facilitates faster light propagation, boosting EZ formation and quantum coherence. Pollack’s EZ model assumes uniform H₂O, but deuterium’s presence tightens the water lattice, shrinking EZ size and altering UV absorption peaks (e.g., 270 nm), which may affect light-driven processes (Pollack, 2013).

**UPEs and Information Transfer**

Mitochondria re-emit light as UPEs (200–800 nm) during ETC activity, particularly through cytochrome c oxidase (CCO) at Complex IV (Van Wijk, 2014). These biophotons, when spectrally limited and coherent, exhibit high OAM, a property that allows photons to carry more information than linear light (Allen et al., 1992). Modern physics suggests OAM in photons may be theoretically unlimited, enabling high-density information transfer. In mitochondria, UPEs encode information via their OAM and spin characteristics, interacting with quantum spin states of electrons and protons (spin 1/2 for ¹H, ³¹P; spin 3/2 for ³³S).

Shannon’s information theory (1948) and Landauer’s principle (1961) establish that information transfer has an energy cost, dissipating heat proportional to temperature (≈0.018 eV per bit at room temperature). Mitochondria manage this cost, using UPEs to process information while dissipating heat, aligning with Wheeler’s “it from bit” philosophy that energy and information are equivalent (Wheeler, 1989). This information is stored not only in biochemical bonds but also in cellular structures, but in membranes, gradients, organelles, and water enabling precise optical signaling via AMO physics.

**DHA as the Evolutionary Link Between Thermodynamics and Information Theory**

Docosahexaenoic acid (DHA), a polyunsaturated fatty acid (PUFA), represents an evolutionary innovation that bridges thermodynamics and information theory in eukaryotic cells. DHA originated in marine phytoplankton via photosynthesis approximately 600–650 million years ago during the Ediacaran period, 50–100 million years before the Cambrian explosion (~541 Ma) (Crawford et al., 2013). This pre-Cambrian emergence in algae created a reservoir of DHA in the ocean, which eukaryotes later incorporated into membranes, enhancing fluidity and electron transfer (Valentine & Valentine, 2004). DHA's high electron density (six double bonds) enables efficient light absorption and energy transfer, increasing cellular information capacity by facilitating UPE production and quantum coherence in membranes (Crawford et al., 2003).

This mirrors Claude Shannon's 1948 paper, "A Mathematical Theory of Communication," which formalized information extraction from noisy signals, enabling signal processing and digital transmission (Shannon, 1948). Just as Shannon's work catalyzed the "Cambrian revolution" in computing by mining buried information, DHA allowed eukaryotes to "mine" photonic information from the environment, driving complexity during the Cambrian explosion (Servais et al., 2016). In thermodynamic terms, DHA reduces entropy by optimizing membrane structure for energy dissipation in dissipative systems, linking Prigogine's far-from-equilibrium order to information processing (Prigogine, 1977).

Modern stressors like blue light and 2–5G liberate vitamin A in its aldehyde form (retinal), toxic to lipid rafts containing DHA, causing dielectric collapse and impairing electromagnetic information decoding (Crawford et al., 2013). This disrupts mitochondrial autophagy/mitophagy, mtDNA integrity, and cellular complexity, underscoring DHA's role as a "selfish" molecule prioritizing information flow over thermodynamic equilibrium.

**CSF, Microtubules, and Consciousness**

CSF, composed primarily of DDW, acts as a waveguide for UPEs, with a refractive index of 1.33 at 270 nm supporting photonic propagation (Tsenkova, 2010). Structured water around biomolecules enhances quantum coherence, distributing UPEs across neural networks. Microtubules, hollow protein structures in axons and dendrites, sustain quantum coherence via π-electron clouds in aromatic amino acids (e.g., tryptophan, absorbing at 220 nm). According to Penrose and Hameroff’s Orchestrated Objective Reduction (Orch-OR) theory, UPEs collapse microtubule wave functions, producing qualia, the subjective experiences of consciousness (Hameroff & Penrose, 1994).

Demyelination, the loss of myelin insulation around axons, disrupts this system. Reduced UPEs from mitochondrial stress (e.g., low redox, non-native EMFs, hypoxia) impair oligodendrocyte precursor cell (OPC) mitosis, decreasing myelination (Wang et al., 2020). This reduces bioelectric currents, increases reactive oxygen species (ROS), and alters CSF composition (e.g., higher deuterium, inflammatory cytokines), impairing its waveguiding capacity. Consequently, microtubule quantum coherence diminishes, desynchronizing wave function collapses and altering qualia, as seen in MS-related cognitive deficits (Chiaravalloti & DeLuca, 2018).

**Evolutionary Context**

Following the Great Oxidation Event (GOE) approximately 2.4 billion years ago, melanin internalized UV-driven UPE production, enabling quantum processing in microtubules and the emergence of consciousness. Modern stressors, non-native EMFs (nnEMFs), blue light, and circadian misalignment, disrupt this mechanism, reducing UPE coherence and exacerbating demyelination, recapitulating pre-GOE challenges (Kazemi et al., 2013). Sun exposure, as a post-GOE environmental factor, optimizes UPE production, supporting myelination and consciousness, as evidenced by studies showing protective effects of sunlight on brain volume in MS patients (Ricca et al., 2018).

**Biophysical Integration:** CSF and MicrotubulesDemyelination’s Impact on Microtubule FunctionDemyelination affects microtubule function through three primary mechanisms:

1.  _Reduced Biophoton Signaling:_ Mitochondrial stress from low redox (e.g., nnEMF, hypoxia) impairs CCO activity, reducing UPE production, particularly in the UV range (200–350 nm). These biophotons typically collapse microtubule wave functions, producing qualia (Wang et al., 2020). Fewer UPEs lead to uncollapsed quantum states in tubulin π-electrons, disrupting neural coherence and altering consciousness, such as the cognitive fog observed in MS.
2.  _Altered CSF Pathways:_ Demyelination-induced ROS may increase deuterium and inflammatory cytokines in CSF, impairing its waveguiding properties. Structured water around microtubules, essential for amplifying UPEs, is disrupted, desynchronizing wave function collapses across neural networks (Tsenkova, 2010). This global reduction in UPE distribution may contribute to dissociative states or fatigue in MS.
3.  _Bioelectric Disruption:_ Myelin enables saltatory conduction, optimizing bioelectric currents. Demyelination slows conduction, increasing energy demands on axonal mitochondria and elevating ROS, further reducing UPEs (Becker, 1960). Microtubules, responsible for mitochondrial transport, face oxidative stress, impairing tubulin polymerization and quantum coherence (Smith et al., 2016). This disrupts neural signaling, altering qualia patterns (e.g., motor deficits in MS).

**Arc Welding Analogy**

Microtubules can be likened to a weld seam in arc welding, requiring a stable arc (UPEs) and shielding gas (CSF’s DDW) to maintain integrity. Demyelination introduces defects, insufficient UPEs (weak arc) and altered CSF (contaminated shielding gas), causing “cracks” in the seam (impaired quantum coherence). This weakens the system’s integrity, manifesting as altered consciousness.Implications for Consciousness

The hard problem of consciousness, how subjective experience arises from physical processes is addressed by framing microtubules as quantum processors. UPEs, distributed via CSF, collapse microtubule wave functions, producing qualia per Orch-OR theory. Demyelination disrupts this process, reducing UPE coherence and desynchronizing collapses, leading to altered qualia (e.g., cognitive deficits, fatigue in MS). This suggests consciousness is a distributed phenomenon, reliant on light-driven quantum coherence rather than localized brain regions, explaining why it persists despite significant neural damage (e.g., lobe removal).

Proposed Interventions

Restoring UPE coherence offers a potential therapeutic strategy for demyelination-related disorders:

-   Circadian Alignment: Exposure to AM UV/blue and PM red light enhances CCO activity, boosting UPE production and supporting OPC mitosis for myelination (Kazemi et al., 2013).
-   DDW Therapy: Reducing deuterium levels in CSF (to <120 ppm) enhances proton tunneling, improves EZ water formation, and amplifies UPE waveguiding, supporting microtubule coherence (Somlyai et al., 1993).
-   Minimizing nnEMFs: Reducing exposure to non-native EMFs mitigates mitochondrial stress, preserving UPE production and quantum coherence (Bikle, 2020).

Future Research Questions

1.  How does deuterium concentration in CSF affect UPE waveguiding and microtubule coherence in vivo, and can DDW therapy restore qualia in MS patients?
2.  Can NMR/EPR or nitrogen-vacancy (NV) center probes measure proton spin entanglement in mitochondria, validating their role as quantum batteries?
3.  How do specific light frequencies (e.g., UV at 220 nm, red at 650 nm) influence UPE OAM and information transfer in neural cells, and can this be harnessed to enhance consciousness?
4.  What role does DHA in diet play in restoring membrane electron density and UPE production in post-Cambrian eukaryotic models, and how does this affect thermodynamic efficiency in mitochondrial systems?

**SUMMARY**

This decentralized photo-bioelectric framework redefines mitochondria as quantum battery masters, utilizing entangled proton spins, UPEs with high OAM, and structured water to manage energy and information. By integrating quantum biology, information theory, and AMO physics, it explains how mitochondria create negative entropy, enabling eukaryotic complexity. The inclusion of DHA as an evolutionary innovation underscores how pre-Cambrian adaptations in phytoplankton laid the foundation for information-rich cellular systems, paralleling Shannon's contributions to modern computing. DHA inclusion into membranes extends this model into the mammalian neural system, and it reveals how CSF distributes UPEs to microtubules, sustaining quantum coherence and consciousness. Demyelination disrupts this system, reducing UPE signaling, impairing microtubule function, and altering qualia, offering a quantum-based explanation for cognitive deficits in neurodegeneration. This framework not only addresses the hard problem of consciousness but also proposes light-first, anti-dopant strategies to restore quantum coherence, paving the way for novel therapeutic approaches in neurodegenerative diseases.

**CITES**

# Thread: Flying Mitigation Tips
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flying-mitigation-tips.31049/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 38
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Sandy Abram — 2025-06-05T07:54:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flying-mitigation-tips.31049/post-353990>

Hi Everyone,

I'm trying to put together a list of tips to help mitigate the negative effects of flying.

Apart from catching a boat, based on my reading and searching here is what I have come up with so far when you're stuck in a big metal tube 30,000+ feet in the sky.

Would love comments and feedback.

Pre-flight:
\- Get plenty of AM Sunlight - boost melanin, mitochondrial function and redox
\- Hydrate - drink plenty of water. I'm guessing some sea salt or lemon would be beneficial for electrolytes
\- Load up on DHA - enjoy seafood meal
\- CT - nice cold shower / plunge to boost mitochondrial function
\- ? Nutrients - I saw a post here about taking COQ10 but not sure if this is valid. And what about something like Vitamin C? Any benefits / info here?
\- ? Raspberries - Also another crazy idea is digging into bunch of raspberries (if they're local and seasonal of course) as I've seen studies they help with oxidative DNA damage and increase expression of DNA repair genes

At airport:
\- Avoid scanners if possible and opt for pat down
\- Access outside spaces - access any outdoor spaces for as long as possible whilst at airport
\- Turn off wi-fi

During flight:
\- Wear yellow / gold - deflects nnEMF
\- ? Eating gold - what about buying edible gold leaf and enjoying some gold leaf on your freeze dried raspberries or in your water? Or is this completely BS or nuts?
\- Wear blue blocking glasses - especially during night or all the time?
\- What about where you sit? Is the front / back of the plane any better than the wing? Are different spots exposed to more radiation eg. window seats?
\- Hydrate - drink plenty of water, avoid alcohol / sugary drinks due to dehydration. Perhaps opt for green tea instead.
\- Don't eat - is this right? Or avoid eating due to circadian mismatch.
\- Turn off bluetooth / put phone in airplane mode
\- ? What about EMF protective clothing

Post Flight
\- Get plenty of sunlight for melanin, mito function, redox and aligning circadian timing
\- Ground yourself - to discharge static electrons
\- Cold Therapy - boost melanin, mito function, redox
\- Hydrate - drink plenty of water (plus sea salt perhaps)
\- Meal timing - start eating meals based on local time. Eat outside!!

Anyway, would love your thoughts and anything that I've missed.

Thanks!!
Sandy

All good ideas.. remember it is not just the radiation / emf .. flying is hypoxic due to cabin pressurisation = lower p02. Sea level (total pressure) is around 760mmhg vs say in a pressurised cabin set at 8000ft ~ 564mmhg = ~ 26% drop in available oxygen pressure = mito hypoxia.. Methylene blue would be the best bang for your $$ however you need to know how to use it to avoid hurting yourself...

Re: MB: I purchased some buccal troches for "just in case"and as part of an emergency medical kit (I'll save that topic for another post ). Haven't used them because clearly I have no idea what I'm doing but should the need arise and the expert advice come with it, I have some on hand.

### warriorT — 2025-06-05T10:46:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flying-mitigation-tips.31049/post-353993>

I would add maximum grounding pre flight as well as post flight. As much DHA (seafood) and Iodine rich food (or dried seaweed capsules) before and after to replenish the cell membranes to enable autophagy and iodine for radiation protection. Magnesium to mitigate voltage gated calcium channel damage from the emf onslaught. Regarding seating avoid the front near the cockpit due to the avionics equipment fields, avoid the wings due to the field generated by the engines and avionics so head for the back of the plane furthest from these. Avoid window seats as they are max exposure to cosmic radiation and UVA (and also i believe there is stronger field from the outer casing of the plane), so choose the aisle. Keep your feet off the floor unless wearing rubber soled shoes and try and ground yourself barefoot on the metal footbar below the seat in front. Try and minimise time spent at or anywhere near the airport as the whole area is a Chernobyl, so outside spaces at the airport are likely to be very bad like military bases (plus inhaling jet fuel is not great), just arrive last minute and go straight to boarding. Read the blog ‘The Jet Lag Rx - Human flight Biohacking’ for how to use light and CT appropriately for max effect before and after the flight depending on your direction and duration of travel

### Mat.I — 2025-06-06T05:44:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flying-mitigation-tips.31049/post-354072>

> [Anthony S said:](/goto/post?id=354040)
>
> Is there anywhere I can read more about using MB for flights?

Not sure. If you study how it works, then you can apply it to certain circumstances where it may be indicated. Just a basic example - CIV is the last step in the ETC e.g. reaction O2 + 4e- + 4H+ > 2H2O. If O2 is insufficient CIV cannot unload electrons efficiently which causes electron back up reduced ATP and increases ROS etc.. What happens when CIV is not working well aerobic glycolysis picks up the slack as no O2 is needed for the back up pathway. MB helps with regenerating NAD+ which allows glycolysis + Oxphos to both continue with out redox blocks.. Prevents full shift and preserves electron flow to CIV even under low O2. Do your own research. There are a few YT videos on the ETC.

I have mentioned on this forum and in my blogs that the 2018 Nobel Prize was the culmination of my massive Webinar, done in 2016, called "What Life Is." Mourou's speech to the Nobel committee uncovers a vast amount of data on the nuclear transmutation of elements using CPA. If you rewatch that webinar and are reading the DM series, you might begin to see why I hacked the periodic table 20 years ago after reading Russian literature. In 1971, Becker mentioned that the Russians had created an artificial magnetic field for their cosmonauts after two of them died in a 1971 incident. When I sat down with him before he died, we talked about how endogenous light might be able to transmute atoms' life uses.

Then, in 2018, the Nobel Prize in Physics was awarded to Gérard Mourou and Donna Strickland for their work on chirped pulse amplification (CPA).

This science highlights the creation of high-intensity, ultra-short laser pulses. This mimics what mitochondria do when they create coherent UPEs.

How many times have you heard me say the sun and mitochondria have a lot in common? I believe your mitochondria are a biological cyclotron that can make an element as needed. Humans flying in a plane at altitude during a solar minimum increase cosmic radiation, and this disrupts the recursive loop that is critical in operating the cyclotron inside you. You might recall from the Rubin Huberman Kruse pod on @tetranow, Rick mentioned he notices how he loses a tan every time he flies. I told him his perception was correct. We never got into why it happens, but it was mentioned. In the Nobel experiments, CPA stretches, amplifies, and recompresses laser pulses (think coherent UPEs), enabling applications such as nuclear waste transmutation by bombarding isotopes to reduce their half-lives. I believe Rick's loss of melanin when he flies is directly tied to the recursive loop we have built into our cells that I just laid out in Decentralized Medicine #55.

I believe mitochondria transform LED light from their semiconductive mechanisms and time-stamped their UPEs similarly, with melanin’s recursive loop generating precise biophoton pulses to modulate mtDNA and transmute elements, mimicking CPA’s temporal precision at the nanoscale.

What would the mitochondrial transmutation sequence look like?

UV photon from coherent UPE ejects K-shell electron, first ionizing the atom. This only operates with atoms on the low-energy side of the periodic table.

This destabilized the nucleus photo-bioelectrically.

Then, a mitochondria-generated neutrino flips a proton to a neutron in the nucleus, creating a new atom.

The cell harvests the element via photo-bioelectric redox feedback. Nick Lane's work on how membranes provide feedback from the environment is likely operational here.

When you realize the cell is capable of doing this, adding new atoms to the cell is very, very unwise. I think our government inside of DARPA learned this in the 1960s, and that is how they started building the bioweapons program. This was one of the key findings from the MKUltra experiments done in the 1960s that Richard Helms aimed to keep from the world. That technology was transferred as top secret to SRI and then to the Brain Health Initiative by Obama in 2013. Biology is time-stamping our atoms in health. Your government has figured out how to weaponize this against you.

### Anthony Smith — 2025-06-12T19:17:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flying-mitigation-tips.31049/post-354567>

> [warriorT said:](/goto/post?id=354547)
>
> A boat with 3000+ people with multiple connected devices in a compressed space sounds like a horror show. Just thinking about how starlink satellites are beaming to Earth, and planes are flying through the beams so would the plane be in an almost continuous field of WiFi from starlink or does it depend on where connections to it are made on the ground so the plane would just be passing through a connected beam intermittently when over land/ dense settlements where people are connected to it? Its insane that they’ve enabled WiFi on planes now, the only airline I’ve found so far that doesn’t have WiFi is Avianca, so I’ve booked with them for myself and cat to get to ES. Have you any additional tips on emf protection for pets (other than same as for humans)? The cat has black fur so at least she has high melanin.

Starlink uses various frequency bands for its satellite internet services, including the Ka band (27-40 GHz), Ku band (12-18 GHz), and V band (40-75 GHz). I do not believe the Starlink signal can get through the metal skin of a plane. The phones and laptops/tablets of all the people around you, who did not turn off WiFi and Bluetooth, even though they MIGHT be on Airplane mode, is of far greater concern than any satellite signal.

My thought in regard to modern travel, get “there” as fast as possible and then control your space/exposure once you arrive.

### Mightygumshoe — 2025-07-14T19:47:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flying-mitigation-tips.31049/post-356301>

My daughter is hypotonic (loose joints) and hypermobile; and stopped flying in 2019. Am glad she went to Europe, Hawaii, etc long beforehand. And got to see part of the world. Now she drives everywhere.

The last time we flew, she had MAJOR joint pain -- paced on the plane, was that bad but no swelling in ankles etc.; this all started after puberty. Back when 2 or 3 no pain not even at 6 yr old on a flight despite loose joints. But in 2019 just 2000 feet off the ground, massive lower back, hip pain, down to the foot (like sciatica). So bad she paced the plane. On the trip home she had a connection -- and second flight was worse than the first. We don't know if air bubbles are forming, or if it something else. Hypoxia, etc. Doctors tried throwing pills with no answers, so she just stopped flying which they also advised. Hard as a working adult because it has limited her career. So much of work involves travel. She has other issues she is dealing with as well. Cannot eat any gluten or any corn at all...no nightshades either. And has chemical sensitivities. Trying to find an answer...or two or three...and people we can trust.

We are not trusting of the medical profession. They ripped a hole in her stomach -- won't share that story but due to puberty from hell, along with the gluten issues cropping up at the same time; and we kind of walked away and we cured most of her problems just by researching. Gut and flying problems all started after puberty. She has been pretty healthy until an office loaded with 5g etc pulled her back to work at least 3 or 4 days a week in an open floor plan.

I am trying to get her to sign up here even if just for 6 months, with a gold membership, to get 1 on 1 help from the master. She is a stubborn adult. I understand her issue with the medical profession because of countless mishaps, culminating in removing a cancerous kidney from my late husband, then putting part of it back in his body because originally, they thought he only had an infection. And conveniently lost the path sample. He rotted in our family room at 43, after 10 surgeries. The insurance companies loved him... $$$. My daughter now 27 was 4, and watched. But she needs to take charge of her health and look forward, not backward. Going to keep pushing her to sign up. God bless. Dr. Jack is a blessing as are all of you. There is so much I am learning from this forum! And the recommended books. Thank you all.

══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Pleasantly Weird
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 97
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### KFDags — 2025-06-05T20:56:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-354031>

Hello there, I am a 39-year-old married mom of two. My son is 7 (he has ASD and is nonverbal,) daughter is 5; I’m a stay-at-home mom, and my husband is a police officer, and retired Army/National Guard. We live in the smallest US state, Rhode Island. I guess you could describe me as pleasantly weird? My mother-in-law called me “eclectic” but she’s just being polite. I have hypermobile Ehlers Danlos Syndrome and so do my children, it appears. We also have a MTHFR polymorphism which, I’m certain, contributed to our health issues.

I am 4’9” and ~95lbs; I stopped growing in fourth grade/age 12. My parents are decent people, with similar health issues, so they never recognized any of my problems, as ‘problems.’ I was often labeled lazy and selfish. I am an only child and had no cousins to play with growing up, so I was alone a lot. Friends come and go, still; I prefer solitude. Most of my childhood was spent being asleep or trying to sleep, playing video games, watching TV, but I also went outside a lot because I had (still have; we now live in my childhood home) a great backyard in the woods.

My biggest struggle was/is being so exhausted that I simply couldn’t function, even prior to starting elementary school. Not narcolepsy at all, but it was a thirst for sleep that was never quenched. This seriously affected my academic ability (later, killed any career aspirations), and I was in classes through high school with lower-functioning kids with behavioral issues. During school breaks, I would sleep 15-18 hours straight. And after school, I’d come home and fall asleep in place, sometimes with my eyes open. I’d wake up to my mom screaming at me to wake up, after she got home from work at 6pm. Once I got my driver’s license, I used to skip school to sleep in my car; ended up with a dead battery once. I missed so much school that I showed up to the wrong class, because I didn’t know what day it was. Teachers would forget who I was.

Right before high school graduation, at age 18, I unknowingly contracted mononucleosis, then walking pneumonia. But it felt no different than my usual daily baseline exhaustion so I went ahead with my life. Consequently, I wound up in critical condition in the ICU, intubated, and in a medically-induced coma for 19 days. I kept trying to pull tubes out and escape so I was given very high doses of drugs like Ativan, fentanyl, and paralytic drugs. They barely worked. Doctors didn’t understand why my body could tolerate such high doses of these drugs. They questioned my parents whether I was an alcoholic or intravenous drug user (I was neither, obviously). After nearly a month in the hospital, I recovered.

I continued to have ongoing, strange medical issues to include kidney stones (spaced every 10 years beginning at age 21); shingles at age 24; POTS (beginning in early childhood,) along with MCAS-type symptoms; heart palpitations, unbearable social anxiety with facial flushing; severe hyperhidrosis, especially axillary; 2:30/3am random vomiting (until pre-teens) & vomiting while asleep (until age 7/8); a transient head-turn syncope (almost full blackouts with hearing loss followed by tinnitus, tunnel vision, loss of speech, unable to move for about 60-90 seconds) and, of course, hypermobility with subluxations.

### KFDags — 2025-06-05T21:41:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-354036>

I got pregnant at 29 but had a natural miscarriage (blighted ovum) at 15/16 weeks. It took my body over a month to rid itself of the hCG. Pregnant again at 30, had a miserable pregnancy, non-stop nausea/frequent vomiting all 9 months, gained nearly 70lbs, new burning/electrical pain on tops of feet, near ankles. My son had two standard prenatal tests come back abnormal that later turned out to be “nothing.” After further testing (calcium spot on heart, can’t recall the other issue), but the NIPT we had done for him was negative. I had my second-ever kidney stone attack four days before my estimated due date, took an ambulance ride to the hospital, and was there on IV dilaudid for four days. Couldn’t keep anything down from pain hours before the dilaudid was supposed to wear off. I was sent home on day 4 with several dilaudid tablets. I stayed in bed for 6 days, until my scheduled induction via foley balloon. Still couldn’t keep anything down.

Foley balloon went in at 9:30pm, fell out at 3am, I was in pure agony, all of a sudden, and my son was born 4:27am. No time for epidural: precipitous labor. My son was quiet, barely alive, cord wrapped around his neck, hypothermic, and blue. (His APGARs: 3, 5, 9.) The placenta also crumbled apart, as if things didn’t suck enough, so the OB spent 45 excruciating minutes taking everything out, piece by piece. I don’t remember much, honestly; my husband said it was probably better I didn’t see our son in that condition. Thank God, he is okay (some lung sounds, but not much else.)

The pregnancy with my daughter (I was 33) was mostly normal except for partial previa, prior to 20 weeks, which caused bright red bleeding for 8 long weeks. I was equally as miserable being pregnant this time, as I was last; however, not as extreme with overall symptoms. The birth was normal, although I was induced (which I was thankful for, at the time, given the circumstances of my son’s birth), with Pitocin. I had the epidural this go around.

One year after pregnancy, I had a bilateral salpingectomy. Following year, some of my ribs just came loose from the cartilage (slipped rib syndrome, I assume?) Instead of two floating ribs - like we human folk are supposed to have - I now have four floating ribs. I hit a lot more curbs now with my car but, other than that, it doesn’t seem to cause me problems (yet).

### Jack Kruse — 2025-06-06T02:32:50+0300

I love the name of your journal. I think I am going to like you.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-06-06T02:39:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-354055>

A section from a future blog.........

**Collagen Vascular Diseases (CVDs)**

Diseases like Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (EDS), systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), and rheumatoid arthritis (RA) involve collagen failure. My decentralized hypothesis that these diseases protect organs from photo-bioelectric collapse is compelling. In EDS, hypermobile collagen (due to defective cross-linking) may absorb excessive mechanical stress, preventing deeper tissue damage from IMM/CCO dysfunction. In SLE, collagen degradation (e.g., in blood vessels) could divert ROS-induced damage away from vital organs, a sacrificial mechanism akin to a crumple zone. This is why lupus patients complain about going out in the sun. Their bodies have been living so long in suboptimal environments that their epigenetic programs are acting to limit ROS damage. This means that exogenous oxygen for them is even more dangerous. Lack of sun and chronic hypoxia at the mtDNA level, blood, and DNA level are real issues. These failures often occur at collagen’s electronic weak spots (interfibrillar bonds), reflecting low redox power from light stress (nnEMF, blue light). these people are almost unusually pale. If they have dark skin they are pale for their haplotype cohorts.

**Modern Light Stress and Collagen Failure:** AM sunlight tightens collagen’s tensegrity system by exciting electrons, enhancing cross-linking and coherence (narrow UPE spectra, 200–400 nm). However, blue light (450 nm) and nnEMF (e.g., Wi-Fi) disrupt this, broadening UPE spectra, spiking ROS, and weakening collagen bonds. This aligns with the observation of increased collagen vascular failures in a manufactured world, as light stress impairs CCO, reduces ATP, and drives mtDNA chaos, leading to systemic entropy (previous discussions on AD, PD).

Why would a professional hockey player get EDS? Maybe to protect himself from trauma due to his light environment.

Wow, never thought of it that way: acting to prevent deeper tissue damage. Definitely would make sense. I did always wonder what purpose it (the hypermobility/soft & stretchy skin) actually served, if anything at all.

Thank you!

### KFDags — 2025-06-06T13:50:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-354085>

April 2024, I had a lumbar laminectomy for occult tethered cord. My neurosurgeon was Dr. Petra Klinge, who was so kind. She works with my son’s neurosurgeon, Dr. Konstantina Svokos.

I refused to wait long for an appointment (it was a 7 month waiting list, the office said) because I was deteriorating. So I found Dr. Klinge’s email in some of her medical publications online, emailed her pictures of me doing some hypermobile poses inspired by the Beighton scale, and I had an appt. after only a few short weeks.

I had lifelong, random excruciating pain in my legs, especially in my early teens. It would occur more often with increased activity. Also constant lower back pain. However, it wasn’t until about age 36/37 that I began to have significantly worse issues with pain, plus new bladder issues. After watching many of Dr. Klinge’s presentations and doing many hours of research, I knew that I had occult tethered spinal cord.

### KFDags — 2025-06-06T23:30:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-354139>

Small update, I guess? Stepped on my glasses a couple of sunrises ago (okay, possibly more than just a couple.) The snap was impressively loud, must say.

Anyway, the silver lining is the new pair will arrive next week without any UV-blocking B/S. Hadn’t thought about a minor detail like that (my regular ol glasses blocking UV) until having to actually go out and do this PIA task.

### tiffanyfinnegan — 2025-06-07T00:21:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-354143>

FYI, you can't wear any glasses for sunrise and try to wear as little as you can when outside - I learned this and it does get a little easier. I carry them with me and put them on if needed but pretty much do the sunrise and dog walk without anything on my eyes. Even with glasses it doesn't do what you need at all based on what I've been told. I believe unless it is quartz it doesn't really let the proper rays through. And of course, glasses better than contact lenses (that's what I've been in for 30 years and didn't know). Good luck and welcome to the group!!!!
