### KFDags — 2026-02-09T23:29:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-366614>

> [KFDags said:](/goto/post?id=355200)
>
> My certifiable goof, Ada. Just had a feeling she’d be born on MJK’s (from TOOL) birthday & I was right! (Our ‘Mama & Me’ favorite band, of course; we are _Tool Buddies_. Her creation, not mine; but top-notch idea nonetheless.)
>
> Anyway, I really thought we were all good, when she was born; but then, she started growing out of diapers on only one side, legs/feet changed color, and her head was meaty & huge. Admittedly called it a “pork chop,” for a time. I have to have a sense of humor about life, at this point, okay?
>
> After yet another missed diagnosis by pediatricians, we found the dx at the pediatric ortho: severe bilateral DDH w/ one socket completely dislocated, & leg asymmetry (hemihyperplasia.) Tested negative for Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome (pediatrician had to do _something_, I guess?) but had ongoing blood work and ultrasounds. Only once did she have an elevated alpha-fetoprotein, which resolved by ~14 months.
>
> Ada’s DDH was exceedingly easy to correct after several months of almost-nonstop wear of the Rhino brace. (Still no explanation for any other symptoms: color change in legs/feet, pork chop.) Shortly after the DDH was discovered, though, she began to grow noticeable pubic hair and was dx precocious puberty. It thankfully resolved on its own within months.
>
> When Logan was evaluated for his craniosynostosis ~August 2021, we took Ada too (she was ~16 months old.) She has a palpable saddle deformity and her cephalic index was 81, but all the sutures were open: there’s no evidence of any problems. Last year, after my OTC surgery, she too had some symptoms that could have been related to occult tethered cord. However, an MRI showed us more negative findings.
>
> The current issue: she experiences these waves of normal behavior for weeks, followed by a period of days or couple weeks where she’s not herself. Most or all of these oddly specific symptoms appear (I’ll describe below) together. When I say together, I don’t mean all of these symptoms appear daily; I mean, we typically see at least 5 or more of these symptoms, sprinkled throughout every day, during this period. It’s hard to describe; a subtle personality shift. We just do not get along. When it’s happening, she has these unexplained behavioral changes (usually mild, but it leaves you puzzled by the interaction) paired with physical symptoms.
>
> **Her symptoms:**
>
> Zero frustration tolerance/instant rage; cannot be reasoned with. Irrationally angry. Consequences **do not** matter. I can get my autistic son who’s melting down under control with more ease, to give you an idea.
>
> Change in bowel habits: constipated but the sudden urge to poop appears; she poops, gets cleaned up, leaves room, and is back to poop again within 90 seconds (never gets it all out the first time.) Occasionally pees her pants or wets the bed.
>
> Memory goes blank, instantly. Completely forgets instructions/plans/conversations we had only seconds before. Doesn’t matter if it’s a preferred activity like buying a toy. “Ohh, yeah...” \[Looks totally lost\] - An entirely different experience compared to when a person forgets something, then reminded of it. - Yes! Believe me, I understand she is a child. What I’m saying is there’s more to this interaction.
>
> Her overall size is concerning; I’m short/small because something went **really** wrong. People don’t understand that saying “oh, she’s small because you’re small.” reinforces my concerns. After losing the baby fat, she’s always been 1st-3rd percentile, except for her head, which is noticeably larger (65th percentile.) Her neurosurgeon (Dr. Konstantina Svokos) agrees her head is quite large. Ada’s head is wider than her body, if you look at her whole profile. I think we’re up to 4-5 strangers inquiring with me if she’s 3 or 4 years old. They’re surprised to hear she’s five. Even our neighbor’s 3 1/2 year old daughter weighs 3lbs more than Ada.
>
> She trips and falls excessively. Drops things just as much.
>
> Motion-sickness out of nowhere (occasionally happens now); very susceptible, even on playground swings.
>
> Pronunciation of words is worse at times, then gets better. Mumbles, hard to understand.
>
> Days of more intense hunger; eats multiple large meals. Then days of barely picking at food. Weird chewing/oral sensory habits (chews on hair, toys, random objects).
>
> Sometimes had trouble choking on food and would loudly gag and/or vomit everything up, at times (occasionally happens now).
>
> It just seems like there’s a super mild hydrocephalus or something going on. Yes, I know I only have a public school education and laughable online college degree that have no relevance whatsoever. BUT, my son’s pathologically-shaped head is what motivated me to find his diagnosis. (I often referenced one particular Japanese study of 500 mild trigonocephaly pediatric cases. The study noted that these patients often had autistic-like traits, irrational responses to mild frustration, etc.- exact behavior my son was exhibiting) That certainly influenced his behavior so why not (possibly) something like a mild hydrocephalus behind hers?
>
> Ada hasn’t yet had an MRI done to check; obviously based on my/my son’s history and her past symptoms, Dr. Svokos justified the MRI. Two months ago, she also had a very thorough ophthalmologist examination - and cooperated beautifully - to check the optic nerve for pressure. There was none. My son had a very different experience during that same exam, as you can imagine; it showed no pressure but he had a copper beaten skull after all. I realize the position or reasons for causing ICP are different (metopic synostosis, pressure in front vs hydrocephalus, more inner-central brain).
>
> Just wondering what you think?

Many months ago, my 5-year-old daughter, Ada, had the MRI to check for hydrocephalus. Of course, we were relieved to find there was no evidence showing hydrocephalus at all. Since my post, all of the above-mentioned symptoms have completely resolved on their own. Every last one! The only remaining issue is she’s a bit smaller than other girls, similar in age. The size gap gets less obvious with time, though! Fingers crossed she passes the very low bar I’ve set at 57”!!

### Jack Kruse — 2026-02-24T16:49:23+0200

### Jack Kruse — 2026-02-24T16:59:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-367572>

Getting larger post puberty tells us we are losing timing at SCN.

The obsession pediatricians have with "growth charts" is a biochemical delusion that ignores the biophysical reality of the **Melanin-Water Battery**.

Before puberty, a child is designed to be a **Time-Expensive, Energy-Dense** system.

**The Particle Accelerator:** A smaller body has a higher surface-area-to-volume ratio, which, counterintuitively, allows the **Internal Sun at the SCN** to stay cooler and more coherent. This maximizes the **220nm UPE signal** to target the leptin receptor needed for the **Neocortical Blockchain** to "index" the world.

**Efficiency:** Staying "smaller" for longer means the child is successfully **concentrating information** from its environment rather than just **expanding matter** (Entropic Expansion). This is the hallmark of a high-voltage, low-deuterium system.
Post-puberty humans sustain a loss of time coherence
Puberty is the moment the **POMC software** on Chromosome 2 shifts the "instruction manual" from **Growth/Development** to **Reproduction/Expansion**.

**Losing the DC Current:** As a human gets "larger" post-puberty, the metabolic cost of maintaining the **DC current** across the entire "Solar Panel" (the skin) increases.

**The Entropy Shift:** Rapid growth in height or mass is often a sign that the body is "venting" energy into **matter** because it can no longer contain it as **coherent light**. The same is true with her ventricles or in a patient with a tumor.

Pediatricians celebrate "high percentiles" because they view the body as a biochemical **furnace,** the more wood (food) you burn, the bigger the fire (child).

**The Reality:** In a **Particle Accelerator** (mitochondria), a sudden spike in size is "Optical Noise." It suggests the **SCN** is losing its 4D timing capability. This is what hydrocephalus is in the brain in refernce to the ventricles and CSF production.

**The Outcome:** Children who grow "too fast" often hit puberty "too early." This is **Quantum Undermining of Life's Time** in its purest form. They are "spending" their lifetime (Time) to buy size (Matter), leading to earlier **heteroplasmy** and the "atrophic" conditions you see in adults.

So I see being smaller as a very positive sign.

### KFDags — 2026-03-19T15:29:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-369220>

I think it’s finally safe to admit that I feel pretty great!! Physically & emotionally fantastic in a way - I assumed - was simply not an option for certain people.

Nearly all of the CCI symptoms - previously consuming many hours of my days - have reduced to only a few mild episodes a month. Previously, upon waking each morning, I was dry heaving or vomiting due to nausea/dizziness & disorientation. Was wearing the Aspen cervical collar probably a lot longer than I should have, at times. Even slept with it on, otherwise my heart would race & I couldn’t breathe to fall asleep.

Sometime around December, it’s as if a switch was flipped. All those issues collectively started improving & the daily anticipated misery just stopped. I’m keeping up with the protocols, and definitely avoiding turning my head to the left. I never have to wear the Aspen collar anymore.

Also working on a passport (only ever been to Florida twice, like 10 years ago.) Still need to follow up with my neurosurgeon, but this rapidly downward-spiraling issue seems to actually be on its way out now! I am so happy!

My son, Logan, is even better! He is putting all his requests in short sentences now. Words still sound jumbled & weird, but he’s actually trying to correct his pronunciation - if you correct him in real time. When he wants to go to bed, he says “ready for bed” which is sometimes followed up with a “tired” or “want sleep.” When he knows my husband is at work, he says “I miss…” \[extra pause, for some reason?\] “dada” because, he says, “dada’s at work.” My favorite, Logan’s been saying, “I love you!” while making a little heart shape with his hands! My son said almost NOTHING last year!!

Last week, there were a couple 70° days so we took advantage, alongside the piles of leftover (nonmelting) snow from the blizzard. Even after those days outside, the words just started flowing. Logan acts so proud of himself; he wants us to know he can say all these new words now. So if you’re not listening & don’t acknowledge him with eye contact, he keeps repeating it until you do.

During our dinner last night, Logan was told he couldn’t have his way: he immediately found another activity, happily. That same scenario used to deteriorate into a 45- to 60-min screaming tantrum, accompanied by various self-injurious behavior or destruction of various things. My husband and I stopped having dinner together a while ago, because of these outbursts. But we’re starting to get back to doing normal people things again.

This is such a sense of relief. I always assumed that since I’m so small, that this was - in theory - a side effect of my tethered cord. What’s strange is I’ve noticed other very small women, also diagnosed with tethered cord. Met the parents of a 19 year old girl, in the waiting room at Dr. Klinge’s office. They said their daughter was 4’10” so just an inch taller than me. Again, anecdotally, in the tethered cord Facebook group, there seemed to be more ladies like me, shopping in the kids department. (Well under 5ft tall.)
Is this nothing or is something else going on here?

### KFDags — 2026-03-19T17:35:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-369224>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=367570)

> Her size staying smaller before puberty = more energy efficient. Smaller ventricles = Good sign

My wild woman is going to be 6 next month. Ada is the only person I can talk about the fun stuff with, like quantum biology, TOOL, everything. Last month, she asked what it meant to count sheep. So I explained the meaning; but, I said how human beings can also be called sheep, because their brains are turned off. That’s how I get her to understand the Warburg shift, etc.

She said, “Oh, like my friend Myra.” I asked what do you mean, and she said, “she wears sunscreen.” So heartwarming!!

Last night we talked about all the Biblical/Kundalini symbolism in many of TOOL’s videos; Schism is her favorite right now. We talked about the shadow and the sacred marriage (the male, representing the carnal mind, and female, representing the divine mind) come together to form a higher consciousness or wholeness in you. \[Those who disagree, this is just my personal interpretation.\] I tried my best to explain the wave-particle duality to her, which, was easier with help of the Schism video. Ada was inspired to create her own board game.

The ovals are where you need to step away from your bad shadow, to activate your higher mind; then, you spin on the spiral, because the wave collapsed, and now you’re ready to have good thoughts! I kinda like the idea!

### KFDags — 2026-04-11T00:55:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pleasantly-weird.31052/post-370679>

I normally only share moments of my childlike whimsy to those who know & barely tolerate me, to being with, but I’m feeling so genuinely incredible right now.

This was a day before my cycle starts; a day typically spent on the couch, hating everything, and just feeling like unrelenting shit. But, no longer am I a useless slug! I spent the day enjoying a strange pattern of snow, hail, sun, & clouds, along with the wildlife.

My CCI symptoms are all but gone, at this point. Still haven’t made the follow up appt yet but I feel no rush now. My head doesn’t feel like it’s about to pop off from all the pressure, my scalp isn’t throbbing randomly, and my heart isn’t racing like it used to. I could go on forever. There is still much work to be done, but the positives keep rolling in.

### Mightygumshoe — 2026-04-11T05:19:27+0300

There is still much work to be done, but the positives keep rolling in.

I am so happy for you. Thanks to following Dr. Jack ideas and actually implementing them, my daughter is doing SUPER well. We found shrimp wild caught, no chemicals and she is eating it 4x a week. Going to try to find salmon with no chemicals too, wild caught. She is earthing outside here in Michigan, even though still cold, feet can tolerate the grass for 20 min during the day and evening whenever possible. She feels better. Her mood is better. Her body is better. She has had ZERO gut trouble in 8 months! And drinks about 3/4 liter of DDW a day. She tries to get sun on way to work with windows down and at noon and a little on an afternoon walk. The inflammation she once had is all gone. She is incredible. Rides 3 miles on our lifecycle bike standing up, like it is nothing. In 20 min I think. Then does more sitting. So we just keep doing, same as you. So happy for you. I prayed for you when I first saw your early posts.

Everybody wants the quick fix, sometimes it is the slow read (Dr. Jack info) that provides the best results. Peace.

That is such wonderful news! Very happy for you and your daughter. And many, many thanks; you are very kind.  Frankly, 8-10 months (for me) is a quick fix as far as I’m concerned! Especially after a lifetime of feeling as if I’m on the verge of death. If only I could’ve found Dr. Kruse sooner.

Oh yeah! I’m sure your daughter must be all fired up! That’s usually how I feel now. I think it’s a taste of what normal must feel like? So far, I have been able to skip over two months’ worth of my ADHD medication (over the course of several months.) Wish I kept track of when I started. Without it, I used to be in a mild catatonic state for most of the day, every day.

We got the kids some new outdoor toys; the best one, so far, is a heavy duty Big Wheel. You better believe I’ve been racing that thing around. Plus, it was sunny and in the 80°s today. Good luck to you ladies!!

# Thread: First update after being in ES for 41/2 months
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-update-after-being-in-es-for-41-2-months.31053/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### UlliJoanna — 2025-06-05T23:28:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-update-after-being-in-es-for-41-2-months.31053/post-354045>

My grand maternal grandmother died at 88 with dementia and breast cancer
My mother suffered with endometriosis, had a hysterectomy at 50 and suffers now with arthritis and sleep apnea
My younger sister suffers with SIBO

I was born in St. Lucia. I had a normal birth, but was bottle-fed and born tongue tied. at three I moved to Hong Kong.
During my early childhood I suffered with hives. I had my appendix removed at aged five. I wet the bed until I was about seven years old. I was outside most of the time I was a real tomboy as a child.

At aged 11, I was sent to boarding school in England. I flew back to Hong Kong three times a year. At 18 years old, I settled in London where I lived until I was 51 years old.

At 13 I was diagnosed with a stigmatism and started to wear prescription glasses. I then began to wear contact lenses when I was 16 years old and only took them out and stopped wearing lenses after I discovered jack kruse in October 2024. Since discovering jack I have taken off my glasses and do not wear contact lenses. I have never worn sunglasses.

At the age of 30 I started noticing strange symptoms. I started to get pain in my hands and was diagnosed with Reynolds disease. I tried to fix everything by changing my diet because that is all I knew. I developed tingling in my hands and my feet, urinary urgency and numbness that spread up to my torso. After a terrible episode of sickness, that involved violent throwing up and diarrhea the numbness and tingling spread and became worse. After many trips to the doctor, they finally did some testing and diagnosed multiple sclerosis in January 2003. Within a year of the diagnosis, I was housebound in a wheelchair and really thought I was dying.. I refused medication because they wanted me to have chemotherapy because they said the MS was so aggressive.

I did some investigation and began to follow a strict diet, which in hindsight, probably contributed to my rapid decline as it involved eating huge amounts of fruit and vegetables fish, but no fat or meat.
Realizing that I was deteriorating very quickly, I saw a kinesiologist who threw muscle testing, told me to stop eating fruit, sugar, wheat, and to start eating fish and vegetables. After a few months I started to slowly improve. I still could not walk. The improvements came about as improved energy , improved brain function, and less spasticity in my legs. Even now if I eat fruit, my legs feel very, very heavy and very numb

In 2011 I became pregnant and the improvements came. I got out of the wheelchair and started to walk first with the Zimmer frame, then onto crutches and then onto one stick since being a El Salvador. I have gone back to using two sticks, but that’s because of the beach. It’s not a sign of decline.
Since having my daughter in 2012, my energy levels are 80% better I can walk for up to an hour around the park and at times my feet have moved properly, which has enabled me to walk at a proper speed with a proper gate as my hips have Moved forward as I walk and held themselves as they are meant to. None of this has held and these improvements lasted for a couple of weeks and then the old patterning returned.

I discovered Jack Kruse in October 2024 and I moved to El Salvador with my 13-year-old daughter in January 2025. I had my consultation with jack in February 2025 after moving here.
Since my consultation with jack, I have seen the sunrise and the sunset every day.
I have spent four hours on the beach sometimes longer and I’m very very brown. I am always outside with just a kiniki bikini on. I don’t have any artificial light after nightfall. I sleep in a bedroom with very little low EMF and I eat a low deteurium diet.
I also try and do three CT sessions each week with the temperature at 12 to 15°C for 20 minutes.

Since introducing CT the improvements are very noticeable. After CT My legs feel lighter my feet move more easily and I have more energy. I also go to the loo daily. This is huge as constipation has been an issue for 22 years.

I have been, and I am still currently reading the demylination blogs. I realize now that I crushed my GLP for many years. I am now supporting my GLP and have been since my arrival in El Salvador in January. I will add fermented foods.

I am also a MTHFR with COMTE at 40%. Listening to jack on a recent podcast talking about CT and EMF, I understand why my body is improving with CT as it helps the body recover from the distress caused by EMF.

I have to return to England in a few days to sort out a few housing financial things, and will return to El Salvador as quickly as I can.
Whilst in England, I will
Drink deteurium depleted water. Having read the blogs it is my understanding that deuterium depleted water is a good thing to drink when recovering from multiple sclerosis. A recent bun./urea test showed my
level of urea nitrogen at 19.1 (6-23mg)
and my creatin at 0.88( 0.5-0.9 mg)

My HSCRP is at 1.29
My progesterone P4 is 0.050
I am now in my menopause. I looked into a bio available progesterone, but could not find one. Would Taking progesterone be advisable?
My thyroid results are all good and it is functioning well.

Whilst in England, I can have a DTI test to measure how much demyelination has occurred.

I can also take CoQ10 to support my healing, but I am aware that there is a dopant risk. It was however mentioned in one of the recent blocks as supporting healing in MS.

I will use a red light therapy box daily and I will continue to watch the sunrise and the sunset but light stability in England is horrendous as we know.

I will do CT

My major win since being here for 4 1/2 months is my improved bowel movement, which now occurs daily. It makes me feel so happy.

Jack, is there anything you think I could also be doing in the UK to support my healing.

Many thanks
Joanne

> Realizing that I was deteriorating very quickly, I saw a kinesiologist who threw muscle testing, told me to stop eating fruit, sugar, wheat, and to start eating fish and vegetables. After a few months I started to slowly improve. I still could not walk. The improvements came about as improved energy , improved brain function, and less spasticity in my legs. Even now if I eat fruit, my legs feel very, very heavy and very numb
>
> In 2011 I became pregnant and the improvements came. I got out of the wheelchair and started to walk first with the Zimmer frame, then onto crutches and then onto one stick since being a El Salvador. I have gone back to using two sticks, but that’s because of the beach. It’s not a sign of decline.
> Since having my daughter in 2012, my energy levels are 80% better I can walk for up to an hour around the park and at times my feet have moved properly, which has enabled me to walk at a proper speed with a proper gate as my hips have Moved forward as I walk and held themselves as they are meant to. None of this has held and these improvements lasted for a couple of weeks and then the old patterning returned.
>
> I discovered Jack Kruse in October 2024 and I moved to El Salvador with my 13-year-old daughter in January 2025. I had my consultation with jack in February 2025 after moving here.
> Since my consultation with jack, I have seen the sunrise and the sunset every day.
> I have spent four hours on the beach sometimes longer and I’m very very brown. I am always outside with just a kiniki bikini on. I don’t have any artificial light after nightfall. I sleep in a bedroom with very little low EMF and I eat a low deteurium diet.
> I also try and do three CT sessions each week with the temperature at 12 to 15°C for 20 minutes.
>
> Since introducing CT the improvements are very noticeable. After CT My legs feel lighter my feet move more easily and I have more energy. I also go to the loo daily. This is huge as constipation has been an issue for 22 years.
>
> I have been, and I am still currently reading the demylination blogs. I realize now that I crushed my GLP for many years. I am now supporting my GLP and have been since my arrival in El Salvador in January. I will add fermented foods.
>
> I am also a MTHFR with COMTE at 40%. Listening to jack on a recent podcast talking about CT and EMF, I understand why my body is improving with CT as it helps the body recover from the distress caused by EMF.
>
> I have to return to England in a few days to sort out a few housing financial things, and will return to El Salvador as quickly as I can.
> Whilst in England, I will
> Drink deteurium depleted water. Having read the blogs it is my understanding that deuterium depleted water is a good thing to drink when recovering from multiple sclerosis. A recent bun./urea test showed my
> level of urea nitrogen at 19.1 (6-23mg)
> and my creatin at 0.88( 0.5-0.9 mg)
>
> My HSCRP is at 1.29
> My progesterone P4 is 0.050
> I am now in my menopause. I looked into a bio available progesterone, but could not find one. Would Taking progesterone be advisable?
> My thyroid results are all good and it is functioning well.
>
> Whilst in England, I can have a DTI test to measure how much demyelination has occurred.
>
> I can also take CoQ10 to support my healing, but I am aware that there is a dopant risk. It was however mentioned in one of the recent blocks as supporting healing in MS.
>
> I will use a red light therapy box daily and I will continue to watch the sunrise and the sunset but light stability in England is horrendous as we know.
>
> I will do CT
>
> My major win since being here for 4 1/2 months is my improved bowel movement, which now occurs daily. It makes me feel so happy.
>
> Jack, is there anything you think I could also be doing in the UK to support my healing.
>
> Many thanks
> Joanne

Hey Jo, we met in El Salvador and spent some time together on Atami beach. I just wanted to say it is great to hear that you're healing! I look forward to following your updates!

### Jack Kruse — 2025-06-06T05:53:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chromosomal-microdeletions-2p13-3-to-2p13-1-w-exoc6b-gene.31054/post-354073>

The deleitions are linked to MT problems in your wife's centrosome of her eggs if the kid has the defect in every cell. My bet is the kid is not got the defect in every cell......it is probably a mosaic related to her UPEs in her stem cell line. Could your sunglasses have linked to this..........**YES**

The DM blogs 5-7 lay it all out. Chromosomal separation is a job of MT. MT need optimized UPEs to separate the chromosomes properly. This genetic defect is from poor parental MT function.

So when you wear sunglasses on this forum and think it is funny, you maybe shocked to know that those glasses could polarize your germ cell line to cause this defect. So yeah it is big fucking deal here. -----> **READ IT. [https://threadreaderapp.com/thread/1930629376795627528.html](https://threadreaderapp.com/thread/1930629376795627528.html)**

Using my decentralized photo-bioelectric framework, we can identify the most probable cause of this abnormality as poor parental MT function due to disrupted UPE coherence, driven by environmental stressors.

Photo-Bioelectric Loop

Sunlight drives UPE production (200–300 nm) via the mitochondrial semiconductor (melanin-mtDNA loop), collapsing MT wave functions to produce qualia (Hameroff & Penrose, 1994). UPEs at 220 nm, emitted by mtDNA, DNA, and blood, activate the leptin-melanocortin pathway, sustaining consciousness (Van Wijk, 2014). In mitosis, UPEs ensure MT fidelity for chromosomal separation.

Arc Welding Analogy

MT dynamics are analogized to arc welding: MTs form the weld seam, UPEs the arc, and structured water (e.g., EZ water in centrosomes) the shielding gas. Environmental stressors introduce defects, impairing MT function (Kruse, 2025).

VDR and Auxins as Fire Alarms
The vitamin D receptor (VDR) on the inner mitochondrial membrane (IMM) slows TCA cycle flux, reducing reactive oxygen/nitrogen species (ROS/RNS) and ensuring UPE coherence (Kazemi et al., 2013). In plants, auxins regulate xylem dynamics, maintaining biophoton coherence (Trewavas, 2014).

Genetic Analysis

The microdeletion (2p13.3 to 2p13.1) was assessed for its impact on EXOC6B function, focusing on centrosome MT dynamics during oogenesis (Wen et al., 2019). Mosaic expression was hypothesized based on UPE variability in stem cell lines.

UPE Fidelity Calculation

Your latitude, solar irradiance did not yield > 1.76 × 10^17 UPE photons daily (Van Wijk, 2014). UPE coherence (SNR) was modeled using Shannon’s theory, with disruptions (e.g., nnEMF) increasing ROS and broadening the UPE spectrum (Shannon, 1948).

Evolutionary Analysis

Historical data on oxygen levels, UV penetration, and extinction events in utero were not disclosed so we do not know. We know the chromospmes were fucked = MT had to be fucked = mtDNA LED UPE array was fucked because of Mom or dada light or temperature environment

Modeled Results

Chromosomal Microdeletion and MT Dysfunction

The 2p13.3 to 2p13.1 microdeletion disrupts EXOC6B, impairing exocyst-mediated vesicle trafficking to the centrosome (Fogelgren et al., 2011). This leads to defective MT nucleation and dynamics, causing errors in chromosomal separation during oogenesis (e.g., aneuploidy). Mosaic expression in the child likely results from variability in UPEs within the maternal stem cell line, as UPEs govern MT assembly/disassembly (Hameroff & Penrose, 1994). Defect likley in INTERPAHSE in the germ line.
UPE Fidelity and Chromosomal Separation

MTs in the mitotic spindle rely on UPEs (200–300 nm) to maintain quantum coherence, collapsing wave functions to coordinate tubulin polymerization/depolymerization (Hameroff & Penrose, 1994). At 13°N latitude, optimal UPE production (1.76 × 10^17 photons daily) ensures high SNR, but stressors (e.g., nnEMF) reduce UPE coherence, increasing ROS/RNS and impairing MT fidelity (Van Wijk, 2014).

Most Probable Cause: Poor Parental MT Function

The most probable cause of the microdeletion is poor parental MT function, driven by reduced UPE coherence. Environmental stressors, nnEMF (e.g., Wi-Fi, 5G), circadian disruption (e.g., dim light, blue light at night sunglasses), and lack of grounding all increase ROS/RNS, disrupting the VDR’s regulation of TCA cycle flux (Bikle, 2020). This broadens the UPE spectrum, impairing MT dynamics in parental germ cells, leading to centrosome dysfunction and chromosomal errors (Kruse, 2025). Dim lighting, linked to altered consciousness and reduced alertness (Biswas et al., 2016), exacerbates this by limiting UPE production.

Evolutionary Context

GOE (~2.4 Gya): Rising oxygen necessitated the VDR to manage ROS/RNS, ensuring UPE coherence for MT function in early eukaryotes (Lyons et al., 2014).

Cambrian Explosion (~541 Mya): Increased oxygen and UV light enhanced UPE-driven MT fidelity, supporting complex cell division (Butterfield, 2011).

K-T Event (~66 Mya): Darkness reduced UPEs, but adaptive mechanisms (e.g., VDR, auxins) maintained minimal MT function (Schulte et al., 2010).

UPEs and Chromosomal Separation

UPE fidelity is critical for MT assembly/disassembly during chromosomal separation. The microdeletion’s impact on EXOC6B disrupts centrosome MT nucleation, but mosaic expression suggests UPE variability in stem cells, potentially mitigating severity (Wen et al., 2019). Parental exposure to nnEMF and circadian disruption likely reduced UPE coherence, impairing MT function and causing the microdeletion (Kruse, 2025).

Clinical Implications for this defect is expected craniofacial issues with neurological issues.

In this disease, similar MT dysfunction occurs due to tau pathology, exacerbated by reduced UPEs from environmental stressors, impairing consciousness (Zhang et al., 2020). The brain’s quantum efficiency (1.84 billion times greater than the NVIDIA H100 GPU) relies on UPEs at 220 nm, disrupted in both CBS and this microdeletion scenario (Kruse, 2025). XOC6B encodes a subunit of the exocyst complex, which supports centrosome function during mitosis. Its deletion impairs MT nucleation, leading to chromosomal segregation defects (Fogelgren et al., 2011).

**Expected Phenotype**

The 2p13.3 to 2p13.1 microdeletion involving EXOC6B disrupts centrosome MT dynamics, leading to chromosomal segregation errors during oogenesis (Fogelgren et al., 2011). Given the mosaic presentation, the phenotype may vary in severity but is likely to include:

Developmental Delays: Impaired exocyst function affects neuronal migration and synapse formation, causing mild to moderate intellectual disability and delayed milestones (Wen et al., 2019).

Neurological Deficits: MT dysfunction in neural progenitors may lead to hypotonia, motor delays, and seizures, similar to phenotypes in other centrosome-related disorders (e.g., microcephaly) (Jones et al., 2021).

Dysmorphic Features: Subtle facial dysmorphisms (e.g., micrognathia, low-set ears) and growth retardation are common in EXOC6B-related deletions (Wen et al., 2019).

Mosaic Variability: The mosaic nature, driven by mtDNA and blod UPEs drives variability in the maternal stem cell line, may result in milder symptoms in some tissues, potentially sparing vital organs (Kruse, 2025).

THE FUTURE
The model estimate a reduced life expectancy of 40–60 years, influenced by environmental stressors and heteroplasmy changes over time, which may exacerbate mitochondrial dysfunction, increasing the risk of corticobasal syndrome (CBS)-like symptoms, cancer, and organ failure. Therapeutic strategies to enhance UPE coherence are proposed to mitigate progression. Life expectancy is influenced by the severity of neurological deficits and secondary complications from heteroplasmy changes. Based on comparable disorders (e.g., Rett syndrome, mitochondrial encephalopathies), the most probable estimate: is40–60 Years: Mild to moderate cases may reach middle age with supportive care, but severe neurological involvement (e.g., intractable seizures) or organ dysfunction from heteroplasmy (e.g., cardiomyopathy) may reduce lifespan (Gorman et al., 2016). Environmental stressors (e.g., nnEMF) accelerating heteroplasmy could shorten this further (KRUSE, 2025).

> Clinical Implications for this defect is expected craniofacial issues with neurological issues.
>
> In this disease, similar MT dysfunction occurs due to tau pathology, exacerbated by reduced UPEs from environmental stressors, impairing consciousness (Zhang et al., 2020). The brain’s quantum efficiency (1.84 billion times greater than the NVIDIA H100 GPU) relies on UPEs at 220 nm, disrupted in both CBS and this microdeletion scenario (Kruse, 2025). XOC6B encodes a subunit of the exocyst complex, which supports centrosome function during mitosis. Its deletion impairs MT nucleation, leading to chromosomal segregation defects (Fogelgren et al., 2011).
>
> **Expected Phenotype**
>
> The 2p13.3 to 2p13.1 microdeletion involving EXOC6B disrupts centrosome MT dynamics, leading to chromosomal segregation errors during oogenesis (Fogelgren et al., 2011). Given the mosaic presentation, the phenotype may vary in severity but is likely to include:
>
> Developmental Delays: Impaired exocyst function affects neuronal migration and synapse formation, causing mild to moderate intellectual disability and delayed milestones (Wen et al., 2019).
>
> Neurological Deficits: MT dysfunction in neural progenitors may lead to hypotonia, motor delays, and seizures, similar to phenotypes in other centrosome-related disorders (e.g., microcephaly) (Jones et al., 2021).
>
> Dysmorphic Features: Subtle facial dysmorphisms (e.g., micrognathia, low-set ears) and growth retardation are common in EXOC6B-related deletions (Wen et al., 2019).
>
> Mosaic Variability: The mosaic nature, driven by mtDNA and blod UPEs drives variability in the maternal stem cell line, may result in milder symptoms in some tissues, potentially sparing vital organs (Kruse, 2025).
>
>
> THE FUTURE
> The model estimate a reduced life expectancy of 40–60 years, influenced by environmental stressors and heteroplasmy changes over time, which may exacerbate mitochondrial dysfunction, increasing the risk of corticobasal syndrome (CBS)-like symptoms, cancer, and organ failure. Therapeutic strategies to enhance UPE coherence are proposed to mitigate progression. Life expectancy is influenced by the severity of neurological deficits and secondary complications from heteroplasmy changes. Based on comparable disorders (e.g., Rett syndrome, mitochondrial encephalopathies), the most probable estimate: is40–60 Years: Mild to moderate cases may reach middle age with supportive care, but severe neurological involvement (e.g., intractable seizures) or organ dysfunction from heteroplasmy (e.g., cardiomyopathy) may reduce lifespan (Gorman et al., 2016). Environmental stressors (e.g., nnEMF) accelerating heteroplasmy could shorten this further (KRUSE, 2025).

Could someone have a microdeletion whereby symptoms present as an infant (hypotonia, motor delay, slower development) then appear to totally normalize neurologically through teenage years and young adulthood - but then at age 27 they start having neurological motor issues (stumbling, fear of falling, paralyzing muscle spasms > now only crawling/wheelchair bound)? This is what has happened to my brother and I'm wondering if the cause is an unidentified microdeletion. Neurologists have labelled it FND potentially due to emotional trauma, but labelling is all they have managed to do. Could it be that a stressor has exacerbated mitochondrial dysfunction after a period of apparent normality but actually a microdeletion is the root of the problem?

This is my first entry into the journal. Found Doc Jack in Aug 2024.

Condition : PCOS since age 14
Current : Suffering from secondary ammenohrea since April 2024.
Started implementing the teachings from Dr. Jack since last year (despite going through major life events - lost my father just before my wedding ), I was able to come back to my lifestyle changes and after several months my testosterone is finally down from 68 ng/dl to 53 ng/dl (was elevated due to insulin resistance ) . For the first time in my life the testosterone is within 1 standard deviation of the normal mark. I was never able to tame that beast before since I was a teenager.
Other notable changes
1\. Great hair quality , and they are growing at an incredible speed
2\. No longer suffering from cold extremities
3\. No longer suffering from afternoon energy crashes and struggling to keep my eyes open
4\. Absolutely no caffeine dependency
5\. Super tanned and super energetic throughout the day.

Work in progress
1\. Resume ovulation
2\. Resume period cycle

So grateful to find you Uncle Jack

### Jack Kruse — 2025-06-06T05:26:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/divyas-optimal-journal.31055/post-354070>

**The GOE as an Electrical Crisis: That caused the first cancer: Endosymbiosis and Heme Proteins stop it.**

The GOE introduced a new player to Earth’s environment: molecular oxygen. For early anaerobic life forms, oxygen was a toxic electrical stressor. Oxygen, with its high electronegativity, changes the electrical resistance of biological membranes, disrupting the delicate balance of charge that early cells relied on for survival. Membranes, which are essentially lipid bilayers, act as capacitors, storing and managing electrical gradients critical for cellular function. The sudden presence of oxygen would have altered these gradients, creating an existential crisis for early life.

This is where endosymbiosis comes into play. Decentralizied Medicine suggests that bacteria and Archea looked at each other as an infection under attack by oxygen that joined them in a bad marriage (cancer) and innovated heme proteins to protect the electrical gradients in membranes while also innovating a new cellular process called apoptosis. People forget oxygen can induce electric and magnetic fields because of its paramagnetism. This means it can affect the oxidation state of iron in the +2 or +3 state. Iron cannot bond oxygen for transport in both oxidation states. It appears like took full advantage of this with mitochondria.

Both of these operations, water creation and apoptosis, were under the control of a heme protein called cytochrome c oxidase. As a result, after this evolutionary event just about any infection stressor.

Water acts like a heat sink for the newly formed cancerous merger of Archaea and Bacteria. What does a heat sink do to semiconductiors?

It alters their light emission: UPEs were born.

UPE = transgeneration epigenetics becomes possible.

Genes no longer control much. LIGHT DOES. Before genes there was ferrodxin biology. This also was light controlled. Ferrodoxin = Fe-S on cytochromes that align the IMJs on cristae in mitochondria.

Iron isevery where in this story.

Your sex steroid problem is also controlled by a CYP heme enzyme.

I got my periods naturally . I am most tanned I have been in my life , but thanks to your guidance , my body got there on it's own. Just wanted to say congrats on your progress thus far.

The first thought that came to my mind is this is based on your environment. The vitamin D levels and Bun:creatinine ratio tell a story of nnemf toxicity. No matter how much you are in the sun, you can not make vitamin D when you are dehydrated on a cellular level. nnemf is that main driving factor of cellular dehydration. Think more along the lines of both personal tech use and living in a densely populated area when it comes to nnemf issues. If you live in a densely populated area, the interference of nnemf can also disrupt the ability to assimilate key light frequencies such as UVB. There is much more to the story when you factor in all the downstream effects of pseudohypoxia, photoreceptor destruction, circadian disruption, etc.

DDW can definitely help, but the water your body makes is more important than the water you drink. The desert analogy Jack posted about in this thread still rings true. Your body is currently a desert, it is time to terraform your cellular environment. You need the AM IR/UV light to build that exclusion zone, rehydrate melanin, and regenerate while eliminating nnemf as much as possible. If you don't address this issue you will be back at square one sooner than you think.

### ddarbari — 2026-01-21T18:28:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/divyas-optimal-journal.31055/post-365692>

Hi [@Nathaniel\_Nathanson66](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/32461/) , sorry for the late response , I was traveling to India. My progress to be honest was very gradual and at times I did feel like giving up but I stayed with it . I have been very religious with the timing of my sun exposure. In India I was able to spend almost a month and I didn't let a single day go by without soaking in at least 5-6 hours of sunlight despite it being winters (which means poor solar yield compared to summers). I also followed my red light glasses routine and morning Sunlight . Apart from that I take 1 30 mins sun session in afternoon (rain or shine ) non negotiable and expose as much skin as I possibly can during Seattle winters (Sometimes it just means my face and eyes but still ).
I haven't been able to tolerate cold plunges so well so I aim for 30-45 sec of cold water at the end of my shower or face dunk in ice water at least 2 times a week. I am also focused on eating real food and in the evening I use the red light in my room and lights out by 10PM everyday . Also DDW water. Needless to say it took about 3-4 months to do the habit stacking for all these changes and to be honest I am still working on myself but yes I am very happy with the changes and extremely grateful for this knowledge.

The key was I kept coming back to re-hear and re-read uncle jack's words to make sure I stay on track until it got really drilled in my head. His words were really powerful motivator and guide in my transition.

Major changes, really stable energy throughout the day , no afternoon slump, no extreme cravings , no bloating issues and most surprisingly I don't crave junk media as well. My concentration during work and cognition as so significantly improved that I am surprised to see that it was never a function of my willpower , rather it is biological .

Last and most imp change as a female I was really struggling to get my cycles (I had extremely irregular cycles and it vanished months on end). Now I am getting them on a much more regulated cadence and much better flow (I had really scanty flow earlier)

Correct, i do try to go south and work remotely during the time sun gets weaker for a couple of weeks on and off . I am incredibly amazed at the progress my body is showing . Since childhood I had problems with irregular periods with absent cycles often upto 6-8 months. I am able to get my cycles consistently in 45 days with no external medicine just regular sleep schedule , DDW water and sun as much as I can. # Thread: When you see the fire, and others only feel the heat
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-you-see-the-fire-and-others-only-feel-the-heat.31056/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jessnet3 — 2025-06-06T18:40:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-you-see-the-fire-and-others-only-feel-the-heat.31056/post-354112>

Lately I’ve felt a loneliness on this path, and wondered how others in this community might have grappled with the same. As a mother to two children with chronic issues, I often find myself having to explain unconventional choices to people who can’t even grasp the basics. That part I accept as par for the course.

What I still struggle with is carrying knowledge that could help others, while they refuse to listen.

Currently, I’m witnessing a friend’s five-year-old daughter dying of cancer, under the care of doctors who should know better but don’t. I’ve made several attempts to wake them up. And still, the inertia of conventional thought prevails. It’s devastating.

Despite countless hours spent sharing what I know, I still see that little girl lying in a hospital bed, an iPad on her lap at night, while chemo ravages her body and pneumonia sets in. She is going to die. And they will never hear me.

How do we hold space for truth in a world addicted to comfortable illusions?

I think of the power in John Galt’s refusal to carry the emotional weight of other people’s denial. I admire that strength. But I’m not fully there yet.

If you’ve stood in this space and found peace (or at least direction), I would be grateful for your wisdom.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-06-06T18:57:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-you-see-the-fire-and-others-only-feel-the-heat.31056/post-354115>

Being solitary is a necessary to re order your thinking. You must subtract the noise from your life to get the signal

SHANNON'S THEREOM

### rtd — 2025-06-06T20:04:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-you-see-the-fire-and-others-only-feel-the-heat.31056/post-354121>

> [Jessnet3 said:](/goto/post?id=354112)
>
> Lately I’ve felt a loneliness on this path, and wondered how others in this community might have grappled with the same.

For me, I have found listening to YouTube videos and lectures, reading the blogs posts along with suggested literature provided in the forum scratches my itch. There is a lot of information in the blogs, most which I do not know and have to side research.

Engaging with physical people, versus online/digital people, regarding this material has been a futility test. I was reintroduced to my limit and the adage "know your audience". The people around me don't have the time, knowledge, personal interest or desire to discuss things outside of the weather, re-parroted news cycle snippets, or which Real Housewife is a C you Next Tuesday, never mind decentralized health topics.

One person asked me about my weight loss- my PCP's nurse. I shared I was trying, and sometimes failing, to do the "Leptin Protocol at jackkruse.com". For another person I printed out The Quilt and gave my EpiPaleo RX book to them after they shared with me they are pre-diabetic. That was two months ago. I see the person every two weeks or so. I don't mention or bring up the book or the content. Consistently they tell me they haven't read any of it yet, like a confession. I say, "ok" and move on.

Many people reject something, anything new. They are so coded, so embedded within their box, their belief system- being blinded by it- no matter how bad the outcome already is or will be. They think and are inside their box. It's a waste of time and energy to persuade them to try something new and to think outside their box- for a start. My take? I always say: there is no box. Just a curious mind. But the latter is something that you either have or you don't. It's the sad truth.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-06-06T23:55:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wine.31058/post-354141>

A study by Olivier Salières, conducted in 2021 at Laboratoire ENERLAB, investigated biophoton emission levels in wines produced from different vitivinicultural practices, conventional, organic, and biodynamic. The key findings, as referenced from the Château de la Vieille Chapelle website, are summarized as follows:

Biophoton Emission Measurement: The study measured ultra-weak photon emissions (biophotons) from wine samples, which are thought to reflect the vitality or energy state of the product.

Comparison Across Practices: It compared wines from conventional, organic, and biodynamic viticulture. Biodynamic wines showed distinct biophoton emission patterns compared to conventional and organic wines, potentially indicating differences in vitality or quality linked to these practices.

Implications: The results suggest that biophoton emission could serve as a non-invasive tool to assess the quality or energetic properties of wines, with biodynamic wines possibly exhibiting higher coherence or vitality in their emissions.

[https://www.chateau-de-la-vieille-chapelle.com/file/Measurements\_of\_the\_level\_of\_biophoton\_emission.pdf.](https://www.chateau-de-la-vieille-chapelle.com/file/Measurements_of_the_level_of_biophoton_emission.pdf.[]\(https://www.chateau-de-la-vieille-chapelle.com/en/documentation\))

### Jack Kruse — 2025-10-05T22:11:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wine.31058/post-360449>

While I am food & wine tasting my way through the world this summer I found myself in a conversation while tasting in wine with a couple from the Napa. Our host from Carter Vineyards was a young women who was 4th generation in the family business. The topic was sales and they were down. One reason: younger people are drinking less. Something we can all relate to. Then she was asked how much she drank.

“Only 2 or 3x per week, but my nonna drinks a lot more”

“How much more?”, we asked.

Blushing, “about a bottle a day”

Surprised, “and how old is nonna?”

“94!”

We then all toasted to nonna. I casually mentioned the story of Jean Calment to our group too. 122.4 months old and drank red port every day.

I wonder how old our younger generations will be when they realize how much they’ve missed avoiding wine. When they learn that, in fact, a Big Mac is more unhealthy than a glass of wine. All the meals that could have tasted better. All the conversations that could have been more interesting from the dopamine perspective. All the learned experiences missed.

All I know is wine will prevail as people adjust. Just as it always has for thousands of years. I do worry that high nnEMF regions will make this equation upside down in zipcodes.

### Johan Lindøm — 2025-10-05T22:59:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wine.31058/post-360451>

Buying High-Biophoton Wines for wines with 20-25% higher biophoton levels (vitality), seek biodynamic or organic bottles with minimal intervention (unfiltered, low sulfur). Prioritize Demeter or EU-organic labels for "living" methods from France, Italy, or beyond.

### Johan Lindøm — 2025-10-07T10:28:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wine.31058/post-360557>

Context is everything.

Wine from high-latitude, high-elevation grapes—with high UPEs, low sulfurs, DDW-based, and high solar-fermented polyphenols (resveratrol)—amps up mitochondria via light/water/magnetism and redox. That's the winner's prize.

DDW twist: Cold, high-altitude bring deuterium low, better EZ water, better electron tunneling and ROS drop. In a 2018 FB post, he told us to cap at 2 glasses of DDW wine to reap it without overload.

How's your redox? Fake frequencies like ALAN, plus alcohol, deplete vitamin A and wreak chaos on your photoperiod. You won't thrive, sleep, or regenerate—and your immunity will degenerate.

Alcohol disrupts circadian rhythms and tweaking clock genes like BMAL1/PER, tanking metabolism, hormones, and immunity. Effects hinge on timing: Late-night doses increase photoperiod entropy. Minimal impact daytime/early evening.

# Thread: Treating Toxoplasmosis Gondii infection naturally?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/treating-toxoplasmosis-gondii-infection-naturally.31060/>
**Subforum:** Feedback/Suggestions
**Posts:** 3

### fouadfo1 — 2025-06-07T06:02:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/treating-toxoplasmosis-gondii-infection-naturally.31060/post-354166>

Any suggestions for treating toxoplasmosis gondii naturally? The pharmaceuticals used (pyrimethamine) is highly toxic.

Thanks !

### Martin D. — 2025-08-25T17:51:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/treating-toxoplasmosis-gondii-infection-naturally.31060/post-358713>

Hi mitochondriacs, I have a question, hoping you can help me out. My wife and I are a little worried.

My wife has a very good immunity to toxoplasmosis because we have kittens and she grew up with them.

She is 15 weeks pregnant and recently tested positive for toxoplasmosis. Very rare. I don't know if it could be a false positive; she wasn't exposed to cat feces. We don't know if it could be due to improperly washed fruit or vegetables; we don't think so, since everyone is exposed to this.
My wife has a fantastic diet, rich in DHA. We take care of our circadian rhythms like the most precious thing we have. We expose ourselves to the sun as Jack taught us. Her vitamin D is even 95 and the environment is low in nnemf because we have tested it.
The obstetrician tells us that we should have an amniocentesis in the second trimester to test for congenital toxoplasmosis. We don't know if it's very risky. Any comments, opinions, or suggestions on what we should do are more than welcome and appreciated. Thank you all.

Here I share the results

Page 1 – Immunology

Toxoplasma gondii, Ac. IgG
• Result: 172 kUI/L
• Reference value: up to 6 (non-immune)

Toxoplasma gondii, Ac. IgM
• Result: Reactive
• Cutoff index: 6.5
• Reference: < 0.80

Toxoplasma gondii, IgG Avidity Test
• Result: 59 %
• Interpretation:
• <70: IgG with low avidity
• 70–79: IgG with intermediate avidity
• 79: IgG with high avidity

Vitamin D3 – 25-Hydroxycholecalciferol
• Result: 95 μg/L

Free Triiodothyronine – Free T3 (T3L)
• Result: 3.1 ng/L
• Reference: 2.0–3.8

Anti-peroxidase Antibodies (TPO, Ac.)
• Result: 17 kUI/L
• Reference: up to 34

Free Thyroxine – Free T4 (T4L)
• Result: 0.9 ng/dL
• Reference values:
2nd & 3rd trimester: 0.5–1.6

Progesterone (PRG)
• Result: 28.3 μg/L
2nd trimester: 25.6–89.4

### Jack Kruse — 2025-10-08T18:23:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/treating-toxoplasmosis-gondii-infection-naturally.31060/post-360668>

Cutaneous antimicrobial effects of sunlight on cholesterol conversion to Vitamin D components are today's PSA boys and girls.

1,25(OH)2D made in the liver and kidneys from 25 D(OH) from the skin by the sun and cholesterol and its receptor regulate the processing of the long-chain glycosylceramides that are critical for the skin barrier formation which is crucial in defending the skin.

Do you know how the heme protein enzymes CYP control this process?

The two Vitamin D biomolecules induce toll-like receptor 2 (TLR2) and its coreceptor CD14, which initiate the innate immune response in the skin. Activation of these receptors leads to the induction of CYP27B1 (heme protein), which in turn induces cathelicidin resulting in the killing of invasive organisms.
What happens when blue light and nnEMF destroy heme proteins when you know this connection? Innate immunity is destroyed. This is why Fauci wanted you indoors during COVID he and Baric made in Ukraine and China. [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2843519/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2843519/)

# Thread: Covid - prevention and treatment
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/covid-prevention-and-treatment.31064/>
**Subforum:** Feedback/Suggestions
**Posts:** 70
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Mat.I — 2025-06-07T08:43:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/covid-prevention-and-treatment.31064/post-354176>

Got covid earlier on this week first few days were nasty, pain was out of this world, however, I just let my system take care of it, didn't take anything.. Few days after - sense of smell / taste still completely absence for.the obvious reasons and still a bit foggy.. I do not usually fuss over this stuff and not one to take any preventative IVM if I were going.to be in a higher risk place etc.. However, it seems that things are definitely getting nastier and harder hitting, could have just got me by chance as I had a lot going on and may have been operating closer to the edge then usual etc..

My questions -
What is everyone doing about this? I know some will take semi regular low dose IVM and extra when going in busy public places especially in the winter months.

Anyone following any preventative protocols that they would.like to share? Are some doing more reactive and only starting when first symptoms or suspected exposure begins. I know nicotine is great, however there is so much other garbage in some of the products.

My main focus now is getting my olfactory bulb back online with some strategic short term use of Nicotine, MB and Melatonin.

Keen to hear what others are doing and/or have done. Also, I already know about the FLCC protocols just in case someone is going to recommend that.

Thanks
Mat

### Saichi — 2025-06-07T14:40:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/covid-prevention-and-treatment.31064/post-354183>

I highly recommend growing bright leaf tobacco and making your own cigarettes in organic hemp tubes or rolling papers. I smoke every day.

### Jacks — 2025-06-07T20:33:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/covid-prevention-and-treatment.31064/post-354226>

If I have to be around crowds (which I mostly avoid if I can these days) I take nicotine before, then rinse my nasal passageways afterwards if I remember. I think some SSKI - 8-10 drops in water (if you are not eating shitload of seafood and seaweed) before going out in crowds might be another useful hack (based on reading BG#12 recently). I only had covid once in the summer of 2022 - the milder later version - but was still knocked out for about 2 days with fever & muscle aches & headache. Just slept a lot, rested, and fasted and I took a week off work since they were giving us extra leave for covid at that time. Sinus issues lingered for a bit afterwards with loss of smell/taste but that returned. I was back to fully functional within about a week. Two in my family had more respiratory symptoms - with a lingering cough, which I never got. I think it hits you at your weakest areas, as I got sinusitis which they did not get.

My respiratory side was not really affected, just the pains and fevers within the first 3 days were the worst. I will look into using nicotine if I'm going to be in busy places. For the nasal rinse I suspect 1% iodine would also work.

Thanks
Mat

### Lukassss — 2025-06-11T12:18:41+0300

Hi Mat,

When I spent a lot of time in bed and had muscle pain, I was saved by an acupressure mat—a **Lyapko mat**—which I placed under my back. It had a pain-relieving effect, and I could feel the results immediately.

I can share another story as well. I saw how this approach helped people with Covid who had trouble breathing. The method involved stimulating circulation throughout the torso (back, sides, and chest). This was done by using a bundle of long wooden skewers—the sharp points were held together in the hand and rhythmically tapped against the skin. During such a session, there were often areas on the skin that resisted getting red or didn't have the same sensation as the rest. These places required stronger stimulation to achieve a uniform reddening and improved blood flow.

It brought great relief.

Personally, I use this method especially in winter, combining it with IR (infrared) therapy. It works remarkably well when you’re grounded, exposed to sunlight, and in an environment with low non-native EMF.

Mat & Other Aussies here

Off the Australian Gov site

21 May 2025
The Australian Government welcomes the adoption of the World Health Organization (WHO) Pandemic Agreement.
The Agreement is a significant step towards strengthening pandemic prevention, preparedness and response.
The Agreement takes account of lessons learnt during the COVID-19 pandemic and supports collective action to address pandemic threats, including by strengthening multi-sectoral disease surveillance and access to vaccines.
While the Agreement has been adopted by the World Health Assembly, there are further steps remaining to finalise technical details. Australia will only commence our treaty making process after the Agreement opens for signature, which is not expected until at least mid-2026.
Once the Agreement has entered into force, Australia and our region will be better positioned to reduce pandemic risks and respond swiftly if a pandemic occurs, saving lives and mitigating the impacts on our economies. Australia will retain full sovereignty in making public health decisions which promote the interests of Australians.
Australia is proud to have served as Vice-Chair of the Intergovernmental Negotiating Body for the Pandemic Agreement, representing our diverse Western Pacific region.
This outcome reaffirms the value of multilateral solutions to shared global challenges.
Australia will continue to play our part to ensure the international community is better prepared to respond to future pandemics
Quote attributable to Minister for Foreign Affairs, Senator the Hon Penny Wong:
"We need international cooperation on health to help keep Australia, our region, and the world safe. The adoption of the WHO Pandemic Agreement demonstrates the value of the international community working together to find solutions to shared global challenges."
Quote attributable to Minister for Health and Ageing, Minister for Disability and the NDIS, the Hon Mark Butler MP:
"The next pandemic is not a matter of if, but when. We have a collective responsibility to protect public health in all of our countries. The adoption of the WHO Pandemic Agreement is an important step forward."

### Nick-Rolser — 2025-06-08T01:57:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lllt-rosacea.31071/post-354246>

With the biphasic responses commonly seen in LLLT, I’m trying to discern how much red-light exposure is the correct dose for my own N=1 in the context of rosacea. I’ve read both the Solar Callus blog and CPC #32 on rosacea, vitiligo, and psoriasis so I’d like to think I’ve got some skin in the game here both intellectually and literally.

I think I'm probably a Fitzpatrick type I or II. Does this protocol seem reasonable? Also, would dunking my face in cold water after each session be beneficial?

My current red-light panel (Mito Red Light):

**Irradiance** >115mW/cm2

**Fluence**: 2.3 J/cm²/min

**LED Peak Wavelengths Red**: 660nm / NIR 850nm (50/50)

Based off of my own research, this is how I’m planning on using it.

**Distance**: ~12 to 18 inches away from face

**Duration**: 5–8 minutes max per session (starting with 3 minutes and working up)

**Frequency**: 3–5x/week.

Any advice is greatly appreciated.

### digitald3 — 2025-06-08T04:03:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lllt-rosacea.31071/post-354248>

Are you not able to use the sun? Morning light is the best red light and midday UV is necessary. And i have a hunch that rosacea is a blue light derived problem. So blocking blue light and zero screen use after sunset is what I would try. If the rosacea is bad I wouldn’t even suggest a daylight tablet after sunset. The skin has blue light receptors so it’s flooded when the lights are on at night.

Also consider that nnEMF is blue light too. So what is your nnEMF situation in your home?

The skin has blue light receptors so it’s flooded when the lights are on at night.

I am able to use the sun right now. I live in New Hampshire currently, planning on moving to Florida in December. I opt for sunlight whenever I can, I just wanted to add the red light therapy as well since I know the importance of red light for assimilating more UV.

When I get a lot of sunlight my face gets even more flushed, which leads me to think I could be overdoing it. But I have no way of knowing what the right amount is because I can't find any info on it. I read one of Dr. Kruses forum posts on the biphasic responses of red-light therapy and how after a certain point it becomes detrimental. I also avoid blue light/screens in the evenings, even bought myself a Light Phone II with an e-ink display and plan on getting a daylight PC when I can afford one.

The one thing I haven't figured out how to mitigate is the WiFi. I just moved into an apartment with roommates so I can't simply shut it off. I'm also in an apartment complex so there are a bunch of other routers all around. I'm assuming the only solution as far as that's concerned is moving as soon as possible, which I plan on doing.

That's pretty much how I look right now, it's very frustrating. Did you find as you got more sun it simply went away or did your skin look more flushed at first? That's the problem I'm dealing with.

### Nick-Rolser — 2025-06-09T00:17:55+0300

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=354261)

> Nobody read my post on polarized light it appears....

Thanks for the suggestion, I just took a look at it. Been printing out the blogs to avoid using my laptop but I’ll read it through carefully to make sure I fully understand the implications.

I currently get all my water from a spring which if I remember correctly is more deuterium depleted. I’m also getting a lot of natural sunlight now that we’re approaching summer here in New Hampshire.

Would you advise against red light devices completely for rosacea then, since there is no polarization? I figured it might be a good way to get more IR-A as an adjunct to natural sun exposure.

### JulieBlueSky — 2025-06-09T08:35:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lllt-rosacea.31071/post-354340>

> [Nick-Rolser said:](/goto/post?id=354300)
>
> That's pretty much how I look right now, it's very frustrating. Did you find as you got more sun it simply went away or did your skin look more flushed at first? That's the problem I'm dealing with.

I didn't really worry too much about how much sun I got before midday (red light) because i started in the uk in January where there is very little sun and not much uv at all even at midday. So once I started with the circ rhythm it sort of dried to pin points and then faded. The light really wasn't strong enough (to the senses!) to cause too much flushing, it would get a bit warm but never really too much.

By March it had all faded and in fact I had great tan by then too. The alcoholic look was before I started getting outside at sunrise. ,

But cannot advise how I would tackle it in summer. My husband has a nasty patch of eczema on his leg and he finds after about 20 mins of sunlight in the morning around 10 o'clock it starts to weep, so he gives it another 10 mins and then stops, that is getting better. But we are not sure whether the weeping is damage or the skin cells going into overdrive with the light and chucking out toxins and stuff it needs to get rid of. So we are hedging our bets!

So don't know whether any of that helps or not.

### WildForWesties — 2025-06-09T21:27:45+0300

### Garry Kirk — 2025-06-09T23:16:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-to-bitcoin.31076/post-354377>

You could buy btc on the strike app and then send it to a coldcard or blockstream jade hard wallet.

Strike is very easy to use and you can set up a DCA very easily on it.

Look for youtube videos on the coldcard and blockstream jade and you're away.

Make sure you back up your seed to a steel or ti solution... plenty of them around.

NEVER share your 12 or 24 word seed with anyone, never take a photo of it, never have it on a phone or computer.

That should get you started.

### Garry Kirk — 2025-06-09T23:17:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-to-bitcoin.31076/post-354378>

### doctorwu85 — 2025-06-23T23:36:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-to-bitcoin.31076/post-355113>

> [Justine said:](/goto/post?id=355105)
>
> Do you have any advice for moving bitcoin? I have been purchasing on Coinbase and I am finding out it's not the best to buy from. Is there anyone on the forum that offers consultations to support people with understanding how to do the whole bitcoin thing? I want to keep investing in it but I feel SO NERVOUS that I'm going it wrong!

Sending from coinbase to a hot or cold wallet is super easy. Just click “send crypto” and make sure you have the address right. The fee is very low. Just keep in mind it could take up to an hour to show up in the wallet depending on the traffic on the network (usually it’s 1-5 minutes).

Coinbase has pros and cons, but min-maxing how and where you buy the BTC is probably not the best place to concentrate your research (just my opinion). Just buy as much as you can wherever you’re set up, and take it off the exchange when possible. Owning and securing your keys properly is way more important long term than figuring out how to pay a 1% fee instead of a 1.5% fee, etc.

I’m not an expert but feel free to DM if you have any questions.

### Garry Kirk — 2025-06-26T13:09:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-to-bitcoin.31076/post-355305>

> [Justine said:](/goto/post?id=355105)

Is there anyone on the forum that offers consultations to support people with understanding how to do the whole bitcoin thing? I want to keep investing in it but I feel SO NERVOUS that I'm going it wrong!

Yes.

Don't worry that it's on coinbase for now. Have you bought cold storage as previously mentioned? either a coldcard or blockstream jade?

If you have great, if not you need one first and you can't do anything until you have one.

Once you have that go to btc sessions and btc university and look up tutorials on sparrow wallet and whichever cold wallet you bought (blockstream/coldcard). Watch them all.

**Move only a SMALL amount first time** and do all this when **you have time to do it, don't rush it.** Go through the steps, and watch what's happening. Absorb the process.

When you have successfully done this, do it again! with another small amount, and then again, until you are comfortable you know what you are doing. You will get confident with it once you have done it several times. It's just like anything new.

Approx time to do this as mentioned above... maybe 7-8 hours and you'll be confident with what you're doing. An infinitesimally small amount of time for self sovereignty.

You can intellectually understand self sovereignty , but once you've done this and sat on it a while, you will feel what it's like to embody it and there's a difference.

G.

### MTA321 — 2025-06-26T17:56:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-to-bitcoin.31076/post-355319>

General advice (in my opinion):

Stick to a bitcoin only exchange when purchasing BTC. You can use other exchanges but why bother when you aren't purchasing shitcoins. I have tried both Strike and River and both are good. Typically when you sign up you will have to "KYC" which is know your customer requirements. Basically an anti-terrorism, money laundering, and general gatekeeping measure that requires you to prove you are who you say you are so you will likely have to upload a drivers license or other verification information. Most all exchanges require this on/off ramp to either get money to the exchange from your bank or vice versa. There are non-KYC options but you will have to do your own research if you want to go down that path.

Once all that is settled, you will connect your bank and transfer money over to the exchange. Typically there are fees when you purchase BTC (usually around 1% depending on the exchange) however if you plan to routinely purchase BTC ($XX every two weeks or $XX each month) you can set up reoccurring transactions and there will be no fees associated with those purchases when using Strike or River. This is usually called dollar cost averaging (DCA) but It all depends on how frequently you plan to purchase BTC whether or not you may feel it is worth it.

Many people suggest (as do I) to purchase a hard/cold wallet. The reason being if you leave your BTC on an exchange and that exchange goes belly up, gets hacked, or the government decides to intervene, you will be SOL. Hardwallets bascially give you, and only you, sole custody and power over your BTC. These wallets generate a seed phrase which acts as master key for any private key(s) you may have for recovery/backup purposes. These private key(s) are derived from the seed phrase and are stored securely on the wallet and used to sign BTC transactions. Hardwallets are basically just secure, fancy, authentication devices as they don't actually store BTC. Personally, I would only go with BTC only hardwallets that are opensourced by design. These are tried and tested and generally have fewer potential vulnerabilities. Good examples are Blockstream Jade, Trezor, and Coldcard.

How often you transfer your bitcoin from the exchange to your hardwallet is up to you and people have various preferences. It may ultimately be whatever $$ that you don't feel comfortable leaving on the exchange. It isn't very important now, but down the road you may want to look into UTXO (unspent transaction output) management. Basically each time you transfer BTC to a wallet you create a UTXO and the more you have, the higher transaction fees can potentially be down the road.

There are tons of tutorials online (BTC sessions and other great ones) on how to set up hardware wallets and make your first transactions. I personally also created a watch-only wallet to view my balance/transactions and so I can receive BTC without physically having to use my hardwallet every time. Watch-only wallets can only receive BTC so if you wanted to send BTC you need your hardwallet to sign those transactions.

This is all being said with the goal of using BTC as a store of value and not using it for routine transactions. If you plan transact with bitcoin for goods/services it is typically best practice to just create a hotwallet and use that as a "checking account" and keep your hardwallet as a "savings account".

### Jack Kruse — 2025-06-08T22:51:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/maternal-afb-elevation.31078/post-354296>

AFP levels may reflect the integrity and activity of NCC-mediated fetal sculpting and subsequent neural development, modulated by UPEs, water structure, and electromagnetic circuits. Here’s a decentralized interpretation:
**
Maternal AFP and Fetal Sculpting:
**
_NCC Activity and UPE Signaling:_ Elevated MSAFP may indicate increased fetal liver production in response to NCC distress during neurulation (4–15 weeks). Suppose melanin deficiency is present or UPE incoherence disrupts NCC migration and placode patterning (e.g., olfactory, trigeminal). In that case, the fetus may upregulate AFP as a protective or signaling molecule, detectable in maternal serum.
**
Water Structure Disruption:** The photomolecular effect and coherent domains (CDs) in the fetus’s 90% water environment are critical for UPE diffusion. Maternal dehydration, high deuterium (e.g., from diet or nnEMF), or blue light exposure could alter water sinks, impairing UPE spectra and triggering fetal stress, reflected as elevated MSAFP.
**
Electromagnetic Feedback:** Hormonal shifts (e.g., hCG, estrogen) during this period may amplify electromagnetic feedback loops. This could be linked to the CYP heme enzyme that controls this process. Suppose NCCs signal developmental anomalies via UPEs or magnetic fields. In that case, the maternal system might interpret this as a need to increase AFP, potentially as a compensatory response to hyperemesis gravidarum (HG).

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/afb-elevation-in-infants-risk-of-autism-post-jab-or-sickness.31079/post-354297>

**Elevated Infant AFP at 7 Months:**

_Ongoing Neural Maturation:_ At 7 months, the brain undergoes significant myelination and neural circuit refinement, processes in which NCC-derived glia (e.g., Schwann cells, oligodendrocytes) play a crucial role. Elevated AFP could indicate persistent NCC activity or stress, possibly due to disrupted UPE-mediated microtubule (MT) dynamics in the recursive loop.

_Liver-NCC Interaction:_ The fetal liver, a significant source of AFP, may remain active if NCC-driven developmental processes (e.g., cranial ganglia, optic cup) are incomplete or aberrant. This could reflect a photobioelectric imbalance, such as melanin deficiency or nnEMF interference, delaying neural maturation.

_Pathological vs. Developmental:_ While elevated AFP at this age is often linked to hepatoblastoma, a photobioelectric perspective suggests it may also signal atypical NCC function (e.g., atavistic patterns), potentially correlating with neurodevelopmental conditions such as autism.
