### Mat.I — 2025-07-31T11:23:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-for-gps-in-australia-re-ldn.31241/post-357269>

> [Mat.I said:](/goto/post?id=355714)
>
> Hello,
> I'm helping someone out as laid out in - [http://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-dose-naltrexdone-ldn.31171/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-dose-naltrexdone-ldn.31171/)
>
> We have provided a general overview of our suggested approach to the psychiatrist, however, this person is incredibly hard to get a hold of between the telehealth appointments as his primary focus is research and administering psychedelics. She has only a telehealth catch up once every 6 months just to stay on the books (in the event that any further assistance is required). The next telehealth isn't until late Nov.
>
> Are there any open minded GP's that would considered prescribing LDN to this person? her journal is here - [http://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journal-for-my-friend.30479/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journal-for-my-friend.30479/)
>
> **In summary (part of what was sent to the psych) "**the intention is not to replace or reduce Amisulpride at this stage, but to add LDN as a terrain support layer: a kind of biological insurance policy to help stabilize glial tone, reduce neuroinflammation, and support redox and myelin repair. From what I understand, LDN works by modulating microglial activity and improving endogenous opioid balance, which may help preserve emotional connection, symbolic filtering, and inner agency while continuing antipsychotic support.
>
> LDN’s broader effects, including glial M1 to M2 rebalancing, redox protection, endorphin rebound, and immune modulation, may also support REM sleep quality, myelin resilience, and overall stress tolerance. The aim is not to duplicate what Amisulpride is doing, but to support the terrain upstream: improving glial tone, redox buffering, and symbolic filtering so that perception and emotional input can be processed without needing to rely solely on dopamine suppression.
>
> The proposed approach is conservative: starting at 0.25 mg at night and increasing slowly every 10 to 14 days if tolerated, with a target dose between 0.75 and 2.0 mg. It would not exceed 2.0 mg in total.
>
> I understand that LDN is not TGA approved for this use, but it is legally prescribable off label in Australia and has been used internationally in neuroimmune and psychiatric contexts, including chronic fatigue, trauma related dysregulation, mood instability, and terrain prone psychosis. It has a strong safety profile at low doses and is well tolerated in clinical practice. To my knowledge, there are no pharmacological interactions between LDN and Amisulpride at these doses.
>
> In this case (where used appropriately) the potential benefit clearly outweighs the risk, especially given the goal is to preserve resilience and support symbolic and emotional integration while remaining on antipsychotic medication".
>
> I just do not want the long term amisulpride (albeit low dose) to destroy this person long term. The psych was baffled as to how such a low dose has been so effective for so long.. I know why but he does not and that is the problem with centralised medicine.
>
> If there are any GP's out there or may know of some open minded one's that would be open to consider this on it's merits, please advise or alternatively send me a DM if you want to remain private.
>
> Thank you.
> Mat

We have now found an open minded GP who is very well versed in LDN and supports our strategy and has provided the script for compounding.. Please disregard the request.

### riksa — 2025-07-04T16:52:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stupid-question-about-the-mrna-gene-integration.31243/post-355724>

There has to be a lot of people who had their whole genome sequenced, before they took mRNA vaccines, even millions. So why not just definitively confirm any changes in their DNA by doing that sequencing again.. right? What If a 100 people come up with irrefutable evidence, a thousand, 10,000? Put that into a study, how could that even be argued against? Weren't these DNA testing companies popular even a decade ago?

Particularly the "long-covid" / vax-injury cohort. I'm sure they seek justice and would agree to it, so If I had wealth why wouldn't I just pay the tests for them? What am I missing?

### Jack Kruse — 2025-07-04T17:33:21+0300

### riksa — 2025-07-04T19:41:59+0300

Right, and I probably don't understand the big picture but I'm thinking in terms of convincing the everyday person. E.g I think hearing the words "SV-40" and getting explained what it does(with proof) would still just not register, you need extra cognitive resources to process those abstractions and you don't want to believe them anyway, which is what is defaulted to. But I think a direct and simple "Of the n people who had the vax, n had their DNA changed permanently" would register, as the "this wont change your DNA we promise" buzz-phrase is imprinted unto everyone's minds. Shattering the illusion.

I remember my thought processes back when I was _really_ low dopamine and nnEMF toxic, so I'm thinking from that perspective. DNA is not novel to anyone, so it would bypass those restrictions in cognition. Does that make any sense?

# Thread: Help with BTC
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-btc.31246/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 20
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### JenniferM — 2025-07-04T23:27:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-btc.31246/post-355758>

We are going to invest in our future the next few days. I really need info on the best and safest way to buy BTC. The who, what, when and where?

### Anthony Smith — 2025-07-05T02:10:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-btc.31246/post-355770>

Use the search here on the forum.
Go to youtube, lots and lots of info there. Listen to the good the bad and the ugly and let your discernment inform and equip you to make the right decision - for you. It is important you don’t just listen to ANYONE and do what they did . . . You need to own this and make the right decision for you. I did find “Crypto Casey” was easy to understand and had some good background info that helped me get my head around the basics.

Start with a very few $, buy, sell, trade get comfortable with the process before moving larger amount's.

### PIAZ — 2025-07-05T03:08:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-btc.31246/post-355773>

> Start with a very few $, buy, sell, trade get comfortable with the process before moving larger amount's.

Thank you Anthony, we were thinking small amount first before a big buy and wondered if others did that. You confirmed it for me. I'll check out the guy on youtube also. Thanks.
[@Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) , Tanstaafl (there ain't no such thing as a free lunch). I went al thru all of your BTC thread picking out info here and there. My intention was asking if others found places more secure, unsecure, ect. The ratings on Coinbase have mixed reviews on the good, bad and ugly.of who, what, when, where. There are two more I will check out also.

### Mat.I — 2025-07-05T11:16:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-btc.31246/post-355786>

Buy from a good exchange (OTC), however move straight (immediately) to cold storage. Do not leave too much BTC on the exchange. I personally do not leave anything over $5K (previously was $10K) on the exchange unless, I need more there for a reason. Also, it is.best practice to have a "clean" laptop just for.BTC. Before, I got into BTC, I.did lots of research on.security and after the research we did a consult with a "security" expert who I knew more than and said what I was talking about was unnecessary and way over the top.. He wasn't a security expert's a-hole.. So, anyone is capable of understanding BTC and knowing what risk level.they are ok.with.. Do.your research and get comfortable with small amounts.. Do not share any personal information with anyone re: your BTC or.stotage methods..

### Mat.I — 2025-07-05T11:21:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-btc.31246/post-355787>

I posted these general notes last year that may help (always do your own research) - [http://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/...ice-or-friendship-come-here.25436/post-338276](http://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-btc-thread-when-you-need-some-answers-love-advice-or-friendship-come-here.25436/post-338276)

### Daniel Renaud — 2025-07-05T22:54:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-btc.31246/post-355825>

Everything depends on your jurisdiction. If I was in a commonwealth country I would learn how to buy from a non KYC exchange. If you don't mind giving up your personal information then there are some good options. I only use Bitcoin only exchanges. If you're going to make a big buy you might want to get your cold storage setup dialed in first. In the US for KYC Bitcoin Unchained is a good partner. They helped me a lot when I rolled my IRA over into Bitcoin. As Jack said, for advice you get what you pay for.

### Daniel Renaud — 2025-07-05T22:55:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-btc.31246/post-355826>

Full disclosure - I booked one hour session with Jack before I made my move into Bitcoin 4 years ago.

### JenniferM — 2025-07-07T00:17:44+0300

### fouadfo1 — 2025-07-05T02:52:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tanning-easily-and-losing-the-tan-quickly-implications.31247/post-355772>

Trying to understand myself a bit better. I can tan really easily, never burn (middle eastern descent), but I lose my tan very quickly.

For example I would sun max on day 1, gets lots of sun, darken to what would make me look like a dark Hispanic (picture Dev Patel after a few hours in the sun), then if I stay out of the sun for 24 hours, I would literally lighten 2-3 shades. In the winter time here in the east coast USA, I will become as light as Zayn Malik (or even lighter) I have to constantly expose myself to high noon sun to maintain a tan. I’m wondering, is this my body showing extreme need for melanin so it’s being “sucked” in to do work? Any other insights?

It replaced melatonin, as the king of health and disease over 600 million years ago. Melanin enhances the process of endogenous light transformation by stabilizing UPE frequencies, and it was this process that drove the evolution of the leptin melanocortin pathways over 600 million years to replace the more rudimentary light pathways of the aromatic amino acids used to build melatonin signaling, the microbiome light show, and serotonin signaling pathways in the gut.

### Ryan M — 2025-07-07T15:17:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breeched-delivery.31257/post-355911>

My wife is 37 weeks and our daughter is breeched. She has been doing all of the "flipping baby" methods we have found online to no avail. She has tried chiro, acupuncture and an ECV two days ago.

We are in Connecticut and Yale New Haven as a whole, does not like to do breeched deliveries. They will do it if you refuse a C section, but they won't let you plan induction, so you could end up with an OB with little experience delivering breeched babies.

There is one OB at UCONN that apparently teaches methods in delivering breeched and does them frequently. We are trying to get an appointment there.

Does anyone, specially an OB, have advice on the safety of delivering breeched? My wife really wants to avoid a C section, but wants to prioritize safety. It's difficult to find statistics in the safety of breeched deliveries because of the incentives to push C sections.

### JenniferM — 2025-07-07T17:09:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breeched-delivery.31257/post-355919>

I was in the acupuncture's office day and a pregnant lady came in so I asked her about how acupuncture will help with delivery. She told me she was there because the baby was breech and heard he had skills. Come to find out the acupuncturist righted the baby.
The acupuncturist is a 4th generation acupuncturist and other medical techniques.
Just a thought for you..

### Jack Kruse — 2025-07-09T01:20:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breeched-delivery.31257/post-356037>

You've just said a lot about your wife in you journal. Now this explains the breech issue.
My thesis posits that the stroma in tissues is piezo and flexoelectric (collagen- and proteoglycan-rich connective tissue) and fully supports structural integrity and photo- bioelectric signaling in tissues like the dental pulp and cornea. You can read about that in the next blog on the eye and you can read about it here on the forum about enamel pitting anothe rmember asked about.

The Moderna jab: Is a big time transgenerational weaknesses in stromal integrity which likely has exacerbate these effects, compromising tissue development and the kids non movement.

Applying my thesis to breech delivery, we can consider how photobioelectric disruptions affect the uterine stroma, placental stroma, or fetal connective tissues, potentially altering fetal positioning.

Breech Delivery and Stromal Photobioelectric Effects

Breech delivery occurs in about 3–5% of term pregnancies and is influenced by maternal, fetal, and environmental factors. My thesis would explain this phenomenon through the following mechanisms:

1\. Uterine Stromal Weakness and Biomechanical Failure
Uterine Stroma Role: The uterus contains a stromal layer (endometrium and myometrium connective tissue) that supports implantation, placentation, and uterine expansion during pregnancy. This stroma, rich in collagen and proteoglycans, maintains biomechanical integrity and facilitates myometrial contractility. We no know spike protein loves the female GU tract. this is why so many jabortions and spontaneous abortions occur.

Photobioelectric Disruption via Other causes:

Lack of melanin = lack of energy redox of tissues.
High-Carb Diet: Excessive carbohydrate intake induces mitochondrial stress and inflammation, reducing ATP production in stromal cells. This weakens uterine collagen matrices, impairing the uterus’s ability to guide fetal positioning through biomechanical forces.

Blue Light (380–550 nm): Blue light exposure increases ROS, disrupting mitochondrial function in uterine stromal cells. This would impair cellular signaling and stromal remodeling, reducing the uterus’s capacity to orient the fetus head-down. It also destroys melanin and leptin signaling.

nnEMF (1G–5G): nnEMF disrupts mitochondrial electron transport and bioelectric potentials, potentially altering the stromal water structure and collagen alignment. This may weaken the uterine stroma, reducing its ability to generate directional forces for fetal rotation.

LNPs of the jabs = demolish charge carrying ability of the stroma.

Impact on Breech: A weakened uterine stroma may fail to provide the biomechanical cues (e.g., tension, contractility) needed to guide the fetus into a cephalic (head-down) position. This mirrors stromal thinning in keratoconus or enamel pitting, where structural failure leads to functional deficits.

A compromised placental stroma should be expected to alter amniotic fluid volume or placental attachment (e.g., placenta previa), both associated with breech presentation. This could limit fetal mobility, preventing spontaneous rotation to a head-down position.

Mom took jab before conception so fetus is GMO.

Fetal Stromal Weakness and Musculoskeletal Constraints = collateral jab effects

Fetal Connective Tissue Role: The fetal stroma, derived from neural crest and mesodermal cells, forms connective tissues like ligaments, tendons, and muscles. These tissues influence fetal posture and movement within the uterus.

Transgenerational Weakness: As in many other diseases in my thesis, transgenerational stromal weaknesses (e.g., from maternal exposure to nnEMF, jabs, light, or poor nutrition) should be expected to impair fetal collagen synthesis or proteoglycan stability, leading to weaker musculoskeletal structures.

Bioelectric Signaling and Fetal Orientation

Photo-Bioelectric Role: Bioelectric potentials (Becker’s current) guide cellular differentiation and tissue organization during embryogenesis. In the context of pregnancy, these potentials will influence uterine and fetal tissue polarity, directing fetal orientation.

Disruption by nnEMF: nnEMF can alter bioelectric gradients (melanin) in the uterine or fetal stroma, disrupting polarity cues that guide fetal positioning. This could confuse the fetus’s orientation, favoring a breech presentation.

Red Light Potential: Red light photobiomodulation (630–850 nm) enhances mitochondrial ATP and bioelectric signaling, potentially supporting stromal health and fetal movement. However, transgenerational stromal weaknesses may limit its efficacy.

So in the 37th week you should make you wife put her gut in the sun constantly to change the kids position naturally. It is still not late to use Nature's methods.

Uterine Innervation: The uterus is innervated by the autonomic nervous system (sympathetic and parasympathetic fibers via the pelvic plexus), established later in development (around 10–12 weeks). Disruptions in bioelectric signaling from nnEMF could impair autonomic regulation of uterine contractility, reducing spontaneous contractions that help reposition the fetus.

Impact on Breech: Altered innervation or bioelectric signaling in the uterus or fetal craniofacial structures may disrupt the coordinated movements needed for cephalic presentation, especially in late pregnancy (28–36 weeks). Risk for craniosynostosis should be high in OB and pediatrician.

Your wife's lack of melanin and the jab means she is like a woman with a genetic stromal disease. She needs to be treated as such. Studies on connective tissue disorders (e.g., Ehlers-Danlos syndrome) show increased rates of breech presentation due to weakened pelvic or uterine tissues, analogous to stromal thinning in my model.

It is your kid too and you have to stop negioticiating with a dumb ass, even if she is your spouse. Your kids life is on the life. Make sure the last 3 weeks she is in the sun. That is your best option. Make her do it grounded for best results.

# Thread: Ryan's path
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ryans-path.31263/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Ryan M — 2025-07-08T05:13:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ryans-path.31263/post-355978>

I started reading blogs by the doctor (Dr. Kruse) in 2014, after many of my fellow Functional Patterns practitioners were recommending his work. I implemented it here and there over the next couple years, never very seriously. I used the airplane protocol, I bought a cheap pair of red glasses that I wore at night doing shift work, I would take occasional cold baths, that was about it.

In 2019, I changed jobs so I would no longer have shift work, but was now in an office most of the time. About one year after starting this job, I started feeling terrible intermittently. I would feel good for about one week and then crappy for about one week. I could barely find a correlation. At the time, I was following the advice of Ben Greenfield and other influencers. I was eating one meal a day, and often exercising once or twice daily for several years. (I would love to know specially what I destroyed doing this, because as you will read, I suffered greatly )

I tried everything Ben sells and suggested and got worse and worse.

In 2020, my Dad passed unexpectedly.

In October 2020, I started experiencing bouts of anxiety. I went back to 3 meals per day at this time, and still did every other day fasts of 16-20 hours often. By December 2020, this had morphed into a frequent throat tightness and chest issues by February 2021. These included heart palpitations, chest pain in different areas, arm numbness, headaches at times, anxiety continued...

I went to a Yale Hospital Cardiologist who performed all the tests: multiple Ekgs, 24 hour holter monitor, multiple stress ekgs, multiple echos, stress echo, etc. I insisted on a calcium scan and it was 0. The cardiologist was eventually frustrated that he could not prescribe a beta blocker nor BP lowering medication and literally left the room in huff saying he has no idea what was wrong.

Exercise never a problem, the pain occurred at rest.

History: Right bundle branch block, only observed on EKG (since 16), excellent exercise capacity on stress tests, however the first echo, in early 2021 showed 50% EF. This had me worried for a long time.

A sought a naturopathic cardiologist (Decker Weiss).via telehealth who started me on taurine, Restorative functions cardiac calm (blend if herbs) and Magnesium. He said he may have to start on these forever. They seemed to help.

Second echo was up to 63% EF.

My primary naturopath since 2017 was getting more and more into the teachings of Jack Kruse at this time. But, based on my symptoms, suggested I test for Lyme. That came back positive.
I tried several different things with my naturopath including methylene Blue, B1, and others. My symptoms persisted.

Blood work consistently showed low white blood cell count since about the time of symptoms, along with low ceruloplasmin, copper and zinc all while eating lots of organ meats. One blood test in 2017 showed normal range wbc count, which was my only baseline.

04/2022
Vibrant labs Lyme and co-infections. A couple positives.

Had a health coach appointment with Dr. Wolfson's clinic in Arizona which came with the lab test. They suggested low does 150mcg, 3 times daily of Lizyme (lithium) for heart issues. Did not help.

I would still go for 5-6 days feeling great followed by a couple days of feeling very low energy, brain fog, achy, etc.

I ended up seeing a Lyme literate doctor who put me on herbs, at $800 per month. I did that for about 8 months until I was starting to feel better.

During the same time period, I was listening to my naturopath who was encouraging more of the light controls, grounding and as much sun as possible here in Connecticut. I'm not sure if it was the herbs or the protocols that were helping me improve, but I suspect the latter.

These are not nearly all the doctors I saw or the protocols I tried that failed. I wasted a shit load of money and struggled for years with my family and two young kids. It's emotional to think about, but I'm pretty good now.

i cold plunge almost daily year round. I have decreased exercise to 3-4/times per week in the summer and 1-2 times per week in the winter. I only exercise in my garage or outside. Red lights only after dark. My whole family is on board. My kids do minimal screens only with red lights on and blue blockers. We all get as dark as we can in the summer (not my wife).

I have not really been able to fast anymore, I feel like I broke something doing it excessively. My neuropath explained how the melanin in my heart was likely degraded so I've learned to balance exercise. I do my best to eat seasonally (my having isn't really with that). I'm extreme with these teachings. I know I need more sun because I know how I feel when I get it. I know winters will always be a struggle for me up here.

I went to ES in March and am buying a condo there for the future. My wife is not currently on board, but I'll try to rent it until she is, or until I need it.

I'm Irish/Italian and my wife is light skin, blue eyes, blonde hair, Scandinavian. She is not a fan of the sun.

I would love someone to shed light on what they believe I damaged with my excessive exercise and fasting. My only symptom that continues is still occasional chest pain which I can not determine if it is heart burn or not (still). Zinc carnosine taken with meals seems to help it. I'll try to update with anything I missed...

My wife took one Moderna. My kids and I took none. The kids are otherwise fully vaxxed. My wife is none months pregnant with our third and I've decided no vaccines is must likely and I'll figure out schooling later.

My wife tolerates the sun and is tanner than she has ever been in her shoulders and chest (while wearing makeup and sunglasses in the sun, which is out of my control right now). Vanity still controls her. Most of her is not tan.

# Thread: Seeking advice for toddler enamel hypoplasia (brown spots)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seeking-advice-for-toddler-enamel-hypoplasia-brown-spots.31268/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jespada — 2025-07-08T21:07:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seeking-advice-for-toddler-enamel-hypoplasia-brown-spots.31268/post-356022>

My son is 1.6 years old and has been diagnosed with **enamel hypoplasia** by his dentist (he has brown spots on his two upper front teeth). He is a normal toddler, under no medication, and we don’t use fluoride (at least not knowingly).

The dentist has advised **topical application of fluoride toothpaste** on those teeth.

However, in the June 2025 Q&A, Dr. Jack Kruse mentioned:

-   **Enamel production:** Enamel is made by **ameloblasts**, which die once the tooth erupts, so enamel cannot regenerate.
-   The structural integrity of enamel depends on its **electromagnetic bonding to dentin**, maintained by nerves and blood supply.
-   **Enamel absorbs deep UV light**, and UV light used by dentists (e.g. to harden bonding materials) is a useful “biohack” to strengthen teeth.
-   **100 nm UV light** (deep UV) is considered beneficial for enamel.

We already follow many of Jack’s recommendations as a family (sunrise exposure, grounding, low EMF environment, dark nights, etc.).

### **My questions:**​

1.  Has anyone here dealt with enamel hypoplasia in toddlers naturally?
2.  Would **UV light exposure** (e.g. dentist curing light) be beneficial in this context?
3.  Are there **non-fluoride approaches** to improve enamel integrity in hypoplasia cases?
4.  Any additional advice to support **oral development and remineralization** at this stage?

Thank you so much for your insights and experiences

### Jack Kruse — 2025-07-09T00:25:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seeking-advice-for-toddler-enamel-hypoplasia-brown-spots.31268/post-356027>

The stroma, a connective tissue framework within the jaw and tooth pulp, plays a critical role in toothdevelopment and enamel integrity. It consists of collagen fibrils, proteoglycans, and mesenchymal cells, includingdental pulp stem cells (hDPSCs), which support the formation and maintenance of enamel, dentin, and pulp.

Transgenerational weaknesses in the tooth bud’s stroma, potentially inherited through epigenetic orenvironmental influences, can predispose teeth to structural vulnerabilities, leading to enamel pits and fissures. Below, I’ll clarify how stromal weaknesses contribute to photobioelectric failure, integrating a decentralized framework focusing on human tooth development.

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

### Jack Kruse — 2025-07-09T00:38:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seeking-advice-for-toddler-enamel-hypoplasia-brown-spots.31268/post-356028>

Central Node: Tooth (Enamel, Dentin, Pulp)
Subcomponents: Odontoblasts, Dentinal Tubules, Dental Pulp Stem Cells (hDPSCs)
Left Branch of slide: Protective Mechanisms

Parotid/Submandibular Glands:
Outputs: Saliva (minerals, buffering), Parotid Hormone (Leonora’s flow)

Mitochondrial ATP drives secretion.

**Stromal Role in Tooth Development**

During odontogenesis, the tooth bud forms from interactions between ectodermal epithelial cells and mesenchymal stromal cells derived from the neural crest. The stroma provides a scaffold for odontoblasts, which secrete dentin, and supports ameloblasts, which form enamel. The dental papilla, a stromal component, gives rise to the pulp and contains hDPSCs, which maintain a tooth's regenerative potential. The stromal layer’s integrity, stabilized by collagen and proteoglycans, is critical for:

1.  Mechanical Support: The stroma anchors odontoblasts and maintains dentinal tubule structure, facilitating dentinal fluid flow, which delivers calcium, phosphate, and protective substances to enamel.
2.  Bioelectric Signaling: The stroma, connected to the CNS via the trigeminal nerve, supports bioelectric potentials (Becker’s current) that regulate cellular activity and mineralization.
3.  Nutrient Supply: Stromal vascularization ensures nutrient delivery to developing and mature teeth, supporting mitochondrial ATP production in odontoblasts and salivary glands.

Transgenerational weaknesses, from the Mother or father germ line or potentially from maternal nutrition, stress, or environmental exposures (e.g., nnEMF, light stress, high-carb diets), may impair stromal collagen density, proteoglycan stability, or hDPSC functionality. This mirrors stromal thinning in keratoconus, where weakened collagen matrices lead to corneal fragility.

In teeth, a compromised stroma results in brittle enamel prone to pits and fissures, as the biomechanical foundation fails to support ameloblast function or resist trauma.

Photobioelectric Failure in the Stroma

Photobioelectric failure refers to disruptions in the bioelectric and biochemical processes driven by light and electromagnetic influences, exacerbated by stromal weaknesses. Here’s how it unfolds:

1.  Protective Mechanisms Disrupted:
2.  Dentinal Flow Impairment: Dentinal fluid, driven by bioelectric potentials and mitochondrial ATP, transports minerals through dentinal tubules to remineralize enamel. A weakened stroma, due to fragmented collagen or reduced proteoglycan content, disrupts tubule architecture, stagnating flow and starving enamel of calcium and phosphate.
3.  Salivary Gland Dysfunction: The parotid and submandibular glands, regulated by mitochondrial ATP and parotid hormone (Leonora’s flow), secrete saliva rich in minerals and buffering agents. Stromal weaknesses may impair neural signaling via the trigeminal system, reducing gland output. High-carb diets suppress parotid hormone through the hypothalamic-pituitary axis, further diminishing salivary flow.

Dentinal Flow (Leonora):

-   Fluid movement through tubules
-   Delivers calcium, phosphate, protective substances
-   Bioelectric potential (Becker’s current)
-   Red Light (PBM, 630–850 nm):
-   Enhances mitochondrial ATP, hDPSC differentiation
-   Increases salivary flow, dentinal flow
-   Supports pulp regeneration

-   Right Branch: Disruptive Factors
-   High-Carb Diet:
-   Suppresses parotid hormone via hypothalamic-pituitary axis
-   Induces mitochondrial stress, inflammation
    -   Blue Light (380–550 nm):
        -   Increases ROS, lowers melatonin/DHA/melanopsin disrupts mitochondrial function
        -   Impairs salivary and dentinal flow
        -   Reduces dopamine, melatonin, photoreceptor repair and circadian signaling
    -   nnEMF (1G–5G):
        -   Disrupts mitochondrial electron transport, water structure, increases glucose and insulin
        -   Impairs bioelectric potentials, creating dentinal flow
-   Bottom Node: Outcomes
    -   Healthy Tooth: Maintained enamel/dentin, vital pulp, regenerated dentin (via hDPSCs)
    -   Decay/Abscess: Demineralization, stagnant flow, pulp necrosis
-   Arrows:
    -   Red light → Glands, Odontoblasts, hDPSCs (enhances function)
    -   Blue light/nnEMF → Glands, Odontoblasts (impairs function)
    -   High-carb diet → Parotid suppression → Reduced flow
    -   Dentinal flow <--> Becker’s current (bioelectric synergy)

### Jack Kruse — 2025-07-09T00:39:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seeking-advice-for-toddler-enamel-hypoplasia-brown-spots.31268/post-356029>

Red light (600–1100 nm) enhances mitochondrial ATP production, boosting odontoblast activity, hDPSC differentiation, and salivary flow. It counteracts stromal weaknesses by supporting collagen synthesis and dentinal flow, promoting enamel remineralization. However, a transgenerationally compromised stroma may limit PBM efficacy due to reduced cellular responsiveness. The sun is how you improve it because the stem cells in the kid are fired do the parents habits and choices that influenced the stem cells that the child came from.

Healthy Tooth: A robust stroma supports enamel and dentin integrity, vital pulp, and hDPSC-driven regeneration, maintaining resistance to trauma.

Decay and Pits/perio disease: A weakened stroma leads to stagnant dentinal flow, demineralization, and enamel pitting, increasing susceptibility to biomechanical failure and caries. Severe cases result in pulp necrosis or abscess or destruction of Sharpey's fibers.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-07-09T00:57:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seeking-advice-for-toddler-enamel-hypoplasia-brown-spots.31268/post-356031>

1.6 years old with this problem = parents redox is bad.

Tooth Bud Development Timeline

Tooth bud development in humans begins during the 6th to 8th week of embryogenesis (late first trimester). Specifically:

6th week: The oral epithelium thickens to form the dental lamina, an ectodermal band along the future dental arches.

7th–8th week: The dental lamina invaginates into the underlying mesenchyme, forming tooth buds for the primary (deciduous) teeth. These buds mark the initiation of odontogenesis, progressing through the bud, cap, and bell stages.

Embryological Origin of Tooth Bud Stroma

The stroma of the tooth bud, which forms the dental papilla and eventually the dental pulp and supportive connective tissue, is derived from cranial neural crest cells (ectomesenchyme). Here’s the breakdown:

Neural Crest Origin: The stroma arises from ectomesenchymal cells, a subset of neural crest cells that migrate from the neural folds to the first and second branchial arches during the 3rd–4th week of embryogenesis. These cells are unique in combining ectodermal (neural crest) and mesenchymal properties.

Composition: The tooth bud stroma consists of:
Mesenchymal cells: These differentiate into odontoblasts (which form dentin), dental pulp stem cells (hDPSCs), and other pulp cells.
Collagen fibrils: Primarily type I and III collagen, providing structural support.
Proteoglycans and extracellular matrix (ECM): These stabilize the stromal architecture and regulate cell signaling.
Interaction with Epithelium: The stroma (ectomesenchyme) interacts with the ectodermal enamel organ, driving reciprocal signaling that shapes tooth morphogenesis. The dental papilla, a stromal condensation, forms the pulp and dentin, while the dental follicle (another stromal derivative) gives rise to the periodontal ligament, cementum, and alveolar bone.

The Time line of the kids diagnosis = really a embryological parent issue.

Innervation of the First and Second Branchial Arches During Embryogenesis

The first and second branchial arches (also called pharyngeal arches) develop during the 3rd to 4th week of embryogenesis and are innervated by specific cranial nerves. The innervation is established as the arches form, driven by the migration of neural crest cells and the development of cranial nerve ganglia. Neural crest cells are like the Mother board of redox for these branchial cleft so pitting enamel makes me worry about many other things derived in these two clefts.

First Branchial Arch (Mandibular Arch)

Innervation: Trigeminal nerve (Cranial Nerve V), specifically the mandibular branch (V3).

The trigeminal nerve provides sensory innervation to the derivatives of the first arch, including the mandible, lower jaw muscles, and oral mucosa, as well as motor innervation to the muscles of mastication (e.g., masseter, temporalis, pterygoids), mylohyoid, anterior digastric, tensor tympani, and tensor veli palatini.

Embryological Origin: The trigeminal nerve arises from the trigeminal ganglion, which forms from neural crest cells and neural tube-derived neurons in the hindbrain (rhombomeres 2 and 3).

Timing of Innervation: how soon the germ line redox problem caused this effect.

3rd week: Neural crest cells begin migrating into the first branchial arch, and the trigeminal ganglion starts forming.
4th week: The trigeminal nerve extends axons into the first arch, establishing sensory and motor innervation as the arch differentiates into mesenchymal stroma and musculature. By the end of the 4th week, the mandibular branch (V3) is actively innervating the arch, coinciding with the formation of the mandibular prominence and Meckel’s cartilage.

Second Branchial Arch (Hyoid Arch)

Innervation: Facial nerve (Cranial Nerve VII).

The facial nerve provides sensory innervation to the taste buds of the anterior tongue (via the chorda tympani, a branch of CN VII) and motor innervation to the muscles of facial expression (e.g., orbicularis oris, buccinator), stapedius, stylohyoid, and posterior digastric.

Embryological Origin: The facial nerve originates from the facial ganglion (geniculate ganglion) and motor neurons in the hindbrain (rhombomere 4).

Neural crest cells also contribute to the sensory ganglia in the head and neck.

Timing of Innervation:

3rd week: Neural crest cells populate the second arch, and the facial nerve begins to form as its ganglion develops.

4th week: The facial nerve extends axons into the second arch, establishing innervation as the arch forms the hyoid bone and associated musculature. By the end of the 4th week, CN VII innervation is established, supporting the differentiation of second arch structures like the hyoid apparatus and facial muscles.

Tooth Bud Context

The first and second branchial arches contribute to tooth development, with the first arch forming the mandible, maxilla, and tooth bud stroma (ectomesenchyme), and the second arch contributing to supporting structures like the hyoid bone which the tongue connects to. The trigeminal nerve (V3) is particularly critical for the sensory innervation of the tooth buds, as it connects the developing dental pulp and stroma to the central nervous system (CNS) via the trigeminal ganglion. This innervation begins in the 4th week as the dental lamina starts to form (around 6 weeks), with nerve fibers extending into the tooth buds during the bud stage (7th–8th weeks) to support sensory and photo- bioelectric signaling.

### S Hudson — 2025-09-01T00:31:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seeking-advice-for-toddler-enamel-hypoplasia-brown-spots.31268/post-358988>

My little boy has browny spots on his 2 front teeth half way up that i cant get off so will the light environment & grounding & correct water fix it? Or just prevent it happening for future adult teeth? He was about 18 months old when i noticed them and he's nearly 3 now.

No. once enamel is formed it can't be repaired save by using dental materials. Enamel forms by an envelope that builds up like piling up bricks as the tooth is erupting. when the tooth erupts, that envelope is no longer present. For milk teeth, as long as its not caries I (as a dentist) never found the need to do anything.

# Thread: HOW I HEALED KERATOCONUS IN TWO SAVAGES
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-i-healed-keratoconus-in-two-savages.31277/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2025-07-10T22:30:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-i-healed-keratoconus-in-two-savages.31277/post-356127>

A new blog post is available today on keratoconus. I promise that what you'll read here is not available anywhere else on this disease, which ophthalmologists still struggle to understand and solve. I used light to do it.

In this blog, you'll see how I cured two of my patients of this disease.

[https://www.patreon.com/posts/decentralized-61-124956764](https://www.patreon.com/posts/decentralized-61-124956764)

How did I come up with new ideas to solve Keratoconus? It was through my studies on narcolepsy in 2005. The discovery that an orexin receptor mutation causes the sleep disorder canine narcolepsy in Doberman Pinschers indicated a significant role for this system in sleep regulation. Narcolepsy is a REM sleep disorder. Additionally, genetic knockout mice lacking the orexin gene were also reported to exhibit narcolepsy.

In humans, narcolepsy is associated with a specific variant of the human leukocyte antigen (HLA) complex.

A lack of either red light or UV light is believed to be a contributing factor to the onset of narcolepsy. Does the same principle apply to eye diseases and collagen vascular diseases?

Today, all of them begin with poor light signaling in the prism of the retina. Light changes the charge density of the retina. The proof is found in genome-wide analysis studies that show that, in addition to the HLA variant, people with narcolepsy also exhibit a specific genetic mutation in the T-cell receptor alpha locus. I spoke about the HLA complex in the brain-gut series when I explained how the chimp brain evolved into the homo sapien brain using the HLA complex. Orexins helped fuel the transition. They are active in this eye disease, too.

### JenniferM — 2025-07-10T23:00:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-i-healed-keratoconus-in-two-savages.31277/post-356131>

My brother dated a lady with narcolepsy. He never mentioned her disability so when I met her and she just fell out asleep I thought she was on drugs. Her car looks like a junkyard car and she keeps on driving. So dangerous.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-07-10T23:27:09+0300

### Jack Kruse — 2025-07-11T20:49:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-got-sleep-apnea.31289/post-356188>

Check out this paper published in June of 2025. It fully supports my decentralized photo-bioelectric thesis in Decentralized Medicine #46 blog post. The paper reveals that peroxisomes act as "sinks" for mitochondrial reactive oxygen species (ROS) via membrane contacts, a process mediated by proteins like ACBD5 and PTPIP51, which increases during oxidative stress to maintain mitochondrial health.

This supports my idea that the GOE (~2.4–2.1 billion years ago), when rising oxygen from cyanobacteria shifted redox dynamics, drove the evolution of such adaptive mechanisms to manage oxygen’s "Holocaust" effects. The ROS transfer likely emerged as a response to intensified mitochondrial ROS production, to create UPEs to alter physiology paralleling my thesis that nature embedded resilience by enhancing function via hypoxia (breath holds) and UV-A light (via neuropsin and melanin), into cellular design. [https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.adn2804](https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.adn2804)

### Jack Kruse — 2025-07-11T20:59:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-got-sleep-apnea.31289/post-356189>

This finding strengthens my UPE hypothesis, as the redox signaling at these contacts could involve biophoton emissions during electron transitions, a process your thesis links to light-mediated communication. The GOE’s ozone layer formation, reducing UV-B/C while preserving UV-A, co-evolved with this redox balance, suggesting light-oxygen synergy need to be explore further by centralized science who remain clueless.

Additionally, the paper’s focus on oxidative stress aligns with my claims that intermittent hypoxia shifts redox states, were what optimized peroxisomal ROS uptake and mitochondrial function. This is a mechanism testable in my resilience model. While the paper lacks direct GOE evidence and focuses on mammals, it provides a modern cellular lens to infer ancient adaptations by first principle thinking, reinforcing my narrative that breath control and UV exposure unlock GOE-honed survival strategies for regeneration. I have integrated this as a GOE-evolved safeguard linked to the melanin reniovation of mammals because it amplifies light and breath, to deepen my thesis’s mechanistic and therapeutic implications.

[https://www.patreon.com/posts/melanin-rx-for-80782201](https://www.patreon.com/posts/melanin-rx-for-80782201)

# Thread: Incidental Pineal Cyst
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/incidental-pineal-cyst.31296/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jill Dee — 2025-07-13T00:03:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/incidental-pineal-cyst.31296/post-356220>

Hello,
New to the Journal… but have been becoming Savage myself for a little while… Thx for sharing your work and all that demands of you in your life. ;-)
I did not find “Pineal Cyst” in search box or previous discussions.

Q.
Incidental Pineal Cyst.
60yr old healthy female
Not calcification, Zero symptoms. 1.5cm found MRI w/o contrast one yr ago 7/2024 in a series of ‘prevention” medical testing done w “Fountain Life” out of FL. All testing was very/good otherwise.

UV morning sunrise added, and full savage-to-be soon at 13N.
How best to monitor? (Rejected the 1yr follow up recommendation MRI w contrast)
Uncle Jack Input greatly appreciated.
Thank You

### Jack Kruse — 2025-07-13T00:45:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/incidental-pineal-cyst.31296/post-356224>

This history suggests a benign condition. Your recent addition of UV morning sunrise exposure and plans for a full-spectrum light regimen at 13°N latitude align with my thesis’s focus on natural light as a restorative force.

Understanding the Pineal Cyst Context

Pineal cysts are common, with prevalence ranging from 1.5% to 23% in healthy adults on high-resolution MRI, often discovered incidentally. For a 1.5 cm cyst without symptoms, calcification, or atypical features (e.g., solid components, nodularity), research indicates a benign course, with stability observed in most cases over long-term follow-up (e.g., 6 months to 14 years). The lack of hydrocephalus, Parinaud syndrome, or other neurological signs further supports this. The pineal gland’s role in melatonin production and its sensitivity to light, as per my thesis, suggest that its function could be influenced by environmental light exposure, though the cyst itself is unlikely to alter this unless it grows significantly.

### Jill Dee — 2025-07-13T02:43:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/incidental-pineal-cyst.31296/post-356229>

Boom!
Thanks so much,
\-keep Rockin’ it…

# Thread: Consciousness Blogs Questions
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consciousness-blogs-questions.31302/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Nick-Rolser — 2025-07-14T15:35:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consciousness-blogs-questions.31302/post-356275>

As a psychology student, I’ve been very interested reading through the latest blogs on consciousness. I was already familiar with Penose and Hameroff’s papers, and after reading Becker’s book it struck me as obvious that the crystalline lattice structure of microtubules probably enabled them to act like semiconductors for the DC current. However, reading through the blogs has helped paint a clearer picture to see how mtDNA, DDW, melanin, light, and oxygen all work in tandem to produce consciousness. Nevertheless, I do have a few questions:

1\. Are Becker’s DC currents and Popp/Van Wijk’s UPE’s the same thing (I.e, “photonic current”) , or is it better to think of them as a coupled system?
2\. Jack says that the high information content of the narrow UPE spectra collapses the wave function via “measurement-like events”. Is this a different process than Penrose’s objective reduction? Is it more akin to a subjective collapse?
3\. Hameroff speculates that the microtubule associated proteins (MAPs) “tune” themselves. Am I correct in assuming that the mitochondrial electric fields is likely to be orchestrating that?

### Jack Kruse — 2025-07-14T20:09:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consciousness-blogs-questions.31302/post-356308>

1\. No Tissues have their own optical density which determines their quantum abilities.
2\. yes. Penrose does not understand how scale change light's abilities and that is on display with his theory
3\. Nothing tunes itself. Light, solitons, and acoustic phonons are needed.

### Nick-Rolser — 2025-09-17T21:31:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consciousness-blogs-questions.31302/post-359653>

I have another question somewhat related to consciousness that I figured I'd add to this thread:

I was thinking about the common occurrence of drug-induced psychosis from psychedelics such as LSD or psilocybn as a result of poor redox potential and destruction of neuromelanin in the substantia nigra/VTA from nnEMF paired with increased UPE emission. The way I'm picturing it intuitively is as though there isn't enough of a "screen" (melanin) to capture the light from the projector (UPEs) and that this elevates dopamine levels in the nigrostriatal pathway.

Second, and perhaps more importantly: I've been trying to fact check the idea that melanin can degrade into dopamine and finding it incredibly difficult. If someone could explain the mechanism by which this happens or point me to a blog that does I'd be very grateful. Is it some lesser-known biophysical mechanism involved? Because the mainstream biochemistry paradigm seems to suggest no such physiological pathway exists.

### Mightygumshoe — 2025-07-16T19:43:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcription-of-your-monthly-powwows.31323/post-356430>

Hi, Would you ever consider using software to transcribe your monthly powwows for premium members, so a person with a premium membership could use a search tool and find what they want to focus on from a particular event? And read it vs. hitting forward searching for a question/topic of interest? Just a suggestion. God bless.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcription-of-your-monthly-powwows.31323/post-356435>

I only asked about transcription because those webinars are for premium members only. I will look for translator software. Thank you so much.

### Greg Cioth — 2025-07-20T04:30:26+0300

Use the TRANSCRIBE icon. I watched the last one in video mode, vs download of audio, and I clicked the "transcribe box" that is the little icon to the right of the "CC" closed caption box and to the left of the Settings link. (bottom righthand corner) This will allow you to search the entire PowWow with time stamps. Somehow I just recently discovered it. Amazing tool.

# Thread: Madeleine's Optimal Journal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/madeleines-optimal-journal.31325/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Mightygumshoe — 2025-07-16T21:16:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/madeleines-optimal-journal.31325/post-356439>

I joined in early May 2025, after reading Dr. Becker's The Body Electric, and half of Light in Shaping Life by Van Wyck (had to put it down for later, kind of deep). A friend introduced me to Dr. Kruse via some YTube videos and later the Danny Jones 4-hour podcast that to say was enlightening falls short. And I was hooked. My main motivation for joining was twofold: 1) find out more about my own body despite being healthy (I take no meds at all ...on the cusp of 63...except lots of Vit D and occasional calcium tablets). After starting red light and seeing big improvements where I had no expectations...had to join the tribe; and 2) get answers for my daughter's health which has been an issue since birth. I raised her alone, as hubsters died young. She is severely gluten and corn intolerant (autoimmune, not allergy), hypotonic, mold and chemical sensitive, and has hypermobile joints and sensitive nerves. Yet something must have gone right...she is a corporate account manager, an engineer, a brilliant musician (composes on piano), a great artist, and gifted with a phenomenal memory recall -- which I credit to math and music. I thank God for her every day!

She tested negative for Celiac in 9th grade but we had already given up gluten for months and weren't going back to eating it for 3 months prior to a test. So am not surprised. She was falling asleep standing up when she ate gluten-laden products; and gained 15 pounds. Also it caused severe mental fog, joint pain etc, and sometimes rashes. Hard in the teen years. And back then she was still eating corn and had a few symptoms intestinally not shared with me. And a lot of joint inflammation every time she ate corn -- she already suffered from loose joints. Piece by piece we started figuring some things out....took years, 15 of them.

I read that Celiac/gluten intolerance often presents at hormone changes (puberty, menopause). Anyhow, fast forward, kiddo made it through college with no issue (commuted locally) and just avoided wheat/barley/rye etc... and eventually corn, then everything was WONDERFUL for about 3 years; then she landed a corporate job, with a technology company in the automotive space (driverless cars, 5g, 6g and more....) and had to return to work most days and sit in an open floor plan with 5g phones and non-native EMF everywhere. And popcorn and wheat everywhere at lunch.... We do know she is sensitive to EMF! Cellphone pain when placed near ear etc. Leg pain with a laptop near thighs....after return to work, some symptoms in the gut returned, along with other new things. For the record we still do not know if she is a Celiac or not...and if gluten exposure or 5g/EMF or both are the problems at work. But I keep telling her I think we can heal your gut. At college wheat and corn were in the lunchroom and on the chairs and she never got sick. Never.

Nobody in our bloodline either side has ever had Celiac though I became severely gluten intolerant (joint pain and massive headaches, no gut issues) after delivering her.... and I did occasionally suffer headaches after drinking a beer or two... if not light beer, which of course I now believe was a gluten sensitivity. But not Celiac. When I went through menopause, I started having joint pain and severe headaches if I ingested wheat and brain fog too; and after I gave it all up (except corn), everything disappeared. I have permission to share the above.

And this is why I am here.

We are finding some answers for my daughter -- and I thank Dr. Kruse for answering my question about body pain during airplane flights (another issue my daughter has) in the latest July 2025 PowWow -- now I can deep dive on it. As can she. No, I had never heard of collagen vascular disease...NEVER. I did research in the blogs but was using other terms....hypotonia, airplane joint pain, etc. FYI, I am/was an electrical engineer who worked with many sensors ... and semiconductors etc for 15 years before switching careers...and I do know what flexo, piezo, pyroelectrics, even ferroelectrics are...that said, how it all ties into the human body and its environment...on that front I am still a newbie despite reading Becker's book and I plan to keep reading. THANK YOU from the bottom of my heart. PS, yes we live in a crappy place, Michigan, with Whitless as governor. Hard to leave family yet I am seriously considering a move south, where I can have land, a garden, more sun, etc. I worked so hard as a single mom (DIY) to get my house the way I like it...put in my own wood floors, molded my own pavers from concrete etc....and I back up to state land with deer, and woodchucks, and fox, and turkey etc....so it is hard to consider leaving...but health is wealth. I get it.

Since April/May of this year, cannot remember right now, I started going outside for the sunrise, getting sun midday, and continued to walk outside here in the evening (Michigan). The only months I don't walk outside are December and January and the beginning of February. But the AM routine is new. As is no sunglasses. Anyhow, I took Dr. Kruse's advice, and started using red light at night (not 7 days a week...but 4 or 5) and wear blue light blocking reading glasses and regular lens glasses for television at night which I rarely watch -- started going to the library again for paper books and revitalized a very old original Kindle from way back (black and white) so I can read on that device too. With these changes, light weightlifting, and now biking and swimming, and limiting caffeine to two morning cups before 11am...I started to and have continued to sleep through the night -- at least 7 hours. Sometimes more. NOT FOR 35 years had I been able to do this!!!

And there have been other changes. Years ago I had something taken off my nose, not cancer but was told precancerous. Well it was always a crusty dry spot forever afterward and I was told to stay out of the sun. Now that I have ditched the sunglasses and the sunscreen (I am very fair haired/light skin toned), I tanned up nicely over time this year, and the rough patch on my nose is completely gone. Was there for 25 years! Completely gone! Smooth skin! People keep telling me I look younger. For the record, I'm on the cusp of 63. I take no meds, except 10,000 units of Vit D in the winter daily, and 5k every other day in the summer. I eat steak once a week, 4 ounces and chicken once a week, the rest is seafood -- shrimp, salmon etc. and I feel awesome. I feel very blessed to have found this group and thank my friend for directing me here. God bless you all. Dr. Kruse if you read this, know you are such a blessing!!! I appreciate you as do countless others.

### Mightygumshoe — 2025-07-26T22:15:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/madeleines-optimal-journal.31325/post-357012>

Short update -- For last three to four months (some things added later), I have been
1) **Seeing morning sun 7 days a week**; also afternoon and evening. Am outside probably 2 to 4 hours a day, t-shirt or tanktop, bathing suit, etc.

2) **Wearing 99.9% bluelight blocking glasses** -- a) readers for computer work; b) straight lenses for nighttime tv/movie watching. Mainly a book (paper) reader but sometimes tablet use.

3) **Red light box used 4x a week sometimes more**. Shooting for 6x. Usually for 20 minutes at a time up to one hour. On face, back of head, feet, sometimes entire upper body. I rotate even during one session. Have two red bulbs in house for nighttime. Yes, I still use regular lights at times too.

4) **Eating shrimp and salmon more during the week** (2-3x, sometimes 4) and less red meat (2x a week); I still eat organic Amish chicken and the bone broth from it. I use butter / olive oil / avocado oils. The idea of an oyster...can't do it. Texture issue for me. Gag.

5) **Dropped Vit D Supplements for now**. Getting enough sun.

6) **Earthing using a mat grounded outside \[ground change is new\], run into my house via window but in a "dead zone" electrically speaking (tested).

RESULT**
1) **I sleep through the night** -- **every night** now, 8 to 9 hours (despite pet interruptions for a 4am feeding...right back to sleep); was 4 at start, then 6, then seven to 7.5 hours and occasionally 8. I also wake up VERY rested. No shiners under my eyes.

2) **I lost 8 or 9 pounds over the last two months** **without even trying.**

Side note -- we have a backyard pool hidden (don't tell our HOA); anyhow in Michigan temps have been getting colder at night. I heat it with a solar heater I made (copper tubing under glass painted black) and a small solar pond pump. (6x10 foot 33" pool).....here at end of July temps are dipping at night. So pool is cooling. I like my pool 85....but am still getting in and swimming at 79....and treading water. I'm a wuss, but it's a start. Don't laugh. Will read more of the CT blog...at least I'm taking action and doing something.

Peace.

### Mightygumshoe — 2025-08-08T17:25:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/madeleines-optimal-journal.31325/post-357826>

The main reason I joined this forum is because of my daughter's health issues. To be frank, she kind of gave up on talking to anyone with MD after their name. From this site, and Dr. Kruse's response on the last Gold forum, I know she has a collagen vascular disease. She also has gut issues. Despite testing negative for celiac (we had already given up all gluten \[wheat/barley/rye\] but had not yet given up corn, she was told she was probably celiac or gluten intolerant. To test it, she would need to eat wheat again for 3 months then have her intestines biopsied. She said no. So, after that we took matters into our own hands.

After reading posts here, and blogs and doing my own research on vascular issues, a leaky gut, etc... I have encouraged my daughter to follow some of Dr. Kruse's protocols and by golly she actually listened.

For the past 3 weeks she has seen the sun every morning -- no exceptions, barefoot outdoors before work. No glasses. For at least 10 minutes.
1) She gets outside later in the day, in a tank top when she works from home and eats dinner outside here in Michigan when she can, or walks before sunset. No glasses during the walk.
2) She purchased blue light blocking glasses for computer work at her office; and for nighttime tv.
3) No wifi at night.
4) She earths outside every day as much as possible. We'll see what happens in winter -- maybe she can earth on the garage floor a bit. She feels joint pain at first when she earths (outdoor), which later subsides. And said her feet turn a little pink, then it all subsides. I feel nothing.
5) This one is the gamechanger. DEUTERIUM DEPLETED WATER -- 5 and 10ppm bottles (glass - she reacts to PET) from Litewater, diluted to 80ppm ---- her GUT is SO MUCH BETTER. WAY BETTER. She committed to drinking it for at least 60 days and will probably drink it for one whole year. As a gift I gave her three deliveries for her birthday, the rest is on her.

Side notes --

I've been reading Pollack's 4th Phase of Water and am starting to grasp some of the science that is in the blogs a bit better. Next, I will go back and read Light in Shaping Life which I abandoned 5 chapters in because it was just too deep at the time and my mind wandered.

Don't know where to put this article about taurine that I recently read online so I am putting it here. It mentions Deuterium, EZ water, taurine etc. [Taurine prevents Mitochondrial Dysfunction and protects Mitochondria from ROS and Deuterium Toxicity](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00726-024-03440-3) (January, 2025)

Dr. Kruse, I read your Epi-Paleo RX book this past weekend and put a nice review in for you on Amazon. Amazing read. I devoured it early Saturday, then went back and reread it with a highlighter. Thank you. I also ordered two of the Paleo books mentioned/recommended in your book. The Leaky Gut portion of the book was of most interest. I shared the Leaky Gut Rx for autoimmunity prevention (bone broth with coconut oil etc) with family. We don't use Coconut oil...time for a change. My daughter is now looking at getting B12, folate and glutathione tests -- and looking for B12 supplements (she cannot have corn or corn derivatives -- so takes none, but does eat a LOT of green leafy salads, veggies etc -- still doubtful is enough); have heard there are topical B12 but she is looking for sublingual/pill form. That book is a must read.
