### Daniel Renaud — 2025-08-24T20:33:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/turn-off-the-internet.31532/post-358686>

Good conversation. You don’t have to be faster than the lion.

### Mat.I — 2025-08-25T03:16:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/turn-off-the-internet.31532/post-358688>

> [hacklose said:](/goto/post?id=358683)
>
> How do you handle your ip address?
>
> Layers on top of layers on top of layers. I realize that's the only solution when you are working with a centralized base layer.
>
> Who has the ability to peel back the layers of the onion?

Like everything in life, people are ok with D's, E's , F's etc.. Only very few will do what it takes to get an A. People make a lot of noise, do nothing and still complain about things.. BTC is what it is and use it how you feel appropriate with your acceptable level of risk/surveillance thresholds. What is going to happen when any layers are peeled back?? someone with poor OPSEC I know the answer there, however someone with good OPSEC to preserve privacy all they will get is a web of probability. I posted general (not detailed) notes last year in effort to get others thinking about this - [http://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/...ice-or-friendship-come-here.25436/post-338276](http://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-btc-thread-when-you-need-some-answers-love-advice-or-friendship-come-here.25436/post-338276)

Moving away from fiat and stacking BTC is a win no matter on how you look at it. Now if someone is not comfortable with bare BTC, that's where people need to do their research and understand what steps are required to achieve their level of comfort. No different to how you go about your every day life in our tech heavy world.. People are ok with wearing apple air pods and complain about tinnitus and vertigo vs someone who uses everything offline with air tubes.. What I'm getting at here is there are many ways to do things, however, the effect is different based on how you approach the situation. No different to people not understanding the basics and leaving their BTC on exchanges or trusting a 3rd party with their BTC. Education is key here, you get what you put in.. People usually stop at convenience, those people get what they deserve in my opinion.. Then they can learn from the bad choices and make better ones moving forward.

### hacklose — 2025-08-25T03:49:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/turn-off-the-internet.31532/post-358689>

> [Daniel Renaud said:](/goto/post?id=358686)
>
> Good conversation. You don’t have to be faster than the lion.

Can you go into further detail? I'm curious about your perspective.

> [Mat.I said:](/goto/post?id=358688)
>
> Like everything in life, people are ok with D's, E's , F's etc.. Only very few will do what it takes to get an A. People make a lot of noise, do nothing and still complain about things.. BTC is what it is and use it how you feel appropriate with your acceptable level of risk/surveillance thresholds. What is going to happen when any layers are peeled back?? someone with poor OPSEC I know the answer there, however someone with good OPSEC to preserve privacy all they will get is a web of probability. I posted general (not detailed) notes last year in effort to get others thinking about this - [http://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/...ice-or-friendship-come-here.25436/post-338276](http://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-btc-thread-when-you-need-some-answers-love-advice-or-friendship-come-here.25436/post-338276)
>
> Moving away from fiat and stacking BTC is a win no matter on how you look at it. Now if someone is not comfortable with bare BTC, that's where people need to do their research and understand what steps are required to achieve their level of comfort. No different to how you go about your every day life in our tech heavy world.. People are ok with wearing apple air pods and complain about tinnitus and vertigo vs someone who uses everything offline with air tubes.. What I'm getting at here is there are many ways to do things, however, the effect is different based on how you approach the situation. No different to people not understanding the basics and leaving their BTC on exchanges or trusting a 3rd party with their BTC. Education is key here, you get what you put in.. People usually stop at convenience, those people get what they deserve in my opinion.. Then they can learn from the bad choices and make better ones moving forward.

I completely agree that most people will not take additional steps to add layers.

Do you believe that the world order has no backdoors in your physical hardware? Software is downstream.
A decentralized network that is built downstream of a centralized network is still centralized.

Supporting surveillance/cybersecurity technology is most often a win in the financial department and a loss in the freedom department.

### Mat.I — 2025-08-25T04:33:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/turn-off-the-internet.31532/post-358693>

> [hacklose said:](/goto/post?id=358689)
>
> Do you believe that the world order has no backdoors in your physical hardware? Software is downstream.
> A decentralized network that is built downstream of a centralized network is still centralized.

No I do not believe they are completely free of backdoors (the surface area can be minimised though and there are a few decent turnkey solutions).. what does Snowden use?? How to minimise cross contamination? compartments? .. My point is yes the base layer is centralised, however, adding our own layers of entropy to this changes the game. Not perfect, I know, but it forces the approach to move from deterministic attribution (i.e. we know it was you) to probalistic uncertainty (i.e. could be you, could be thousands of others).. It is not about eliminating risk entirely it is about flipping the script to move in your favour. This will raise cost of surveillance & lower confidence of attribution. Case of hard to kill or easy to kill?? which one?

### hacklose — 2025-08-25T04:42:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/turn-off-the-internet.31532/post-358694>

> [Mat.I said:](/goto/post?id=358693)
>
> No I do not believe they are completely free of backdoors (the surface area can be minimised though and there are a few decent turnkey solutions).. what does Snowden use?? How to minimise cross contamination? compartments? .. My point is yes the base layer is centralised, however, adding our own layers of entropy to this changes the game. Not perfect, I know, but it forces the approach to move from deterministic attribution (i.e. we know it was you) to probalistic uncertainty (i.e. could be you, could be thousands of others).. It is not about eliminating risk entirely it is about flipping the script to move in your favour. This will raise cost of surveillance & lower confidence of attribution. Case of hard to kill or easy to kill?? which one?

My point is that with a hardware backdoor, it doesn't matter how many layers you add on top with software.

I don't know what Snowden uses at this point. He probably wouldn't publicly announce what he is using if he is as intelligent as we are led to believe. He may announce something that he isn't actually using as a means of misdirection.

I believe the difference between hard to kill and easy to kill is that the person that thinks they are hard to kill is not actually hard to kill. When you add more software layers you also look like you have more to hide.

### Mat.I — 2025-08-25T04:51:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/turn-off-the-internet.31532/post-358695>

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/turn-off-the-internet.31532/post-358697>

they cannot see all layers end to end if end point is intact. How to keep end point intact? strip out the proprietary BIOS/intel ME firmware? Or get a decent turnkey solution with all this done off the shelf ?

### hacklose — 2025-08-25T21:27:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/turn-off-the-internet.31532/post-358727>

> [Mat.I said:](/goto/post?id=358697)
>
> they cannot see all layers end to end if end point is intact. How to keep end point intact? strip out the proprietary BIOS/intel ME firmware? Or get a decent turnkey solution with all this done off the shelf ?

I would be very suspicious of any solution claiming to strip this capability out. How would you know for sure?

### Mat.I — 2025-08-26T01:00:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/turn-off-the-internet.31532/post-358734>

> [hacklose said:](/goto/post?id=358727)
>
> I would be very suspicious of any solution claiming to strip this capability out. How would you know for sure?

Easy, I'm not building a detailed how to guide here.. You don't trust.. you verify. In short dump firmware and confirm ME modules are neutered. With the other 2 open source firmwares required you just verify the hashes. People also use boot keys to verify each time the boot is initiated quite straightforward. Or this can be done DIY without a turnkey subject ro purchasing the correct machine to start from. If this goes beyond your ability, I would say outsource to a trusted and verified pen tester who you know really well..

### Mat.I — 2025-08-26T01:09:10+0300

Only BUP should be left there in ME for handshake at boot..

# Thread: WHAT IS THE NEW FACTOR X CIRCA 2025?  Meet Google's Alphafox3.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-new-factor-x-circa-2025-meet-googles-alphafox3.31542/>
**Subforum:** Factor X
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2025-08-23T18:43:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-new-factor-x-circa-2025-meet-googles-alphafox3.31542/post-358658>

[https://x.com/DrJackKruse/status/1959277281202856212](https://x.com/DrJackKruse/status/1959277281202856212)

This science issues a stark warning: our fixation on the distant specter of Artificial General Intelligence is a dangerous distraction today. The real, world-altering disruption is already here, moving at lightning speed while we await a singular event. The chess game is over. The new, infinitely more consequential game is Protein Folding. Right now, the DoD is using vaccines and fake food to get atoms inside of us to ruin signal fidelity. Soon, they will use light in your environment to do this with quantum precision. They will use their new AlphaFold 3 algorithm to do it for you. This is an AI model from Google DeepMind that predicts 3D structures of biomolecular complexes, including proteins, DNA, RNA, ligands, and ions. This is a next-gen weapon that will be used to control all those who complied with the jab. Why? All those people now have intercalated DNA missense mutations this program will target. Google already has the ability to alter the light in your environment without your consent. They do this with indoor lighting, tech screens, and your email color temperature. Their terms of service give them this power, and you complied with it. You have no idea what POWER you gave them.

And with AlphaFold 3, we’ve just been checkmated. Think of proteins not as biology, but as the universe's ultimate nano-machinery: copying machines, scissors, shears, engines, lights. All are built from a linear code of amino acids that crumples into a 3D object with a function. For decades, predicting that fold from the code was one of science's grandest challenges. Redox chemistry in cells controls this. Now Google has figured out how to control redox chemistry using light to refold the semiconductors that make up your life.

AlphaFold 3 has effectively solved it. This isn't a future threat; it's a present reality that most do not understand. What does this imply?

1.  Designer proteins that target specific cells with terrifying precision.
2.  The foundation for true nano-robots.
3.  A complete rewriting of medicine, biology, and materials science. The glow-in-the-dark rabbit isn't a curiosity; it’s a harbinger. We are now holding the code to build anything we can imagine at the molecular level.
4.  The power to create, or destroy, is already in our hands. We can now open the door to heaven or hell with the flip of a photonic switch. The question is no longer "Will artificial general intelligence be good or bad?" The question is: Why are you waiting for a future intelligence when a present, alien one is already unfolding in your brain? Your expectation of artificial general intelligence is itself a programmed illusion, a distraction from the profound and immediate revolution happening right now. AGI is a transhumanist wet dream. Their great game has begun, and most of you aren't even watching it unfold.

# Thread: WiFi news......from Rusty
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wifi-news-from-rusty.31547/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 53
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2025-08-25T16:18:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wifi-news-from-rusty.31547/post-358703>

You use WiFi. Almost everyone does. It typically operates on 2 different bands: 2.4 GHz & 5 GHz. 2.4 GHz = Longer range, slower speeds. 5 GHz = Shorter range, faster speeds. When you set up your WiFi, both bands are TURNED ON by default. That's why you see 2 WiFi names, one with the name & the other one with 5G (This 5G has nothing to do with the wireless 5G on your phone). Both WiFi bands are on = MORE RF radiation. You don’t need both. { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Turn one off.

### Mat.I — 2025-08-26T09:49:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wifi-news-from-rusty.31547/post-358750>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=358703)
>
> You use WiFi. Almost everyone does. It typically operates on 2 different bands: 2.4 GHz & 5 GHz. 2.4 GHz = Longer range, slower speeds. 5 GHz = Shorter range, faster speeds. When you set up your WiFi, both bands are TURNED ON by default. That's why you see 2 WiFi names, one with the name & the other one with 5G (This 5G has nothing to do with the wireless 5G on your phone). Both WiFi bands are on = MORE RF radiation. You don’t need both.[View attachment 31856](https://forum.jackkruse.com/attachments/31856/) Turn one off.

I say turn off all wifi for good.. it is not needed. Convenience comes with a cost.. Don't really like this post as it implies it is ok to still cook yourself with 1 network instead of 2.

### Mat.I — 2025-08-26T10:08:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wifi-news-from-rusty.31547/post-358751>

[@Rusty](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/26197/) - how did you confirm that disabling the SSID even in the admin settings actually stops RF transmission in that particular band?? As from what I have found (which I have posted about previously) that on most routers this only disables the broadcast of SSID and still transmits as a "hidden network". Some have an option to turn off the 2.4 or 5 outside the SSID, but found some still transmitted a "hidden network".

### Rusty — 2025-08-26T12:45:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wifi-news-from-rusty.31547/post-358758>

> [Mat.I said:](/goto/post?id=358751)
>
> [@Rusty](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/26197/) - how did you confirm that disabling the SSID even in the admin settings actually stops RF transmission in that particular band?? As from what I have found (which I have posted about previously) that on most routers this only disables the broadcast of SSID and still transmits as a "hidden network". Some have an option to turn off the 2.4 or 5 outside the SSID, but found some still transmitted a "hidden network".

You're right Mat. Some ISP provided routers are notorious for this stuff.

You can use an open source app like WiFi Analyzer to scan for nearby networks. hidden networks will still show up even without SSIDs.

Ref : [https://www.acrylicwifi.com/en/blog/hidden-ssid-wifi-how-to-know-name-of-network-without-ssid](https://www.acrylicwifi.com/en/blog/hidden-ssid-wifi-how-to-know-name-of-network-without-ssid/)

### Mat.I — 2025-08-26T12:58:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wifi-news-from-rusty.31547/post-358760>

> [Rusty said:](/goto/post?id=358758)
>
> You're right Mat. Some ISP provided routers are notorious for this stuff.
>
> You can use an open source app like WiFi Analyzer to scan for nearby networks. hidden networks will still show up even without SSIDs.

Yes, I use the analysers (including some advanced tools) and found the MACs (aka BSSIDs) always tied back to the router transmitting the hidden networks..I was yet to come accross a standard (non re-flashed one or open source firmware) that would actually disable the radios. Even with the toggle switches on the router on many all it would do is not broadcast the SSID..

IMO - best to avoid any wifi and go hardwired.. yes you minimise carrier wave exposure with one less network, however you are still getting pulsed.. Doesn't really change much in the grand scheme of things.

### Rusty — 2025-09-03T13:22:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wifi-news-from-rusty.31547/post-359076>

WiFi extenders?

Worst invention ever.

A WiFi extender/repeater isn’t just “1 more router”

It literally mirrors the radios of your main router.

Your main WiFi router usually has 2 radios.

Add an extender. It also has 2 radios (2.4 + 5 GHz)

So now instead of 2 transmitters in your space,

You’ve got 4 separate RF transmitters blasting.

And it doesn’t stop there.

The latest WiFi 7 standard unlocks the 6 GHz band too.

So now 1 router can run 3 radios at once

\- 2.4 GHz
\- 5 GHz
\- 6 GHz

Imagine adding an extender to that.

Now you don’t just have 3 radios,

You have 6x transmitters blasting all the time.

That’s way more artificial signals than your body was ever built to handle.

and this is just the Routers & Extenders..

Add in your Cellphones, AirPods, Smart watches

More & more Intracellular dehydration

More & more activation of VGCCs in the cells

More & more weakening of Blood-Brain-Barrier, blood-ocular/placenta/testis/CFS/skin/barrier & influx of toxins

MORE Sickness.

MORE Chronic health problems that no one can explain.

This is all compounding faster than the speed of light.

Meanwhile, Big Tech keeps pushing SMART upgrades you don’t actually need.

And the truth is,

you’ll probably keep buying

because the same blue-flickering screen you’re staring at right now was designed to make you.

### warriorT — 2025-09-16T08:18:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wifi-news-from-rusty.31547/post-359581>

If you are moving through rental properties would it be possible to carry a portable hardwiring kit to always be able to use Ethernet or does it depend on how each propertys wifi router is set up? What would a generic hard wiring kit look like?

### hacklose — 2025-09-16T19:17:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wifi-news-from-rusty.31547/post-359609>

> [warriorT said:](/goto/post?id=359581)
>
> If you are moving through rental properties would it be possible to carry a portable hardwiring kit to always be able to use Ethernet or does it depend on how each propertys wifi router is set up? What would a generic hard wiring kit look like?

Each property will be setup differently, so it will depend.
It will depend if the property uses a modem/router combo unit or a separate modem and router.

Most routers will enable you to turn off the wifi at the software level in the router settings, but I have seen some that do not allow it.
You should want to confirm with an RF meter that when you turn off the wifi bands in the router's settings, they actually turn off.

I prefer using routers with no wifi hardware inside. Mikrotik has a few units like this, but there are other options.

### rtd — 2025-09-16T22:32:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wifi-news-from-rusty.31547/post-359615>

> [warriorT said:](/goto/post?id=359581)
>
> If you are moving through rental properties would it be possible to carry a portable hardwiring kit to always be able to use Ethernet or does it depend on how each propertys wifi router is set up? What would a generic hard wiring kit look like?

1) A long Ethernet Cable. Recommend at least Cat5e shielded. A 50 foot should coil nicely for travel.
2) Small Network switch to add ethernet connections.
3) A few small Ethernet patch cables, at least 6ft
4) Velcro ties that secure to the cables. These help to stay tiddy
5) Some travel bag to store all this stuff. EagleCreek travel stuff comes to my mind. Maybe check out a marina/boat shop for waterproof travel bags.
6) A few RJ-45 to USB-C adapters. USB-C is popular on current devices. Laptops, cell phones etc may not have a physical RJ-45 port

At the location, check the router/cable modem. If there is a physical RJ-45 port (Ethernet) on the router/modem, you should be able to plug in an Ethernet cable and get a link light.

Turning off the WIFI broadcast, they can vary. For starters, maybe you do not have administrator privilege or the password to access the router or modem to make software changes, like disabling broadcast. Or maybe you are in luck and the modem has an external button to toggle the WIFI on/off.

### Rusty — 2025-09-25T06:19:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wifi-news-from-rusty.31547/post-360027>

If your house already has coaxial (TV/cable/DTH) wiring in the walls, you can plug MoCA (Multimedia over Coax Alliance) adapters at each end (router side + device side).

Many homes (especially in the US & parts of Europe) have coax jacks in multiple rooms so this adapter basically lets you repurpose the old TV cables in your home into a high-speed wired backbone

Also, MoCA is very different from PLC (Power Line Communication)

PLC forces data through your electrical wiring creating dirty electricity & turning your whole grid into a noisy antenna.

MoCA runs over shielded coax lines that were built to carry high-frequency signals.

That means:

\- No dirty electricity riding on your 50/60 Hz lines.
\- Less interference
\- Faster speeds

In fact, the latest MoCA 2.5 standard can deliver up to 2.5 Gbps across your coax network.

If you’ve ever wished for WIRED connections in every room without tearing open walls, MoCA is the hidden upgrade sitting in your house right now.

Good tip.. I have seen this done a few years back. One suggestion to add is to bond the coax main distribution splitter to earth that way the sheild on the coax will not float and become a noisy antenna.

### Mat.I — 2025-09-25T11:01:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wifi-news-from-rusty.31547/post-360032>

> [Mat.I said:](/goto/post?id=360030)
>
> Good tip.. I have seen this done a few years back. One suggestion to add is to bond the coax main distribution splitter to earth that way the sheild on the coax will not float and become a noisy antenna.

Also from memory, subject to the coax config (e.g. star with multiple branches via splitter) some mods will be required at the branch points to manage signal attenuation + any splitters must be rated for the op band of Moca. Margin is likely ~ 50 to 60dB so tou have to manage the attenuation budget by the configuration..

### Rusty — 2025-10-08T02:48:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wifi-news-from-rusty.31547/post-360635>

WiFi extenders?

Worst invention ever.

A WiFi extender/repeater isn’t just “1 more router”

It literally mirrors the radios of your main router.

Your main WiFi router usually has 2 radios.

Add an extender. It also has 2 radios (2.4 + 5 GHz)

So now instead of 2 transmitters in your space,

You’ve got 4 separate RF transmitters blasting.

And it doesn’t stop there.

The latest WiFi 7 standard unlocks the 6 GHz band too.

So now 1 router can run 3 radios at once

\- 2.4 GHz
\- 5 GHz
\- 6 GHz

Imagine adding an extender to that.

Now you don’t just have 3 radios,

You have 6x transmitters blasting all the time.

That’s way more artificial signals than your body was ever built to handle.

and this is just the Routers & Extenders..

Add in your Cellphones, AirPods, Smart watches

More & more Intracellular dehydration

More & more activation of VGCCs in the cells

More & more weakening of Blood-Brain-Barrier, blood-ocular/placenta/testis/CFS/skin/barrier & influx of toxins

MORE Sickness.

MORE Chronic health problems that no one can explain.

This is all compounding faster than the speed of light.

Meanwhile, Big Tech keeps pushing SMART upgrades you don’t actually need.

And the truth is,

you’ll probably keep buying

because the same blue-flickering screen you’re staring at right now was designed to make you.

### Anthony Smith — 2025-10-11T21:01:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wifi-news-from-rusty.31547/post-360893>

A hard wired ethernet set up takes care of RF - WiFi/bluetooth - IF - you turn those features off on ALL devices.
SOME networks can pick up a 60Hz (50Hz) electric field - you need to measure - and if so the network needs to be grounded.

### warriorT — 2025-10-11T22:18:40+0300

### Mat.I — 2025-10-13T11:05:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wifi-news-from-rusty.31547/post-360965>

> [Anthony Smith said:](/goto/post?id=360893)
>
> A hard wired ethernet set up takes care of RF - WiFi/bluetooth - IF - you turn those features off on ALL devices.
> SOME networks can pick up a 60Hz (50Hz) electric field - you need to measure - and if so the network needs to be grounded.

Or drain the noise at the source (0V) barrel on the DC side of the AC to DC power supply for router.. Or just bond and drain to a earth (portable stake in ground) anywhere else in the network. E.g. metal case + terminal network switch or at user device or via an alligator clip on sheilded and bonded ethernet metal jack etc.. many options to ground it.

### Mat.I — 2025-10-13T11:11:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wifi-news-from-rusty.31547/post-360966>

> [Mat.I said:](/goto/post?id=360965)
>
> Or drain the noise at the source (0V) barrel on the DC side of the AC to DC power supply for router.. Or just bond and drain to a earth (portable stake in ground) anywhere else in the network. E.g. metal case + terminal network switch or at user device or via an alligator clip on sheilded and bonded ethernet metal jack etc.. many options to ground it.

Also, I have had decent network setups on stock cat5e cable that had no induced voltages or EMI due to very neat and segregated cable runs (comms separate from LV). If you want to bulletproof it just go all sheilded with metal Jack's and bond to an earth.. Just bare in mine you may loose the bond at some equipment that does not have (metal outer jack casings) so you have to earth strategically in these cases.. I will find the thread and link it re: metal vs plastic jack casings..

### Rusty — 2025-10-29T09:51:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wifi-news-from-rusty.31547/post-361811>

Fiber optic cables transmit light, not electrical signals.

That means:

Zero electric fields (EF)
Zero magnetic fields (MF)
Zero dirty electricity (DE)
Zero conducted or radiated RF (EMI)

The only places where EMF returns is the endpoints, the media converters/switches that turn light back into electrical Ethernet signals.

**Q : Can you use Fiber Optic instead of Ethernet?**
A: Yes, you can absolutely use optical fiber instead of Ethernet cables.

You’ll need media converters (or fiber capable switches) at each end to turn the light signal (fiber) into an electrical signal (Ethernet)

Router → Media Converter → Fiber Cable → Media Converter → Device (via short Ethernet)

If you’re wiring multiple rooms, you’d only add more converters + fiber runs.

Total cost should be roughly $150-$200

**Q: How does this compare to normal shielded ethernet cables?**
A: Fiber Optic cable is completely different league.

It is completely immune to dirty electricity, induction or ground loops. The only EMF source is at the media converter boxes, and even that’s tiny and localized.

The shielded ethernet cable?

Even though it’s shielded, remember.. it still carries electrical signals.

### Mat.I — 2025-10-31T00:15:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wifi-news-from-rusty.31547/post-361916>

> [TheCircleBastiat said:](/goto/post?id=361907)
>
> Also, on my modem/router combo, if I turn off both the 2.4g and 5g bands (5g already off thanks to Rusty's instructions on X), is it then effectively an RF-free modem?

No you must measure or use the free network analyser to pick up any hidden SSID's. As typically when you disable in settings (even advanced) it transmits as a "hidden" network. If you have one of these modem/routers (which is most of them based on my experience) the only option if you wish to retain the modem/router - is to reflash the firmware with a a free open source one (quite straight forward) then when you disable radios they actually disable all radios...

### Mat.I — 2025-10-31T00:18:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wifi-news-from-rusty.31547/post-361917>

> [Mat.I said:](/goto/post?id=361916)
>
> No you must measure or use the free network analyser to pick up any hidden SSID's. As typically when you disable in settings (even advanced) it transmits as a "hidden" network. If you have one of these modem/routers (which is most of them based on my experience) the only option if you wish to retain the modem/router - is to reflash the firmware with a a free open source one (quite straight forward) then when you disable radios they actually disable all radios...

I have had ones that you can only pick up via RF meter. so I would say in all cases for absolute certainty a meter is required. As one did not transmit any "identified network" .. It is like a "hidden" beacon layer. So the only definitive test is with an RF meter.

### bulkbiceps — 2026-03-08T19:05:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-panels.31556/post-368392>

> [Mr.AJ said:](/goto/post?id=368386)
>
> I have a feeling that panel story that Jack talks about with his father in law was a lot more about restoring water production at CCO in an acute setting (injury) rather than as a replacement / supplement for the sun. Polarized light (panels) = D-aminos = nothing works properly.

Probably, trying to wrap my head around the polarization description I keep seeing. Guessing this definition fits it best?

**"Artificial light** from sources like LEDs or fluorescent bulbs can appear **polarized** because the emission process often involves directional or structured interactions, such as electron transitions in a specific orientation or reflection from surfaces. In quantum terms, this means the photons are emitted in a **non-random mixture of polarization states**, sometimes even in a coherent superposition, leading to measurable polarization.

**Sunlight**, on the other hand, is **unpolarized** because it originates from the Sun’s photosphere, a vast region where countless independent atoms emit light in random directions and phases. Each emission event is uncorrelated, and the resulting light is a **classical statistical mixture** of all possible polarization states. This mixture cannot be described as a pure quantum superposition—instead, it’s a **mixed state** with no preferred polarization direction. As a result, the quantum state of sunlight is not a coherent superposition of polarization modes, but rather a probabilistic distribution, which is why it is considered unpolarized."

Best I can see from search engine queries...

If that's the case, could it not be improved by forcing the light through a depolarizer? Like a matrix of ground glass powder or smaller diffuser with nanoparticle glass beads?

### Mr.AJ — 2026-03-08T19:52:10+0200

Plenty about it written on the forums. Just search.

### Anthony Smith — 2026-03-08T20:14:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-panels.31556/post-368396>

> [bulkbiceps said:](/goto/post?id=368392)
>
> Probably, trying to wrap my head around the polarization description I keep seeing. Guessing this definition fits it best?
>
> **"Artificial light** from sources like LEDs or fluorescent bulbs can appear **polarized** because the emission process often involves directional or structured interactions, such as electron transitions in a specific orientation or reflection from surfaces. In quantum terms, this means the photons are emitted in a **non-random mixture of polarization states**, sometimes even in a coherent superposition, leading to measurable polarization.
>
> **Sunlight**, on the other hand, is **unpolarized** because it originates from the Sun’s photosphere, a vast region where countless independent atoms emit light in random directions and phases. Each emission event is uncorrelated, and the resulting light is a **classical statistical mixture** of all possible polarization states. This mixture cannot be described as a pure quantum superposition—instead, it’s a **mixed state** with no preferred polarization direction. As a result, the quantum state of sunlight is not a coherent superposition of polarization modes, but rather a probabilistic distribution, which is why it is considered unpolarized."
>
> Best I can see from search engine queries...
>
> If that's the case, could it not be improved by forcing the light through a depolarizer? Like a matrix of ground glass powder or smaller diffuser with nanoparticle glass beads?

There appears to be some confusion between electrical polarization and light polarization . . .

**Electrical polarization:**
Unlike ordinary electronic components such as resistors and capacitors, LEDs are polarized, which means they can only emit light normally when powered in one direction. If the LED is connected in reverse, not only will it not light up, but it may also burn out sometimes. Therefore, it is very important to figure out the LED polarity, the LED symbol and connect the LED correctly.

**Light polarization:**
Many common light sources such as sunlight, halogen lighting, LED spotlights, and incandescent bulbs produce unpolarized light. If the direction of the electric field of light is well defined, it is called polarized light. The most common source of polarized light is a laser. [https://www.edmundoptics.com/knowledge-center/application-notes/optics/introduction-to-polarization/](https://www.edmundoptics.com/knowledge-center/application-notes/optics/introduction-to-polarization/)

While most LED’s are electricity polarized they do not necessarily put out polarized light (some LED’s do). Reflected light off water, glass etc - from any source, natural or artificial - becomes polarized.

Back in the day, as a professional photographer, if I was photographing a shinny object under incandescent studio lights I would have to put a polarizing filter on the lights **to polarize them** and use another polarizing filter on the lens so I could control the glare.

Also see from UC Davis:
\- In fact “natural” light from light bulbs and the sun is “unpolarized,” which comes about because each of the individual light sources (atoms) are aligned in random orientations, and all send out random, unaligned light waves.

### [2.6: Polarization](https://phys.libretexts.org/Courses/University_of_California_Davis/UCD%3A_Physics_9HC__Introduction_to_Waves_Physical_Optics_and_Quantum_Theory/2%3A_Physical_Optics/2.6%3A_Polarization)

Transverse waves in three dimensions like light have a degree of freedom related to the plane into which the transverse "displacement" occurs. For light in particular this leads to …

 phys.libretexts.org

### Manan — 2025-08-27T06:21:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-degree-burn-suggestions.31557/post-358789>

Context:

I live in Himachal Pradesh, India (**Latitude:** 32°). About 12 hours before this writing I had a superficial burn caused by steam through a gas stove, it affected my forehead, eyebrows and skin right under eyes. After 15 seconds I had water pouring over my head( 3 minutes), it felt hot and when I moved my felt very hot. Then I put my head in ice water for about 2 minutes. Then I went to ER with frozen corn reducing my burning sensation(no ice pack at home).

Followed SOP of BP and O2 level at hospital and had a teatnus injection. Thirty minutes later I was applied silver sulfadiazine( white paste/liquid) for reducing murning sensation from minimal to none even though it was not needed. Got my eyes checked after an hour, they were good with little dryness. Now 12 hours later I feel good and have few dark spots. While writing this I touched some of it to see that my skin peeled off and is healed with white skin(non tan).

Follow up:
I plan to sit under sun for Blue and UVA light to help skin regenerate faster with NO and POMC for skin pigmentation. Is there anything else I could do or anything I did wrong?

# Thread: Is the 5G antenna actually turned off? (Guide)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-the-5g-antenna-actually-turned-off-guide.31573/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 56
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Rusty — 2025-08-30T21:05:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-the-5g-antenna-actually-turned-off-guide.31573/post-358913>

Here’s a quick hack to check if 5G is truly off on your phone

**For iPhone users:**

→ Go to your keypad

→ Type **\*3001#12345#\***

→ If it says LTE

You’re on 4G, the 5G radio isn’t being used.

→ If it says NR (New Radio) or NR-SA / NR-NSA.

That’s true 5G.

**For most Androids:**

Type: \*#\*#4636#\*#\*

Go to Phone Information, scroll down

You’ll see which network type is active (LTE, NR etc)

**For most Samsung’s:**

Dial \*#0011#

If none of the above works,

Download **Network Signal Info/NetMonster** from Play Store.

### Rusty — 2025-09-16T22:51:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-the-5g-antenna-actually-turned-off-guide.31573/post-359616>

> [S Hudson said:](/goto/post?id=359508)
>
> what is the benefit turning off the phones ability to use 5G with the signal being pumped all around us?

5G uses beamforming technology where antennas focus signals directly toward active devices rather than radiating equally in all directions

That’s why it radiates much less than existing 2G, 3G, 4G technologies in all directions

but..

MUCH MORE in the direction of the 5G equipped user.

What does it mean?

It means that if you do not have a 5G device in your home or your hand, there will be only minimal radiation from the 5G base station (including satellites)

It also means that, if you use a 5G device, the power density radiated in your direction from the base station will depend significantly on the amount & speed of data you require.

If you are standing with a friend & he is watching a 4K movie on a 5G smartphone..then you both are radiated a hell of a lot more than while your friend is doing a phone call or texting. Most people do not realize that the power density needed to stream 4K video is thousands of times higher than what’s needed for a phone call.

That’s why

1\. Stay away from packed areas where everyone and their dog is glued to their 5G devices
2\. Turn off the 5G antenna on your cellphone

### Mat.I — 2025-09-21T01:31:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-the-5g-antenna-actually-turned-off-guide.31573/post-359773>

> [warriorT said:](/goto/post?id=359760)
>
> Mat I super value your input, are you able to comment on the biological impact of carrying a 5G capable handset (the 5G cannot be disabled due to grapheneOS) however it would not actually be using 5G in El Salvador as they have not rolled out there (yet) so I'd expect it would not be receiving focused signals directly with the beam forming technology used for 5G as no 5G towers would be sending signals - would this handset therefore be considered inactive 5G wise, or would it still be trying to actively connect to a 5G signal from somewhere, therefore incurring 5G damage to the person despite not using it?

There are standards that govern this like building codes / regulations but for mobile devices aka user equipment. My understanding is if there are no 5G networks the phone would only sniff for them at the firmware intervals. But the sniffing should not transmit (should.only be listen/receive only). That is it should only transmit when it detects the network (with uplink established) this is standard telecommunications protocols.

I do not have experience withGraphene OS phone (and personally would not buy one) but I find it strange how you cannot select your network preferences as this is a stock standard setting... If you cannot access via setting surely there would be a "hidden" menu access code?? Maybe search for this online.

Obviously, if you wanted absolute concrete certainty you need to log the frequencies with a spectrum analyzer fitted with the appropriate antenna that suits the target frequencies (inclusive of a signal decoder software to see SSB structure)..

If there is no 5G where you are, there should be no transmission other than intermittent sniffing (listening only).

Thank you!

You are right. I was really talking about the duty cycle not the instantaneous power density.

The beam stays “on” far more often, so over time you absorb way more energy. My point was about the load on the link & how it scales exposure.

### Rusty — 2025-09-21T12:08:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-the-5g-antenna-actually-turned-off-guide.31573/post-359792>

> Do they even stream phone calls on a 5G signal or do they drop down to 3G/4G?

From a biological signaling perspective, the sharp 5G spikes may be worse. Our cells respond more to pulsed, high peak bursts than to steady lower levels.

### Mat.I — 2025-09-22T02:35:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-the-5g-antenna-actually-turned-off-guide.31573/post-359809>

> [warriorT said:](/goto/post?id=359789)
>
> That is super helpful thank you. Would the antenna try to connect to 5G satellites in low earth orbit in the absence of 5G towers - could an uplink be established that way?

I thought this was only experimental and trial phases. The front ends & antenna of the devices would only be tuned to terrestrial 3GPP bands. If you are referring to the 5G-NTN (this is also only in trials phases as I understand). Whilst some phones may have the modem hardware, carriers would have to support this + it would need it to also have firmware to support.. I know they have standardised the 5G-NTN in recent 3GPP specs (based on what I have read on this), however it is in very early trial stages. All this means is the standard exists and R&D is being done.. True 5G - NTN may happen on a commercial level in some countries over the next few years... For this to be active you need the hardware + firmware + carrier provisioning + sat infrastructure.. I do not expect this to be an issue for you at this stage.

> [warriorT said:](/goto/post?id=359789)
>
> There is a way to disable 5G and use 4G only, however I understand much of El Salvador uses 3G and 2G - there does not seem to be any way to have just 2/3/4G only with a model that has 5G capability.

Are you in ES now? If you want to experiment, grab a ES sim and you may find you have other options based on the sim carrier profile e.g. 2G/3G/4G.. You will likely not get the option if you are in a place &/or with a carrier where certain bands have been shut down etc..

> [warriorT said:](/goto/post?id=359789)
>
> Am curious why you would not use GrapheneOS?

Does not make any sense to me.. Just on a very basic level it is software only (i.e. band aid) over shitty hardware. Every so called benefit/layered control is on a software level only.. Maybe I'm being to critical.. But it just does not really make sense to me that's all if your goal is max sovereignty & hardware level control when using the device.. Depends on why you "need" the phone and "what" is used for.. If you go minimalist with the above goal in mind then there will be trade offs for "convenience" with virtually no or minimal apps on the phone.. This decision is very individual - like BTC security & privacy, some will go all out and some will do nothing at all or kid themselves saying privacy does not matter...

### Rusty — 2025-10-08T02:44:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-the-5g-antenna-actually-turned-off-guide.31573/post-360633>

Type **\*3001#12345#\*** on your iPhone Keypad

You’ll find the Field Test Meter.

Of all the numbers there,

**RSRP** is the most important.

**RSRP = Reference Signal Received Power**

It tells you the Cellular signal quality.

and is a direct reflection of how hard your phone has to work & how much it radiates.

The weaker this number gets,

The louder your phone shouts back at the tower.

**above -90 dBm →** Strong, healthy signal

**\-90 to -100 dBm →** Your phone starts working harder

**Below -100 dBm →** Phone ramps up transmit power increasing radiation

**\-110 dBm or lower →** Max transmit power, Max radiation

If RSRP _is below -100 dBm_, that means your phone might be transmitting upto _**1000x more RF Radiation**_

### Mat.I — 2025-10-08T03:11:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-the-5g-antenna-actually-turned-off-guide.31573/post-360636>

> [Rusty said:](/goto/post?id=360633)
>
> The weaker this number gets,
>
> The louder your phone shouts back at the tower.
>
> **above -90 dBm →** Strong, healthy signal
>
> **\-90 to -100 dBm →** Your phone starts working harder
>
> **Below -100 dBm →** Phone ramps up transmit power increasing radiation
>
> **\-110 dBm or lower →** Max transmit power, Max radiation
>
> If RSRP _is below -100 dBm_, that means your phone might be transmitting upto _**1000x more RF Radiation**_

People often miss this basic operation principle.. Good to see you mentioning this.
Re: 1000 x more RF based on transmit (locally at device this should be caped at ~ 200mW) however, where this becomes a problem is the duty cycle.. E.g. if you assumed an RSRP of - 80dBm @ tx ~ 10mW say duty cycle 0.05% vs -110 dBm @ ~ 200mW duty cycle 10% could result in ~ 4000 x more. 10mW x 0.0005 = 0.005mW vs 200mW x 0.10 = 20mW. Now 20mW/0.005mW = 4000 x

### Rusty — 2025-10-11T09:52:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-the-5g-antenna-actually-turned-off-guide.31573/post-360879>

> [Mat.I said:](/goto/post?id=360636)
>
> People often miss this basic operation principle.. Good to see you mentioning this.
> Re: 1000 x more RF based on transmit (locally at device this should be caped at ~ 200mW) however, where this becomes a problem is the duty cycle.. E.g. if you assumed an RSRP of - 80dBm @ tx ~ 10mW say duty cycle 0.05% vs -110 dBm @ ~ 200mW duty cycle 10% could result in ~ 4000 x more. 10mW x 0.0005 = 0.005mW vs 200mW x 0.10 = 20mW. Now 20mW/0.005mW = 4000 x

### Mark Copeland — 2025-11-04T17:39:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-the-5g-antenna-actually-turned-off-guide.31573/post-362140>

> [Rusty said:](/goto/post?id=360633)
>
> Type **\*3001#12345#\*** on your iPhone Keypad
>
> You’ll find the Field Test Meter.
>
> Of all the numbers there,
>
> **RSRP** is the most important.
>
> **RSRP = Reference Signal Received Power**
>
> It tells you the Cellular signal quality.
>
> and is a direct reflection of how hard your phone has to work & how much it radiates.
>
> The weaker this number gets,
>
> The louder your phone shouts back at the tower.
>
> **above -90 dBm →** Strong, healthy signal
>
> **\-90 to -100 dBm →** Your phone starts working harder
>
> **Below -100 dBm →** Phone ramps up transmit power increasing radiation
>
> **\-110 dBm or lower →** Max transmit power, Max radiation
>
> If RSRP _is below -100 dBm_, that means your phone might be transmitting upto _**1000x more RF Radiation**_
>
>

### Rusty — 2025-11-05T14:03:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-the-5g-antenna-actually-turned-off-guide.31573/post-362194>

Likely be an ratio average of peak signals over a time duration. I guess could be a good proxy for activity density.. e.g. choppy vs continuous but this is fairly easy to distinguish by sound alone e.g. cell tower vs wifi and so on..

Got a response from one of the members from the RD-100 development team

`"I think that the Duty Cycle is essential for the analysis. It is not 100% accurate since there are usually many signals in the air...for a better picture of the signal, use the RF Modulation mode, both in 5mSec resolution and 1mSec resolution. I predict that if there is 2 signals on the air, one of 5% with 10mW/m2 peaks, and the second one of 5% with 5mW/m2, the RD-100 will show 10% on the duty cycle."`

### Rusty — 2025-11-05T14:04:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-the-5g-antenna-actually-turned-off-guide.31573/post-362195>

> [Mark Copeland said:](/goto/post?id=362140)
>
> Does airplane mode stop most of the signal?

Most but not all.

Sometimes it won't even turn off the WiFi and bluetooth completely (required for airdrop)

UWB chip is always on.

If Location Tracking is on, that's another one.

### Mark Copeland — 2025-11-05T18:26:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-the-5g-antenna-actually-turned-off-guide.31573/post-362210>

>
> Sometimes it won't even turn off the WiFi and bluetooth completely (required for airdrop)
>
> UWB chip is always on.
>
> If Location Tracking is on, that's another one.

Thanks for sharing this. I checked my phone and the signal was -116dBm. Usually, I have 5G & Cellular Data off at home because it doesn't work well anyways, my home is too far from cell towers. I use an iPhone. Anything else you recommend to mitigate exposure?

### Anthony Smith — 2025-11-05T22:03:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-the-5g-antenna-actually-turned-off-guide.31573/post-362220>

> [Mark Copeland said:](/goto/post?id=362210)

Turn it off - but GPS etc still functions.
iPhone - drain the battery or get a phone you can remove or turn off the battery.

If you use tech . . . You have nnEMF.

I predict that if there is 2 signals on the air, one of 5% with 10mW/m2 peaks, and the second one of 5% with 5mW/m2, the RD-100 will show 10% on the duty cycle."`

Yes for intermitent non-overlap of duties that makes sense for a basic detector.. d1 + d2 = d or for full overlap d1 = d2 = d or partial would be d1 + d2 - d overlap..

### Anthony Smith — 2026-01-05T20:04:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-the-5g-antenna-actually-turned-off-guide.31573/post-364794>

ANY phone is traceable/trackable and gives off nnEMF when in use.
ANY phone is not traceable/trackable when turned off AND in a Faraday bag.
You may invest in a phone that you can disconnect the battery BUT the second you turn it on “they” have you, so what’s the point?

Also keep in mind most countries have cameras everywhere. They can/are tracking your car AND many have digital face recognition.

AND . . . IF you use credit/debit cards they know where you are AND what you bought.

I do not have one. Of course, when you reconnect the battery it will ping. I have a satellite phone for back up that gives me 150 min. a month that cumulate and don't expire. I don't keep it on. I guess the best would be to buy throw aways (burner phone). Use and toss. Most ppl don't pay attention to the Stingray antenna's law enforcement use at intersections. Pretty controversial, invasion of privacy. We already live in a controlled censored society.

# Thread: Proper use of EMR-TEK lighting
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/proper-use-of-emr-tek-lighting.31574/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area

### ColoPE — 2025-08-31T01:45:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/proper-use-of-emr-tek-lighting.31574/post-358927>

Hey all, I have a Krypton UV / IR light from EMR-TEK. Their guide talks in terms of under 10 minutes use for certain organs, but it doesn't even differentiate between UV and IR. I have searched here and in the blogs for some guidance on best use. This would be for adding to the sun we get daily - up to 4 hours. It is especially for use once it gets really cold here to sit out (CO). We are looking for therapeutic guidance, not maintenance. I'm guessing one should use red only in the early parts of the day and UV only midday, but I have no idea how much is too much.

Also, in light of the 80 year old that used Jack's light and cured cataracts, is it a bad idea not to use the covering goggles that came with it? Guidance appreciated.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-08-31T18:39:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/proper-use-of-emr-tek-lighting.31574/post-358956>

I would think the manufacturer is responsible for telling you how to use it no?

### ColoPE — 2025-09-01T01:29:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/proper-use-of-emr-tek-lighting.31574/post-358991>

### Elster402 — 2025-10-07T05:38:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/proper-use-of-emr-tek-lighting.31574/post-360546>

Are you sure it was “cataracts,” or was it acute macular degeneration of Dr. Kruse’s then 83-ish-yo father-in-law? In the Danny Jones and other interview(s), Dr. K described instructing his FIL to go to his office to sit in front of his \[EMR-TEK?\] lamp for a few sessions per day WITH the protective glasses on, but his FIL neglected to do so, and \[thereby\] reversed his AMD, which was a revelation on the utility of red light therapy without the protective glasses to heal eyes and beyond. But the manufacturers and healthcare providers won’t instruct people to not use the goggles due to liability.

I recall a separate discussion about cataracts in perhaps another interview in which Dr. K mentioned a single replacement lens manufacturer in Germany which makes lenses that don’t \[?filter parts of the light spectrum?-(did I get that correct?)\] as do all other manufacturers. Dr. K said I think that he had a patient who was able to find an American cataract doc who agreed to replace the cataract with that German lens. Dr. K emphasized the potential downstream health hazard of replacing a cataract with a “filtered” lens, disrupting the light-eye pathway—>POMC…

Please correct me if I misstated the record.

### NickD — 2025-11-07T11:18:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/proper-use-of-emr-tek-lighting.31574/post-362278>

I have the Krypton unit. I've had it for a year now, I think. I don't use any eye protection - but I don't look directly into the light. I usually read a book or something, or I'm playing with my kids in front of it. I only use the UV functions in the morning and during the day. I don't use them at night. I work shifts and have it as a supplement and not as a complete source of light...

# Thread: Leptin prescription for female hormones
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-prescription-for-female-hormones.31577/>

### MillieMartire — 2025-09-04T03:08:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/atrial-fibrillation.31595/post-359100>

Hi. I have been following Jack for a little under a year through podcasts and reading blogs. I am a new member and just joined in the last month or two. Last week I was taken to ER due to rapid heart rate. I get it off and on. My iWatch alerted me to posible AFIB last year, but I have stopped wearing that due to emf and listening to Jack and following protocols. In ER I was in afib and they controlled it with medication. I have not taken any meds in 4 years (used to be on BP meds). I hate it that I am back. I practice circadian rhythm and wake up and go outside for sunrise and watch sunset and get sunlight any chance I get, especially at noon. I live in the Washington DC area and just listed our house to move out of here. I had lost a lot of weight (100 lbs) a few years ago on keto and IF and made the mistake of going on Semiglutide to lose the last 50. I had some adverse reactions, but stopped taking it when I heard Jack talk about it and did some research. No matter what I do the weight has come back (all except about 30 lbs) - I am starting the EPI paleo RX and have made changes to eat locally sourced and in season. I have purchased some red lights from EMR tek but they are taking forever to arrive. I try and do cold plunges as I am able. I am a member of Dr. Alexis bookclub and am trying to reduce nnEMFs. I work on the computer and need a couple more years of working before I can retire. We are trying to move lower lattitude and thought florida would be it, but my elderly mom and my sister need help in TX. WE are considering TX (Outside Dallas or outside Houston) until I heard Jack on the Sept Powow and he said neither is good. I am under an incredible amount of stress and now this afib has added to that. I would love some pointers on what I can do until the house sells and we can move. Also, I cannot seem to find anything on afib, so help locating info would be appreciated. I am working my way through the blogs and material. Thank you. I am excited to be here with like minded savages.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-10-28T04:22:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/atrial-fibrillation.31595/post-361731>

Light at night and heart disease links are strong! This was just published this today in JAMA Network Open.

Using 13 million hours of personal light exposure data in >88,000 people, they found that those with the brightest nights had significantly higher risks of developing coronary artery disease, myocardial infarction, heart failure, **atrial fibrillation,** and stroke.

These associations were robust after adjusting for established cardiovascular risk factors, including physical activity, smoking, alcohol, diet, sleep duration, socioeconomic status, and polygenic risk. Keep your homes dark at night and get in the sun for photorepair of your arteries and heart!

[https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamanetworkopen/fullarticle/2840489](https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamanetworkopen/fullarticle/2840489)

### MillieMartire — 2025-10-28T14:10:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/atrial-fibrillation.31595/post-361749>

Thank you. I have been blocking blue light and night light for 18 mo. Not enough to undo the damage yet (I was a night owl for 40 years) - moving south to a better light environment trying to reduce nnEMFs within 8 weeks. I will keep reading and learning with a thankful heart that I found this info and even though I am older I can begin to heal!

### Mat.I — 2025-10-29T00:39:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/atrial-fibrillation.31595/post-361790>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=361731)
>
> Light at night and heart disease links are strong! This was just published this today in JAMA Network Open.
>
> Using 13 million hours of personal light exposure data in >88,000 people, they found that those with the brightest nights had significantly higher risks of developing coronary artery disease, myocardial infarction, heart failure, **atrial fibrillation,** and stroke.
>
> These associations were robust after adjusting for established cardiovascular risk factors, including physical activity, smoking, alcohol, diet, sleep duration, socioeconomic status, and polygenic risk. Keep your homes dark at night and get in the sun for photorepair of your arteries and heart!
>
> [https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamanetworkopen/fullarticle/2840489](https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamanetworkopen/fullarticle/2840489)

The "night" light exposure windows could have been tightened to get more accurate data. E.g.

Exposure: Personal Light Tracking
Extraction of personal day and night light exposures from wrist-worn light sensor data in this cohort has been previously reported.[32](https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamanetworkopen/fullarticle/2840489#zoi251081r32) ,[33](https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamanetworkopen/fullarticle/2840489#zoi251081r33),[35](https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamanetworkopen/fullarticle/2840489#zoi251081r35) In short, sensor data (100 Hz) were downsampled, cleaned for periods of nonwear and data corruption, and transformed according to the device manual and subsequent testing under reference lighting conditions[35](https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamanetworkopen/fullarticle/2840489#zoi251081r35) (eMethods in [Supplement 1](https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamanetworkopen/fullarticle/2840489#note-ZOI251081-1)). Data were then grouped into 24-hour light exposure profiles for each participant, represented by mean light exposure within each of the 48 half-hour clock time intervals (eg, all light between 12:00 AM to 12:30 AM). Factor analysis was applied to the 24-hour light exposure profiles.[35](https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamanetworkopen/fullarticle/2840489#zoi251081r35) This unsupervised analysis revealed 2 temporal light exposure clusters, which we labeled as day (07:30 AM to 8:30 PM) and night (12:30 AM to 06:00 AM) (eMethods in [Supplement 1](https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamanetworkopen/fullarticle/2840489#note-ZOI251081-1)).

As "night" should begin post sunset when solar angle = less than 0? This would be the most circadian sensitive phase. They left a lot on the table here as they captured the worst of the worst night owls or disturbed sleepers.. Would have been good to have a contrast here to see the effects. Night should be defined by solar angle not clock time..

E.g. solar angle based or chronotype aligned definition of this (night) would have permitted biologically meaningful comparisons across seasons, and would have better quantified how > sunset light exposure, rather than late night illumination alone, contributes to cardiovascular risk. They would have this raw data available.

# Thread: Eyes, brain, skull, and spine disease all begin here.........
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eyes-brain-skull-and-spine-disease-all-begin-here.31603/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2025-09-06T19:56:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eyes-brain-skull-and-spine-disease-all-begin-here.31603/post-359190>

A new blog has been released with significant embryological implications for our modern world. Most won't see this wisdom, but I will force you to see what Nature is cooking for humanity if we persist. [https://www.patreon.com/posts/decentralized-71-124096262](https://www.patreon.com/posts/decentralized-71-124096262)

While conventional medicine attributes nanophthalmos to genetic factors, such as mutations in PRSS56 or MFRP, which lead to compromised ocular growth, a decentralized medicine perspective shifts the focus to disrupted bioelectric buoyancy as the root cause. This buoyancy is a thermodynamic dance involving mitochondrial water production, melanin hydration, and photo-bioelectric gradients that sustain precise tissue expansion. Intrinsically tied to brain and skull development, it relies on ancient evolutionary adaptations from the Great Oxidation Event (GOE) around 2.4 billion years ago, when rising oxygen levels favored organisms with hydrated melanin and deuterium-depleted water (DDW) for efficient bioelectric signaling.

In this framework, the eye's growth falters when modern stressors, non-native electromagnetic fields (nnEMF) from devices and artificial light at night (ALAN, e.g., blue light), damage melanopsin (a light-sensitive protein in the retina), mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA), and the glyoxalase pathway (which detoxifies harmful byproducts). Dehydrated melanin transitions from a protective resistor to a hyper-conductive state, erasing the ultra-precise one-trillionth-amp DC currents needed for cellular renovation at cytochrome c oxidase, a key mitochondrial enzyme. Compounding this, contemporary diets and environments increase deuterium loading through the kinetic isotope effect (KIE), where heavier deuterium isotopes slow proton tunneling in the Grotthuss mechanism. This is a quantum process that facilitates efficient water conduction in cells. This isotopic burden, absent in GOE-adapted systems relying on DDW for optimal mitochondrial spin and energy transfer, hampers ocular morphogenesis, resulting in the short axial length characteristic of nanophthalmos.

The outcome?

A complex story of Turing's morphogenesis ideas in one disease. In 1952, Alan Turing, a British mathematician best known for his work on code-breaking and artificial intelligence, was convicted of engaging in homosexual acts and sentenced to chemical castration. Amid that personal drama, he still found the time to publish a visionary paper on the mathematics of regularly repeating patterns in nature, which could be applied to the stripes on tigers and zebra fish, the spots on leopards, and the spacing in rows of alligator teeth, to name a few. Turing's 1952 paper on morphogenesis reaction-diffusion model posits that patterns, such as spots, stripes, or even organ scaling, arise from the interplay of diffusible morphogens: an activator that promotes a trait and an inhibitor that suppresses it, diffusing at different rates to create instability in an otherwise homogeneous field. This model, once speculative, has gained empirical support in developmental biology, including eye formation, where morphogens like Sonic Hedgehog (Shh) and Fibroblast Growth Factor (FGF) act as inhibitors and activators, respectively.

In the context of nanophthalmos, a condition of proportionally small but structurally normal eyes. Turing's mechanism illuminates how disruptions in morphogen gradients during early embryogenesis can impair axial growth and scaling, linking to the bioelectric and quantum disruptions. Turing's model extends naturally to the eye's morphogenesis, which originates from the diencephalon and involves precise patterning of the optic vesicle, cup, and lens. Here, morphogens create spatial gradients that dictate tissue identity, proliferation, and size. For instance, Shh, secreted from the ventral midline, acts as a long-range inhibitor, repressing distal retinal markers, such as Pax6, while promoting proximal optic stalk identity.

FGF, often from the surface ectoderm or lens placode, serves as a short-range activator, inducing neural retina differentiation and optic cup invagination. This activator-inhibitor dynamic mirrors Turing's equations, where local activation and lateral inhibition generate patterns like the ventral optic fissure or retinal cell mosaics. A key example is the Pax6-Tgfb2-Fst network, which forms a Turing-like system in the optic vesicle. Pax6, the eye's master regulator, auto-activates with Follistatin (Fst) as a short-range enhancer, while TGF-beta2 (Tgfb2) inhibits over longer distances, polarizing the vesicle along proximal-distal axes and enabling spontaneous retinal organization, even in vitro organoids. This self-organization ensures coordinated growth between retina, lens, and surrounding tissues, tying into brain development via shared diencephalic origins. However, Turing's model alone doesn't fully explain scaling, how patterns adjust proportionally to organ size, as seen in varying egg sizes yielding complete chicks. Additional "tuning" mechanisms, like Hox genes in digit patterning, modulate wavelength (spacing) in Turing systems. In the eye, N-myc downstream of Shh and FGF coordinates retinal proliferation with overall eye size via cell cycle regulators like p27Kip1, ensuring proportional growth.

_**Disruptions here decouple retina from eye scaling, leading to thin retinas in small eyes seen in this disease. This blog parts the sea so one can see how brain, skull, and spinal morphology go awry via transgenerational sculpting of modern light.

**_

Open to other suggestions if anyone has them.

### Mr.AJ — 2025-09-07T15:56:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goats-milk-for-infants.31605/post-359208>

Just to elaborate on this from my own research. MD's are saying that goats milk is not safe for babies under 1 year old due to lack of vitamins (iron b12 etc) and too much protein, which they say can cause kidney problems (retards) and lactose issues (lower in goats milk than cows). They instead recommend taking a fortified goats milk formula.

But if you think back to 200 years ago, before great companies like Nestle were making deuterium loaded formula for infants, surely the logical step for a mother who could not make enough milk was to feed the baby milk from the family farm. I don't think it was either drink moms milk or baby dies.

So basically I think all of the advice I'm reading today is bs.

### Mr.AJ — 2025-09-07T16:05:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goats-milk-for-infants.31605/post-359210>

Literally right above this grok regurgitated the "too much protein bad" then I ask for actual published studies or evidence showing kidney damage in infants from excess protein and got this:

There is limited direct evidence from published studies specifically linking high-protein milk, such as goat’s milk or high-protein infant formulas, to kidney damage in healthy infants. However, some studies and clinical observations address related concerns, particularly about the impact of high protein intake on infant kidney function. Below is a summary based on available research:

\### Key Findings from Relevant Studies
1\. \*\*High-Protein Formula and Kidney Function (Escribano et al., 2010)\*\*:
\- A European multicenter clinical trial examined 601 formula-fed infants assigned to either low-protein (LP) or high-protein (HP) formulas for the first year of life, compared with breastfed infants. At 6 months, infants on HP formula showed significantly higher kidney volume and serum urea levels compared to those on LP formula or breastfed infants. However, the study did not report direct kidney damage (e.g., acute kidney injury or chronic kidney disease) but noted that higher protein intake increased kidney size and workload, which could have long-term implications. The authors called for further research on long-term kidney function effects.\[\](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S008525381554876X)
\- \*\*Key Point\*\*: Increased kidney size and urea levels suggest a higher renal workload, but no conclusive evidence of damage was reported in healthy infants.

2\. \*\*Nutritional Management in Infants with CKD (Shaw et al., 2022)\*\*:
\- A review in \*Pediatric Nephrology\* discusses nutritional management for infants with pre-existing chronic kidney disease (CKD) stages 2–5. It notes that high protein intake, including from high-protein milk or formulas, can exacerbate issues in infants with CKD due to increased urea levels and potential hyperphosphatemia, which may strain already compromised kidneys. The study emphasizes adjusting protein intake to avoid excessive renal stress but does not directly link high-protein milk to kidney damage in healthy infants.\[\](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00467-022-05529-x)
\- \*\*Key Point\*\*: Infants with pre-existing kidney issues are more vulnerable to high protein intake, but this does not confirm damage in healthy infants.

3\. \*\*General Protein Intake and Kidney Health\*\*:
\- A 2018 meta-analysis by McMaster University researchers, published in \*The Journal of Nutrition\*, examined high-protein diets in healthy adults and found no evidence that high protein intake causes kidney damage in healthy individuals. While this study focused on adults, it challenges the broader myth that high protein inherently harms kidneys, suggesting that healthy kidneys can handle higher protein loads. No similar comprehensive meta-analysis exists specifically for infants.\[\](https://brighterworld.mcmaster.ca/articles/myth-busted-researchers-show-that-a-high-protein-diet-does-not-affect-kidney-function/)
\- \*\*Key Point\*\*: Extrapolating to infants, healthy kidneys may tolerate high protein without damage, but infants’ kidneys are less mature, so caution is warranted.

\### Context for Infants
\- \*\*Kidney Maturity\*\*: Infants have immature kidneys that are less efficient at handling high protein and mineral loads compared to adults. High-protein milk (e.g., goat’s milk or high-protein formulas) can increase renal solute load, leading to higher serum urea and potential dehydration, which could stress the kidneys.
\- \*\*Goat’s Milk Concerns\*\*: Goat’s milk is naturally high in protein (approximately 3.5–4 g/100 mL, compared to 1–1.5 g/100 mL in human milk) and minerals like potassium and phosphorus. While no studies directly confirm kidney damage from goat’s milk in healthy infants, its high protein and mineral content could pose risks, especially if unpasteurized or used as a primary milk source, due to potential dehydration or electrolyte imbalances.
\- \*\*No Direct Evidence of Damage\*\*: No studies explicitly document high-protein milk causing acute kidney injury or chronic kidney damage in healthy infants. Most concerns are theoretical, based on increased renal workload or risks in infants with pre-existing kidney conditions.

\### Limitations and Gaps
\- \*\*Lack of Specific Studies\*\*: There are no large-scale, randomized controlled trials directly investigating whether high-protein milk (e.g., goat’s milk or high-protein formulas) causes kidney damage in healthy infants. Most studies focus on infants with CKD or compare formula types without reporting overt kidney damage.
\- \*\*Long-Term Effects Unknown\*\*: As noted in the Escribano study, the long-term impact of increased kidney size or workload from high-protein intake in infancy is unclear and requires further research.
\- \*\*Confounding Factors\*\*: Studies on infants with CKD or other conditions often involve additional factors (e.g., dialysis, congenital anomalies), making it hard to isolate the effect of high-protein milk alone.

\### Conclusion
No published studies conclusively show that high-protein milk, such as goat’s milk or high-protein formulas, directly causes kidney damage in healthy infants. However, evidence suggests that high protein intake increases kidney workload, as seen in higher kidney volume and serum urea levels, which could pose risks, particularly for infants with pre-existing kidney issues or if high-protein milk is used exclusively without supplementation. For healthy infants, the American Academy of Pediatrics recommends breast milk or iron-fortified formula for the first 12 months, as high-protein milks like goat’s milk may stress immature kidneys and lack essential nutrients. Always consult a pediatrician before introducing high-protein milk to an infant’s diet.\[\](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S008525381554876X)\[\](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00467-022-05529-x)\[\](https://brighterworld.mcmaster.ca/articles/myth-busted-researchers-show-that-a-high-protein-diet-does-not-affect-kidney-function/)
