**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/black-sandy-beach.31850/post-363768>

**LENR as a Bridge from Stellar Plasma to Cellular "Alchemy"**
Condense matter physics posits that the Sun is not powered by internal nuclear fusion but rather acts as an anode in a vast galactic electrical circuit, with energy supplied via large-scale plasma discharges and Birkeland currents (field-aligned currents that transport charged particles along magnetic field lines). These currents are envisioned as connecting the Sun to planets, facilitating energy transfer and potentially influencing planetary surface and atmospheric chemistry through electromagnetic interactions.

The SAFIRE project, an experimental effort inspired by Electric Universe ideas, simulates solar-like plasma environments in a laboratory setting using electric discharges across an anode (often metallic, including iron composites) in a hydrogen-rich chamber. Key findings from SAFIRE include the observation of apparent low-energy nuclear reactions (LENR), where plasma discharges lead to elemental transmutations, such as the appearance of new elements (e.g., titanium, barium, calcium) on the anode surface that were not present in the original material, and structural changes at the atomic level, including melting and recrystallization of probes exposed to the plasma.

From first principles under my decentralized framework, a plasma discharge from the Sun via Birkeland currents would indeed provide a mechanism for isotopic variations in iron (δ 57Fe) between Mars and Earth, as follows: Plasma-Induced Nuclear Transmutations and Isotopic Shifts: SAFIRE experiments demonstrate that high-energy plasma states, created by voltage gradients (e.g., ~270 V, equivalent to extreme effective temperatures), can split molecular hydrogen into monatomic hydrogen and free protons.

These protons, in an electrically charged metallic matrix (like an iron-based anode), facilitate LENR-like processes that alter atomic nuclei, leading to transmutations. While SAFIRE does not explicitly detail iron-specific isotopic changes, the broader implication is that such discharges can rearrange nucleons, potentially favoring the production or depletion of specific isotopes based on binding energies and reaction pathways. For iron, which is multivalent and abundant in planetary reservoirs, this could manifest as shifts in the 57Fe/54Fe ratio.

In a solar context, Birkeland currents carrying protons and ions from the Sun could bombard planetary surfaces or mantles, inducing similar nuclear modifications. Mars, with its thinner atmosphere and weaker magnetic field (compared to Earth's protective magnetosphere), would be more susceptible to direct penetration by these charged particles, potentially leading to greater transmutation effects that preserve or revert iron isotopes toward chondritic values (δ57Fe ≈ 0‰), as observed in SNC meteorites.

The science behind this is published in cendensed matter physics or LENR mechanisms and not well known.

If we have evidence that LENR is operational in the SAFIRE lab what does it mean for cells and their mitochondrial who evolved under the sun? [https://www.patreon.com/posts/decentralized-85-124737948](https://www.patreon.com/posts/decentralized-85-124737948)

This implies Darwin's theory on evolution is missing a lot and maybe now we know why much of Newton's life was spent studying alchemy.

Isaac Newton was a devoted alchemist who secretly spent decades performing experiments in search of the philosopher's stone and the ability to transmute base metals into gold. While Newton was very much an alchemist who sought transmutation, he lived in a different era where chemistry was not even a formed science.
Newton died 16 years before Lavoisier was born.
Lavoisier introduced the "Galilean method" in chemistry, contributing to its "scientific foundation".
On the basis of his own experiments he could observe that "in all chemical reactions the same quantity of matter is present before and after the reaction". Lavoisier consequently makes the hypothesis that in a chemical reaction transmutations from one element to another do not occur.

Louis Vauquelin was a chemist in Lavoisier's tradition who, after Lavoisier's death, observed that hens excreted more calcium than they consumed from their diet of oats. He concluded, correctly, that lime (calcium oxide) had been formed (from potassium) but could not explain the mechanism, as the concept of low energy nuclear reactions was unknown at the time.
This idea seemed to both Newton as he became a scientist. I believe this is why Newton was interested in alchemy. The sequence of events I described in the blog above highlights a specific historical argument that some experimental results suggesting biological elemental transmutation, particularly those by Vauquelin and Vogel, were marginalized by the scientific community because they contradicted the prevailing dogma of the immutability of elements, a principle established by Lavoisier.

This perspective argues that the scientific community neglected robust evidence because it lacked a theoretical framework (nuclear physics was unknown). What happened when nuclear physics was known? The disconnect due to Bohr and Heisenberg is why no one is studying LENR in quantum biology. All cell biology is based on condensed matter physics that LENR operates on.

**Implication for Mitochondria:** Evolved ~1.5–2 Ga ago via endosymbiosis, mitochondria internalized solar-like plasma dynamics: proton gradients (from UV-decomposed water) drive ETC, but LENR suggests they transmute elements (e.g., C + O → Fe traces) for cofactor synthesis, optimizing under variable solar UV/EMF. Quantum biology confirms: proton/electron tunneling in ETC achieves 60–70% efficiency via coherence, far beyond classical limits. In low-UV states (modern deficiency), this fails, broadening UPEs and reversing TCA cycles—our "entropy surge" results in modern disease epidemics.

**Challenging Darwin: Transmutations as the "Missing Photonic Leap"**

Beyond Gradualism: Darwin's framework excels at macro-selection but misses quantum/nuclear drivers. SAFIRE/LENR implies evolution via plasma transmutations: solar Birkeland protons fractionate isotopes (EMIS in currents) and forge elements, providing "leaps" like Cambrian DHA integration or GPCR radical-pair asymmetries ~1.2 Ga ago amid rising solar luminosity. Biological transmutations (microbial Cs-137 deactivation 35–200x faster than decay via coherent states) show cells as LENR sites, conserving nucleons but altering elements, reviving Prout's 1815 hydrogen-progenitor idea. This "alchemical evolution" explains stasis (DHA's 600 Ma irreplaceability) as lock-in from photonic mandates, not random drift.

**mtDNA UPEs as Evolutionary "Marching Orders":** Mitochondria's UPE output (ROS photons ~200–300 nm) encodes quantum information, biased by PV using unpolarized solar UV, directing metabolism via leptin/GPCRs. LENR in cristae lattices (Vogel's 1848 organism transmutations) suggests UPEs signal transmutative events, reducing impedance for stellar flow which is my "geometry correction" over ATP "production." In SAFIRE experiments, plasma excites similar emissions; evolution tuned mitochondria to amplify solar photons into DC signals (DHA-like), explaining why aromatic acids absorb UVC for redox fidelity. _**The pieces all fit.**_

# Thread: CHARLIE KIRK'S DEATH HAD A LOT IN COMMON WITH SOCRATES TRIAL AND DEATH
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/charlie-kirks-death-had-a-lot-in-common-with-socrates-trial-and-death.31855/>
**Subforum:** The Kruse Longevity Center
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-09T02:33:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/charlie-kirks-death-had-a-lot-in-common-with-socrates-trial-and-death.31855/post-362375>

As a student of a brilliant philosophy professor, I can tell you Charlie Kirk was a Socrates. He did more to teach America’s university students about how to have logical arguments than the totality of leftist philosophy profs combined. He offered rational dialogue radical profs offered hate. I will honor his memory by continuing to use logic to refute fascist authoritarian ideas so that students stop signing up for DEI classes and programs. One way his legacy will be honored is when students learn how to convey through logical arguments DEI really stands for "didn't earn it."

Socrates' story is a profound lens for examining the discomfort and conflicts in the modern technocracy. The Athenian philosopher's trial and execution in 399 BC weren't just about personal vendettas; they exposed how societies punish those who dismantle illusions and challenge entrenched power. Socrates' relentless questioning, his Socratic method, forced people to confront their inconsistencies, exposing corrupt leaders and false wisdom. He was a threat because he instilled doubt in a centralized system that thrived on unquestioned certainties. Refusing to compromise his principles, even at the cost of his life, he embodied the idea that true intelligence disrupts comfort.

Socrates, the greatest philosopher of all time, was actually the most hated man in Athens. Charlie Kirk was the most hated man in the DNC, Israel, and with RINOs.

Socrates was accused of cruelty and corrupting the youth.The popular court, the Heliaia, sentenced him to death; and Socrates, one of the brightest minds in history, died by drinking hemlock.

But why all the hype?

Apparently, Socrates wasn’t doing anything dangerous:He asked questions, spoke with anyone, nobles, ordinary citizens, young people. But his questions, in their frankness and simplicity, demolished the certainties of his interlocutors, forcing them to confront the emptiness of their own convictions and the inconsistency of their reasoning. He taught them to doubt.

Socrates was a character far too uncomfortable for the doubts he instilled. He had the audacity to expose corrupt politicians and false teachers who, believing they knew, boasted false truths and fake knowledge. That is why he was sentenced to death. He was a threat to the status quo, a danger that had to be eliminated.

During the trial, Socrates refused to repent or beg for mercy. He even refused to call upon an orator (the equivalent of our lawyers). Why? Because according to Socrates:“ You cannot use your rhetorical skill to play with words, charm the crowd, perhaps even lie, even if my life is at stake.”

Socrates was the first principled thinker who taught me Intelligence is uncomfortable—this is what Socrates’ trial taught me. It is clear today the masses want illusions, not truth; they want to be flattered in a few words. Intelligent men are shamed. They are forbidden, ostracized, despised, because they disturb the dreams of the masses, question authority, and reveal the deceptions of institutions.

Charlie Kirk followed in the footsteps of history’s most dangerous men—not dangerous because they wielded armies or empires, but because they wielded questions.
Like Socrates, Charlie Kirk used reason and dialogue to expose the contradictions of a decaying society. He asked the questions that modern ideologues could not answer without betraying their own moral incoherence:

What is a woman?
When does life begin?
Can a nation survive if it hates itself?
Why are Christian values treated as hate speech?

For this, Socrates was sentenced to drink poison. For this, Charlie Kirk was hated, hunted and ultimately killed.
Kirk’s death also mirrors a deeper, eternal pattern, the one embodied in Jesus Christ, who was not crucified for violence or rebellion, but for speaking truths that threatened corrupt power: “Because I tell you the truth, you do not believe me.”

Like Christ, Charlie exposed the spiritual rot of the age. He called sin by its name. He refused to flatter the mob. And like Christ, he was not killed for what he destroyed, but for what he revealed.

At the heart of Charlie Kirk’s mission was this confrontation: the clash between objective moral truth and inter-subjective moral relativism. Objective truth holds that good and evil, right and wrong, exist independently of human opinion, cultural fashion, or political consensus. Inter-subjective morality, by contrast, is the postmodern lie that morality is just what groups agree it is, that truth is social, not eternal.

Charlie stood for the former. His enemies stood for the latter.
To them, there was no sin except “intolerance,”no truth except consensus. But consensus has justified every tyranny in history, from slavery to genocide. Consense in science is also pseudoscientific. Truth, on the other hand, judges every consensus, including our own.

It is no accident that nations like Israel and China operate on inter-subjective truth. The Chinese Communist Party and Zionism determines right and wrong not by reason or revelation, but by fiat. A man is free—or not—based on political utility. A religious gathering is virtuous—or seditious—based on state approval. Truth is not discovered—it is assigned. This is the future Kirk fought against—a future in which objective moral law is erased and replaced with the shifting sands of power.

He stood in the breach.

And he paid the price.

Charlie Kirk believed in the original idea of America—not just as a country, but as a moral proposition: that rights come from God, not government; that law is accountable to a higher law; that liberty without virtue is suicide. In defending these truths, he stood with the Founders, not with the degenerates who mock them from within the institutions they built.

And now he is gone.
But as Søren Kierkegaard wrote: “The tyrant dies and his rule is over. The martyr dies and his rule begins.”
Charlie Kirk was not a tyrant. He was a truth-teller in an age of lies. And if his voice has been silenced on earth, it will echo in the hearts of those who still believe America is worth saving—not just as a flag, but as a moral idea grounded in eternal truth.

I learned about Socrates as a young man and I think his method is built into my teaching methods. I learned about Kirk as an old man,
I understand his choice of lifestyle all too well.

There are Parallels Between Socrates and Modern Technocratic CareersIn today's technocracy, career conflicts often stem from a similar dynamic: the system rewards conformity and punishes doubt. Just as Socrates & Kirk were accused of "corrupting the youth" by encouraging critical thinking, modern workers who question centralized structures—be it Big Tech's data monopolies, corporate hierarchies, or profit-driven healthcare—risk being labeled disruptive or unemployable. For instance:

Exposing False Certainties: Socrates demolished the pretensions of politicians and sophists who peddled "fake knowledge." In technocracy, employees in tech or finance might uncover algorithmic biases, unethical AI practices, or exploitative business models, only to face retaliation. Whistleblowers like those in recent AI ethics scandals are often sidelined, echoing Socrates' fate. This breeds conflict: pursue truth and risk your career, or stay silent and lose purpose.

The Masses' Preference for Illusions: As you noted, people crave flattery over truth. In technocratic workplaces, "hustle culture" and metrics-driven success stories on social media create illusions of fulfillment, while questioning them (e.g., "Is this automation truly progress?") leads to isolation. Intelligent, reflective workers feel shamed for not buying into the hype, much like Socrates was despised for disturbing Athens' complacency.

Refusal to "Play the Game": Socrates rejected rhetoric to charm the crowd, prioritizing integrity. Similarly, in modern careers, climbing the ladder often requires networking, self-promotion, or ethical compromises—skills that clash with genuine inquiry. This leaves many conflicted, trapped between authenticity and survival in centralized systems.

These parallels highlight why careers feel so fraught: the technocracy, the Gaza genocide, like ancient Athens, is fragile when probed. It demands loyalty to institutions that concentrate power, stifling the doubt Socrates championed. But decentralization offers a way out, fostering environments where questioning becomes a strength, not a liability.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-09T02:35:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/charlie-kirks-death-had-a-lot-in-common-with-socrates-trial-and-death.31855/post-362376>

How Decentralized Medicine Transforms the Landscape

Decentralized medicine takes a similar approach, applying Socratic questioning to healthcare's centralized illusions. In technocracy, medicine is often algorithm-driven and profit-oriented, with doctors reduced to following protocols that prioritize efficiency over truth-seeking. Questioning Big Pharma or institutional dogmas can lead to professional exile, mirroring Socrates' trial. DeMed, however, decentralizes control, empowering patient-centered inquiry:

-   Questioning Institutional Deceptions: Socrates exposed false teachers; DeMed uses blockchain for transparent health data, allowing practitioners to challenge corrupt practices like overprescription or data monopolies. Careers in DeMed could involve community DAOs where doctors collaborate openly, testing treatments without corporate censorship, reducing burnout from bureaucratic illusions.
-   Fostering Ongoing Curiosity: Echoing Socrates' endless pursuit of wisdom, DeMed emphasizes holistic, evidence-based care over rigid certainties. Professionals could use decentralized platforms to crowdsource research, questioning outdated models (e.g., mitochondrial health vs. symptom suppression). This creates flexible careers, telehealth consultants or DAO healers—where doubt leads to better outcomes, not accusations of "corrupting" the system.
-   Protecting Intelligent Dissent: Centralized medicine shames whistleblowers; DeMed's distributed networks make it harder to silence critics. A doctor exposing flaws in a protocol could gain reputation in a transparent ecosystem, alleviating the conflict between ethics and job security.

By decentralizing medicine, we can create a future where Socratic intelligence, doubting, questioning, revealing—is the path to advancement, not death by hemlock, career suicide, or murder by a Zionist ideology.

Charlie was a gift, a modern enigma for the technocracy. Not just his vast knowledge, but his photographic, total recall, memory that focused discussion & pared obfuscation. He asked the zionist difficult questions right before his death, like Socrates did of Athens, and some in the polis just couldn't live with the answers to those questions. The inconvenient truth does not sit so easily alongside the ideology of zionism.

We need to bring back Classical education to our chosen schools. Grammar Logic Rhetoric, Goodness Truth and Beauty. Virtue, Order, History. Memorize large bodies of information, Imitate the masters, learn logical and civil debate where we aren't enemies, we are working toward truth.

### duckrabbit — 2025-11-09T07:07:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/charlie-kirks-death-had-a-lot-in-common-with-socrates-trial-and-death.31855/post-362399>

As another philosophy professor, I would suggest Ivan Illich's work Medical Nemesis as a philosophical guide/rationale for decentralization. Illich became famous in the 70s for his criticisms of the professionalization of society and the attempt to force citizens to be utterly reliant on classes of others for their education, healthcare, happiness. JK you might recognize him from coining the term 'iatrogenic' meaning medicine harming instead of helping. I remember reading this for the first time after having worked in a big hospital for a few years and it was like reading an old testament prophet. Pretty remarkable fella.

### duckrabbit — 2025-11-10T05:37:45+0200

If you like his work check out his essay Hospitality and Pain. I read it every couple years and get something more out of it each time. Sadly, I look at old videos of Illich back in the day speaking to rooms filled with 50-100 doctors, now I can't remember the last time I saw 100 doctors together for anything, never mind to listen to a humanities lecture. Wittgenstein wrote once, 'a philosopher is not a member of a community of thought', meaning that thinking is not about taking sides. Illich is truly an exemplification of this.

### Paul 22 — 2025-12-14T20:25:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/charlie-kirks-death-had-a-lot-in-common-with-socrates-trial-and-death.31855/post-363874>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=362375)
>
> As a student of a brilliant philosophy professor, I can tell you Charlie Kirk was a Socrates. He did more to teach America’s university students about how to have logical arguments than the totality of leftist philosophy profs combined. He offered rational dialogue radical profs offered hate. I will honor his memory by continuing to use logic to refute fascist authoritarian ideas so that students stop signing up for DEI classes and programs. One way his legacy will be honored is when students learn how to convey through logical arguments DEI really stands for "didn't earn it."
>
> Socrates' story is a profound lens for examining the discomfort and conflicts in the modern technocracy. The Athenian philosopher's trial and execution in 399 BC weren't just about personal vendettas; they exposed how societies punish those who dismantle illusions and challenge entrenched power. Socrates' relentless questioning, his Socratic method, forced people to confront their inconsistencies, exposing corrupt leaders and false wisdom. He was a threat because he instilled doubt in a centralized system that thrived on unquestioned certainties. Refusing to compromise his principles, even at the cost of his life, he embodied the idea that true intelligence disrupts comfort.
>
> Socrates, the greatest philosopher of all time, was actually the most hated man in Athens. Charlie Kirk was the most hated man in the DNC, Israel, and with RINOs.
>
> Socrates was accused of cruelty and corrupting the youth.The popular court, the Heliaia, sentenced him to death; and Socrates, one of the brightest minds in history, died by drinking hemlock.
>
> But why all the hype?
>
> Apparently, Socrates wasn’t doing anything dangerous:He asked questions, spoke with anyone, nobles, ordinary citizens, young people. But his questions, in their frankness and simplicity, demolished the certainties of his interlocutors, forcing them to confront the emptiness of their own convictions and the inconsistency of their reasoning. He taught them to doubt.
>
> Socrates was a character far too uncomfortable for the doubts he instilled. He had the audacity to expose corrupt politicians and false teachers who, believing they knew, boasted false truths and fake knowledge. That is why he was sentenced to death. He was a threat to the status quo, a danger that had to be eliminated.
>
> During the trial, Socrates refused to repent or beg for mercy. He even refused to call upon an orator (the equivalent of our lawyers). Why? Because according to Socrates:“ You cannot use your rhetorical skill to play with words, charm the crowd, perhaps even lie, even if my life is at stake.”
>
> Socrates was the first principled thinker who taught me Intelligence is uncomfortable—this is what Socrates’ trial taught me. It is clear today the masses want illusions, not truth; they want to be flattered in a few words. Intelligent men are shamed. They are forbidden, ostracized, despised, because they disturb the dreams of the masses, question authority, and reveal the deceptions of institutions.
>
> Charlie Kirk followed in the footsteps of history’s most dangerous men—not dangerous because they wielded armies or empires, but because they wielded questions.
> Like Socrates, Charlie Kirk used reason and dialogue to expose the contradictions of a decaying society. He asked the questions that modern ideologues could not answer without betraying their own moral incoherence:
>
> What is a woman?
> When does life begin?
> Can a nation survive if it hates itself?
> Why are Christian values treated as hate speech?
>
> For this, Socrates was sentenced to drink poison. For this, Charlie Kirk was hated, hunted and ultimately killed.
> Kirk’s death also mirrors a deeper, eternal pattern, the one embodied in Jesus Christ, who was not crucified for violence or rebellion, but for speaking truths that threatened corrupt power: “Because I tell you the truth, you do not believe me.”
>
> Like Christ, Charlie exposed the spiritual rot of the age. He called sin by its name. He refused to flatter the mob. And like Christ, he was not killed for what he destroyed, but for what he revealed.
>
> At the heart of Charlie Kirk’s mission was this confrontation: the clash between objective moral truth and inter-subjective moral relativism. Objective truth holds that good and evil, right and wrong, exist independently of human opinion, cultural fashion, or political consensus. Inter-subjective morality, by contrast, is the postmodern lie that morality is just what groups agree it is, that truth is social, not eternal.
>
> Charlie stood for the former. His enemies stood for the latter.
> To them, there was no sin except “intolerance,”no truth except consensus. But consensus has justified every tyranny in history, from slavery to genocide. Consense in science is also pseudoscientific. Truth, on the other hand, judges every consensus, including our own.
>
> It is no accident that nations like Israel and China operate on inter-subjective truth. The Chinese Communist Party and Zionism determines right and wrong not by reason or revelation, but by fiat. A man is free—or not—based on political utility. A religious gathering is virtuous—or seditious—based on state approval. Truth is not discovered—it is assigned. This is the future Kirk fought against—a future in which objective moral law is erased and replaced with the shifting sands of power.
>
> He stood in the breach.
>
> And he paid the price.
>
> Charlie Kirk believed in the original idea of America—not just as a country, but as a moral proposition: that rights come from God, not government; that law is accountable to a higher law; that liberty without virtue is suicide. In defending these truths, he stood with the Founders, not with the degenerates who mock them from within the institutions they built.
>
> And now he is gone.
> But as Søren Kierkegaard wrote: “The tyrant dies and his rule is over. The martyr dies and his rule begins.”
> Charlie Kirk was not a tyrant. He was a truth-teller in an age of lies. And if his voice has been silenced on earth, it will echo in the hearts of those who still believe America is worth saving—not just as a flag, but as a moral idea grounded in eternal truth.
>
> I learned about Socrates as a young man and I think his method is built into my teaching methods. I learned about Kirk as an old man,
> I understand his choice of lifestyle all too well.
>
> There are Parallels Between Socrates and Modern Technocratic CareersIn today's technocracy, career conflicts often stem from a similar dynamic: the system rewards conformity and punishes doubt. Just as Socrates & Kirk were accused of "corrupting the youth" by encouraging critical thinking, modern workers who question centralized structures—be it Big Tech's data monopolies, corporate hierarchies, or profit-driven healthcare—risk being labeled disruptive or unemployable. For instance:
>
> Exposing False Certainties: Socrates demolished the pretensions of politicians and sophists who peddled "fake knowledge." In technocracy, employees in tech or finance might uncover algorithmic biases, unethical AI practices, or exploitative business models, only to face retaliation. Whistleblowers like those in recent AI ethics scandals are often sidelined, echoing Socrates' fate. This breeds conflict: pursue truth and risk your career, or stay silent and lose purpose.
>
> The Masses' Preference for Illusions: As you noted, people crave flattery over truth. In technocratic workplaces, "hustle culture" and metrics-driven success stories on social media create illusions of fulfillment, while questioning them (e.g., "Is this automation truly progress?") leads to isolation. Intelligent, reflective workers feel shamed for not buying into the hype, much like Socrates was despised for disturbing Athens' complacency.
>
> Refusal to "Play the Game": Socrates rejected rhetoric to charm the crowd, prioritizing integrity. Similarly, in modern careers, climbing the ladder often requires networking, self-promotion, or ethical compromises—skills that clash with genuine inquiry. This leaves many conflicted, trapped between authenticity and survival in centralized systems.
>
> These parallels highlight why careers feel so fraught: the technocracy, the Gaza genocide, like ancient Athens, is fragile when probed. It demands loyalty to institutions that concentrate power, stifling the doubt Socrates championed. But decentralization offers a way out, fostering environments where questioning becomes a strength, not a liability.

Wow, you have written and voiced many brilliant points Dr Kruse, but this is at the pinnacle for me and really uncovers the deepest crisis of all next to abandoning the sun; Americans and Western culture have abandoned reason and truth and their own souls. And in doing so, God.

# Thread: YOUR FUTURE NEEDS TO BE PLANNED OUT WELL.  (BITCOIN)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-future-needs-to-be-planned-out-well-bitcoin.31856/>
**Subforum:** The Kruse Longevity Center
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-09T03:14:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-future-needs-to-be-planned-out-well-bitcoin.31856/post-362382>

A story from history.

Billionaire Daniel Keith Ludwig took extraordinary precautions before his death, having his genetic material frozen in anticipation of potential legal battles over his estate. Ludwig feared that his estranged ex-wife's daughter might contest his will, and his foresight would prove remarkably accurate decades later. Forty years after Ludwig passed away, the ex-wife’s daughter filed a lawsuit claiming a share of the billionaire’s fortune. The case hinged on a single, crucial question: was Ludwig her biological father?

Thanks to advancements in DNA testing, scientists were able to analyse the genetic material Ludwig had preserved. The results were definitive—he was not her father, and her claim to the estate was dismissed. This case demonstrates how modern science can resolve legal disputes that linger for decades. It also highlights the lengths to which wealthy individuals sometimes go to protect their estates, combining foresight with cutting-edge technology.

Ludwig’s decision to preserve his DNA ultimately ensured that the truth could be established, preventing a fraudulent claim from altering the distribution of his fortune. The story of Daniel Keith Ludwig reminds us that estate disputes are not only matters of law and finance but also questions of biology, proof, and innovation. Four decades of uncertainty ended because of a frozen sample and the power of DNA analysis, illustrating just how far science can reach into the legal system.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-09T03:19:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-future-needs-to-be-planned-out-well-bitcoin.31856/post-362383>

Your first lesson based on the history.

Scenario 1: The Cryo-Key CustodianIn a world where Bitcoin trades at $5 million per coin, an aging Bitcoin OG named Elena Vasquez, who amassed her fortune mining in the early 2010s, faces the ultimate estate planning dilemma. Her multisig wallet requires three keys: one held by her, one by a trusted family lawyer, and one embedded in a time-locked smart contract.

Fearing that her heirs—two children from different marriages—might feud over access, Elena opts for a radical solution inspired by cryogenic preservation trends.Before her death, she undergoes a non-invasive brain scan using advanced neural mapping technology, encoding a mnemonic seed phrase fragment into a digital "mind vault" stored in a decentralized neural network. This vault is programmed to unlock only upon verification of her heirs' identities through a combination of DNA sequencing and live biometric scans, cross-referenced against her preserved genetic sample.

Decades later, when Elena's body is revived from cryo-sleep (a service she funded with a small Bitcoin allocation), her consciousness interfaces directly with the wallet via neural link, confirming the transfer. But a twist emerges: one heir, suspecting the other of tampering with the scan data, challenges the process in court. Quantum-secure DNA analysis proves the integrity of the mind vault, dismissing the claim and ensuring the fortune—now worth trillions in depreciated fiat terms—passes smoothly. This case sets a precedent for "cryo-keys," blending self-custody with human longevity tech, but raises ethical questions about whether revived OGs can reclaim assets post-thaw.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-09T03:22:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-future-needs-to-be-planned-out-well-bitcoin.31856/post-362384>

The second one:

Scenario 2: The Phantom Heir Hack

By 2045, with Bitcoin at $10 million per coin, the estate of Marcus Hale, a reclusive Bitcoin OG who pioneered hardware wallet designs, becomes a battleground for digital ghosts. Marcus set up a 2-of-3 multisig where one key is held in a physical vault, another in a shamir's secret share distributed among heirs, and the third encrypted with his personal AI avatar—a sentient program trained on his lifetime of writings and videos.After his passing, a mysterious claimant emerges online, purporting to be Marcus's long-lost child from an anonymous encounter, backed by forged blockchain timestamps and deepfake videos. The claimant demands access, arguing the AI avatar recognizes them as kin based on behavioral patterns. Heirs counter by invoking the multisig's dead-man switch, which requires unanimous biometric approval. The dispute escalates to a global crypto-court, where forensic AI auditors dissect the avatar's decision logs. It turns out the phantom heir used quantum computing to brute-force a partial seed, but the avatar's embedded "loyalty protocol"—a hash of Marcus's voiceprint and DNA—rejects the claim as fraudulent. The estate is preserved, but the incident sparks a wave of "heir-verification protocols" in Bitcoin wallets, mandating pre-death genetic registries to prevent AI-manipulated inheritance scams, narrowing the margin for error as hyperinflation makes even satoshis life-changing.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-09T03:23:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-future-needs-to-be-planned-out-well-bitcoin.31856/post-362385>

Scenario 3: The Quantum Inheritance SiegeFast-forward to 2050, Bitcoin soars to $20 million amid total fiat collapse, turning early holders like Sophia Lin into de facto nation-state equivalents in wealth. Sophia, wary of quantum threats to ECDSA signatures, migrates her holdings to a post-quantum multisig wallet with keys sharded across orbital satellites, family neural implants, and a terrestrial vault.Her will stipulates that heirs must assemble the shards within a 72-hour window post-death, verified by a decentralized oracle network. When she dies, governments—desperate for revenue—launch legal sieges, claiming "national security" to seize unclaimed crypto estates. One heir, delayed by travel restrictions in a hyperinflated world, risks the timer expiring and funds burning to a dead address. In a dramatic turn, Sophia's preserved retinal scan (frozen like Ludwig's DNA) is used to activate a hidden failover: a biometric oracle that extends the window upon proving lineage via ocular genetics. Courts rule in favor, citing Bitcoin's immutable principles, but the case accelerates adoption of "inheritance DAOs"—autonomous organizations that custody keys via community governance, protecting OGs' legacies from state overreach while ensuring heirs navigate the razor-thin safety margins of ultra-valuable sats.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-09T03:23:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-future-needs-to-be-planned-out-well-bitcoin.31856/post-362386>

Scenario 4: The Mnemonic Memory MergeIn 2060, as Bitcoin hits $50 million per coin, the estate of Raj Patel, a Bitcoin OG turned philanthropist, tests the boundaries of memory and identity. Raj's multisig involves a 3-of-5 setup: keys with heirs, a charity, and one encoded in his augmented brain via neural lace—a mesh of nanobots storing data subconsciously.Fearing amnesia or cognitive decline in old age (or for heirs), Raj cryogenically preserves a full brain connectome scan before death. Years later, a disputed heir claims Raj verbally promised them a larger share, producing altered audio logs. The resolution? Heirs undergo a "memory merge" procedure, interfacing with Raj's scanned connectome to "relive" his final intentions, cross-verified against DNA-linked smart contracts. The scan reveals no such promise, invalidating the claim and releasing funds. This pioneers "mnemonic estates," where Bitcoin keys are tied to preserved consciousness data, but it ignites debates on privacy: Can heirs access a parent's thoughts without consent? As fiat vanishes, such merges become essential, tightening self-custody against the perils of forgotten phrases in a world where a single Bitcoin funds empires.These scenarios illustrate how Bitcoin's evolution could intertwine with biotech, AI, and quantum tech, amplifying both risks and safeguards for OGs. The core lesson echoes Ludwig's: foresight in preservation—be it keys, genes, or minds—will define who truly inherits in a hyper-Bitcoin future.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-09T03:57:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-future-needs-to-be-planned-out-well-bitcoin.31856/post-362388>

A Socratic Decentralized Future: Resolving Conflicts That Will Arise

Socrates' story teaches that intelligence thrives when it's free to disturb illusions, but technocracy suppresses this, fueling career angst through insecurity, purposelessness, and isolation.

Decentralization counters this by building systems resilient to doubt: transparent, autonomous, and community-driven. In this world, your blueprint for planning a private, sovereign, and safe future with extreme wealth should align with Socratic virtues, pursuing truth without fear, whether as a Decentralized innovator exposing financial hypocrisies or a DeMed practitioner challenging health dogmas.

Challenges remain in Bitcoin, like access barriers or regulatory resistance, but the potential is a society where the uber rich who will become the "most hated" become the most valued for their disruptions in society as it is forced to evolve away from fiat principles.

This vision resonates with modern thinkers linking Socrates to tech: just as he warned about writing creating illusions of knowledge, today's AI and decentralization demand we question tools to avoid blind dependence on them. That must happen in this technocratic jungle if we are to survive and thrive into the future. If we embrace this, many conflicts will evolve into opportunities for genuine flourishing in my opinion.

I have changed my lifestyle and have mostly stabilised MCAS, HIT, fibromyalgia, freeze, POTS, Pomphlyx, through supporting my circadian health among other self help tools for the last 20 months -ish. I know health is not linear, but I am finding it challenging to stabilise my HS which has multi system involvement ( skin, GI, mood, focus, etc ) which is getting worse, and continuing to have nocturia, night sweats, metabolic issues, insulin resistance, slow metabolism. ### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-09T15:53:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hidradenitis-suppurativia.31857/post-362415>

### Hidradenitis Suppurativia (HS)​

(HS) is a skin condition that begins in the eye and then can affect the gut and brain by breaking down the barrier that exists there to destroy other tissues in the body. How?

It destroys one key barrier in the eye that starts the cascade. The Bazan short loop. Then it extends to the long loop which opens the gut barriers in the liver.

SCN controls the skin clocks and that is where the defect in HS comes. Your T regulator cells cannot turn themselves into NK and myeloid cells to control your skin microbiome and Voila you have HS.

HS is an autoimmune destruction of your skin due to poor information transfer. With time here, **eventually, you’ll learn the lesson I delivered in Vermont 2017 and 2018 is at the core of your problem. Namely, that charge density and the coherence quotients of electrons and protons is how cells really transfer information to and fro. It isn’t what centrlized biology thought the answer would be. That is why your doctors have been impotent in helping people with this condition.**

# Thread: HOW BLOCK ROCK STEALS YOUR VALUE AND TIME RIGHT IN FRONT OF YOUR EYES?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-block-rock-steals-your-value-and-time-right-in-front-of-your-eyes.31862/>
**Subforum:** The Kruse Longevity Center
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-10T15:26:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-block-rock-steals-your-value-and-time-right-in-front-of-your-eyes.31862/post-362455>

BlackRock started buying American public utility companies They buy them with debt, when the purchase goes through the debt goes to the power company, not to BlackRock This means entire states power bills will instantly rise to pay off the debt. It’s a racket and it’s started

“What if I told you that your electricity bill isn't just paying for the power you use? It might also be paying off Wall Street's debt.”

“Here's how private capital works. Firms like asset management giant BlackRock don't usually buy companies with their own money. Instead, they raise giant funds from pension systems, governments, and wealthy investors.

Then they go shopping, whether it's for a factory, a toll road, even your power company, they use part equity from those investors and part debt borrowed from banks and bond markets.

Here's the catch: that debt doesn't sit on BlackRock's books. It sits on the company they just bought, and it's the company, not the asset manager, that has to pay it back.

Now, when the company is a utility, like water, gas, or electricity, it's not just any business. Utilities are monopolies.

You can't just shop around for a new power company. So, where does the money to pay back that debt come from? It comes from you, the ratepayers. In leveraged acquisitions of utilities, the bills you pay every month are the revenue stream that services Wall Street's loans.

Let's take a real case. Last month, state regulators approved a $6.2 billion buyout of ALLETE, the parent company of Minnesota Power, the state's main power utility. The buyers, Global Infrastructure Partners, which is owned by BlackRock, together with Canada's Pension Fund. A public utility that hundreds of thousands of Minnesotans rely on is shifting into the hands of private capital. Critics, like one administrative law judge who advised the acquisition be stopped because it's not in the public interest, warn that buyers are paying a big premium over the utility's actual worth here, potentially hundreds of millions above market value.

And when investors pay more, they expect to earn more back.

Where's that money gonna come from?

Well, the fear is from higher rates or cuts to services, bigger bills for you while your utilities get worse, while BlackRock collects steady fees for managing the invest

# Thread: Hello from Saskatoon, Canada, curious about Chinese Medicine/Acupuncture
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-saskatoon-canada-curious-about-chinese-medicine-acupuncture.31864/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### stuart31 — 2025-11-10T18:32:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-saskatoon-canada-curious-about-chinese-medicine-acupuncture.31864/post-362467>

Hello everyone my name is Stuart Flood. I’m a Chinese Medicine Acupuncturist located in Saskatoon, Canada. I found Dr Jack's work about 2 months ago and my head has been split wide open ever since. The timing could not have been better as I had been in the midst of researching how to help people with GI disorders. Now with this new lens of light, water and magnetism, nutrition has been put in a different context to say the least. The health picture makes so much more sense now giving plausible answers to questions like “How do ‘fit’ people get so unwell even when their diet and exercise are seemingly dialed in?”

Though now I'm still just getting started in my second year in the clinic, autoimmunity and cancer are where I plan to focus in the future with my Acupuncture practice. I’m currently in a post-graduate program studying the seminal Chinese Medicine texts from the Warring States period in China, the Huang Di Nei Jing (Yellow Emperor's Internal Classic). The teacher of the program, Ed Neal, has created a new medical model and what he terms a “unified theory of illness” called Neijing Nature Based Medicine(NNBM) from what he's learned studying these texts. As a TCM doctor and MD he has been using techniques from these texts to treat cancer and other complex diseases.

I brought up Dr Kruse's work and quantum biology to Dr Neal as I thought he'd find it fascinating. The basic themes of Decentralized Medicine and NNBM dovetail on so many levels like the importance of the sun, life is a coherence of light and the problems of artificial light/nnEMF. Unfortunately, he seemed disinterested in the connections between Chinese Medicine and things like UPE's and the regenerative current and shut it down pretty quick. He claims that UPE's are something different from quantum biology. I guess he's never heard of Fritz Popp's work on biophotonics? His lack of curiosity is quite strange to me.

Regardless I'll continue down the many rabbit holes in Dr Kruse's blogs as it seems relevant to my practice of CM, particularly as ancient people were hardly exposed to nnEMF aside from fire. Hence their stories about medicine/disease have a big gap for modern life. Putting a context on why nnEMF, artificial light, circadian disruption leads to modern disease is so important to clinical practice. Plus the implications for acupuncture treatment with how the regenerative current could help patients heal are massive. Though understandably, if people are truly unwell, they need to be in a higher quantum yield environment than the 52nd latitude... At least I could help plant that seed in people of the importance of light, water and magnetism.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-saskatoon-canada-curious-about-chinese-medicine-acupuncture.31864/post-362544>

# Thread: Pregnancy ultrasounds?

### K-in-Oz — 2025-11-14T08:24:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pregnancy-ultrasounds.31882/post-362629>

Is it advisable to avoid pregnancy ultrasounds when there is no indication of issues?
What are the risks of getting/not getting one?
If anyone here is able to share their experience I would appreciate it.
Have had one pregnancy in the past and got several ultrasounds throughout (had gestational diabetes - I did not know anything about light or nnemfs at the time.)
I am currently planning for next pregnancy

### Mat.I — 2025-11-14T11:02:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pregnancy-ultrasounds.31882/post-362633>

I would personally avoid. Some will get none some who want to avoid it but want some assurances will get the ~ 19 to 20 week anatomy scan (but a standard US not 3d or 4d and keep it short). Each case if different especially if there are risk factors and indications for monitoring things.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-14T16:35:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pregnancy-ultrasounds.31882/post-362644>

If you and your eggs are high risk or bang someone with high risk sperm, you might want to carefully look at your relative and absolute risk of this decision tree.

### Anthony Smith — 2025-11-14T19:48:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pregnancy-ultrasounds.31882/post-362651>

Our decision tree when pregnant:
If they were to find a “condition” as a result of a test and the only “fix” was an abortion - we did NOT get the test.

### Anthony Smith — 2025-11-15T01:25:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pregnancy-ultrasounds.31882/post-362671>

My wife was 37 when our first was born and 38 when our twins were born. Yes, three in 18 months!
There were concerns voiced by Dr’s with both pregnancies but we stuck it out.
We have three wonderful children now having children of their own.

### Mat.I — 2025-11-15T02:21:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pregnancy-ultrasounds.31882/post-362673>

Our first was in 2018 and second was 2020. My Wife was 31 at our first. We went through a mid wifery program. She was pushed for booking a C section by the Dr due to having to undergo an emergency C section for the first birth which I put down negligence and being talked into a sweep (bad choice).. You live and learn. So back to the second (my son) she ignored what the Dr was saying in.absolute terms about scar tissue and rupture etc.. My Son was born naturally (quick birth within 30 to 40mins). No one knows everything and he had nothing to say other than well done.. what is convenient for people working in.the hospitals (if we agreed to booking the C section) could cause major issues.. Sometimes you just have to listen to your gut and go by feel..

### Mat.I — 2025-11-15T02:24:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pregnancy-ultrasounds.31882/post-362674>

Different world now post 2019/2020 though.. No doubt we will see lots of issues thrown in.the normal basket due.to.the control group they are measuring from.. Everything is "normal" these days and people buy that garbage..

Me with allergies/intolerances, mental health challenges, and what I'm now inclined to think of as EMF sensitivity (periods of dizziness, palpitations, weakness/fatigue, facial flushing etc). Hi Mat, thanks for your responses. I'm thinking perhaps at least 1 or 2 ultrasounds may be better off on the agenda given our health statuses but I will have to do some thinking and research. We are 32 (me) and 37 (him). Me with allergies/intolerances (including MCS), mental health challenges, and I believe EMF sensitivity (periods of dizziness, palpitations, weakness/fatigue, facial flushing etc). Him with more advanced stuff, a few autoimmune conditions as well as being approx 6 years post liver transplant and on immunosuppresants. My firstborn came in 2021 and I was 28 at the time - I had gestational diabetes, ended up injecting insulin towards the end of the pregnancy and had an induced labour and high blood pressure during labour (this was 4+ years ago and I had no knowledge of emf/light/vaccine related stuff at the time). Baby came out fast and not breathing, code blue - quickly rectified though. Kiddos health challenges have been intense temperament/highly emotionally dysregulated (but developmentally regular in terms of physical/mental capacities etc) and ongoing issue with congestion/inflammation of adenoids/tonsils that we have been working on.

Theres a lot I will do very differently with the next pregnancy from diet, to staying away from emfs as much as possible, mitigating artificial light and getting lots of natural light. First preg was during covid and I was working from home so always close to a device. But naturally despite all the efforts I'm making now, I'm under no illusions that I am magically a perfectly healthy specimen haha.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-18T17:09:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pregnancy-ultrasounds.31882/post-362804>

Ultrasound initially causes a temporary narrowing of the exclusion zone (EZ) water layer, immediately followed by a
significant, rebound expansion to several times its original size. This effect is considered a way to promote EZ growth. The effect is thought to be a hormetic response, where a low dose of a stressor (ultrasound) triggers a beneficial reaction. The formation, growth, and violent collapse of tiny bubbles (cavitation bubbles) in the water generate localized high temperatures, pressures, and shock waves. This energy release happens in the form of lightand can cause mechanical stress and microstreaming (tiny, rapid fluid movements). Many believe the light release of the bubbles could be harmful to a fetus who is using very precise UPE signaling to grow out its body plan. No data I know of confirms this. We do know bubbles release light when they pop.

The mechanical forces, including shear stress and shock waves produced by the collapsing bubbles, may temporarily disrupt the initial EZ structure. The subsequent, significant expansion of the EZ suggests that the system reacts to this disruption by actively rebuilding and expanding the EZ layer, which in turn increases the charge separation and potential energy stored in the water. I believe this is because the EZ water is reacting to the released UPEs from bubble rupture.

Many believe falsely that ultrasound is safe. In pregnancy I do not think it is. I understand why its used but when its used should be well thought out by OBs but they are among the most clueless in centralized medicine.

A developing fetus is not an injury in a post natal human. This EZ growth effect is hypothesized by unwise influencers to be a mechanism by which therapeutic ultrasound can aid in processes like tissue repair, by increasing the body's potential energy and promoting organized water structures. They equate injury repair with good. They never think about the context of a fetus and how it uses quantiuzed UPEs to undergo morphogenesis.

# Thread: ALL THINGS GOOGLE.......
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/all-things-google.31890/>
**Subforum:** The Kruse Longevity Center
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-17T04:36:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/all-things-google.31890/post-362727>

Know youre enemy to become their enemy. [https://x.com/maniaUFO/status/1989738049925005348](https://x.com/maniaUFO/status/1989738049925005348)

Everyone needs to read The Art of War and use it against this company at every level.

### Anthony Smith — 2025-11-17T06:16:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/all-things-google.31890/post-362729>

Having traveled extensively my whole life I have noticed (experienced) the different world views held by different peoples.
Everyone’s opinions about everything comes from the information they have (and how it was presented to them).
Coming from England I had the English view of the world (thank you BBC) but then I joined British Airways (BA) and found myself in a different country every week. Traveling to the USA regularly I noticed how differently American TV reported “the news”.
Americans view of the world and their opinion of their place in the world is VERY different than the world‘s view of America and Americans.
Same can be said of ALL countries.
Google has taken this from the national level down to the next level, the personal/individual level.
I have no doubt even all of here have been affected to a certain degree BUT I expect most of us here have been affected less than most people out there (or we would not be here).
Use a different browser Stay conscious, stay independent, stay decentralized.

### Szen — 2025-11-23T16:36:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/salt-or-no-salt.31920/post-363038>

Understood, I see where my mistake came from, it was from a misreading in this [Ask Jack thread](https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/redox-rx-blog-and-salt-in-water.31331).

[Redox RX blog](https://jackkruse.com/redox-rx/) from old website.

> 6\. **If your redox level is low you should avoid all alcohol no matter what**. You should also **avoid black and red tea and salt** because both are loaded with fluoride salts (F).
> \[...\]
> 7\. **Use [reverse osmosis water](http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B005HG9ERW/ref=as_li_qf_sp_asin_il_tl?ie=UTF8&camp=1789&creative=9325&creativeASIN=B005HG9ERW&linkCode=as2&tag=jackru-20), [spring water](http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B0069FTP0G/ref=as_li_qf_sp_asin_il_tl?ie=UTF8&camp=1789&creative=9325&creativeASIN=B0069FTP0G&linkCode=as2&tag=jackru-20), or [mineral water](http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B005HG9ERW/ref=as_li_qf_sp_asin_il_tl?ie=UTF8&camp=1789&creative=9325&creativeASIN=B005HG9ERW&linkCode=as2&tag=jackru-20) for your drinking water consistently.** Avoid all fluoride products, drugs, and pesticides.
> \[...\]
> **Avoid any exogenous salts in your drinking water if you can**. This is not an axiomatic rule. Why? **Salts.** In addition to metals, salts can also conduct electricity. In cells we do not want a salt disturbing semiconducting flow of electrons or protons. This creates loss of efficiency for energy transfers because it creates a _**smaller exclusion zone**_ in water in a cell. In salt electric conduction, there are no free electrons moving, so the conductivity depends on the specific ions used in the salt. When a salt is in water, it is either melting or dissolving, so that the ions of the salt are free to move to conduct the current. Their atomic sizes however limit coherent conduction with 100 efficiency that is seen in a semiconductor. K+ is the key element in life's equation with water to create a semiconductor that has the same properties we see in a topologic insulators.

Underlines are my own emphasis. Putting it here so the next person searching this information doesn't make the same mistake as me.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-25T15:24:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/peptide-use-and-why-you-should-not-use-them.31924/post-363147>

[https://x.com/DrJackKruse/status/1992781404036305231](https://x.com/DrJackKruse/status/1992781404036305231)

### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-25T15:25:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/peptide-use-and-why-you-should-not-use-them.31924/post-363148>

Does all electricity use generate radiation? Yes, it is true. All electricity use from our power grid generates radiation, specifically extremely low-frequency (ELF) electromagnetic fields (EMF) from the movement of electric current in power lines, wiring, and devices. This radiation, which includes both electric and magnetic fields, is a form of non-ionizing radiation and is present whenever current flows, from household appliances to high-tension power lines. Sources of radiation Power lines and wiring: Any electrical conductor carrying current, including overhead power lines and the wiring in your home, produces EMF. Household appliances: Almost all electrical devices generate ELF-EMF when turned on, including refrigerators, vacuum cleaners, hair dryers, and computers. Other electronic devices: Other products, such as televisions and some smart devices, also emit radiation. Electrical current: The radiation is generated by the acceleration and deceleration of electrons as a current flows through a circuit. Is all this radiation generated polrized? Yes, virtually all electricity use generates electromagnetic radiation (EMR). Moreover, this man-made radiation is polarized extremely low frequency (ELF) radiation. Polarization of the Radiation Man-made EMR is Polarized: Unlike most natural EMR (such as sunlight), which is unpolarized (oscillates in random orientations), the EMR from human-made electrical sources is polarized. Fixed Orientation: Man-made EMR is produced by electrons oscillating in a specific, forced direction along wires or within circuits. This gives the electric and magnetic fields a defined, consistent plane of oscillation (linear, circular, or elliptical polarization depending on the source geometry, e.g., 3-phase power lines produce circularly/elliptically polarized fields). Biological Implications: Some scientists theorize that the polarized nature of man-made EMFs, rather than their intensity, might be a key factor in their potential biological effects, as this coordinated oscillation can force charged molecules within biological tissues to move in a specific, unnatural way. Health agencies such as the World Health Organization (WHO) and the National Cancer Institute (NCI)continue to study potential health effects, classifying ELF fields as "possibly carcinogenic to humans (group 2B)" based on some epidemiological studies suggesting an increased risk of childhood leukemia.

# Thread: Iron in food does not cause diabetes LIGHT DOES.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iron-in-food-does-not-cause-diabetes-light-does.31925/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet

══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-23T15:05:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iron-in-food-does-not-cause-diabetes-light-does.31925/post-363029>

I am so sick of food gurus who get it wrong ALL THE TIME. ANSWER The Real Driver of Iron-Related Metabolic Disease: Light, Not Steak Heme synthesis is a mitochondrial, light-gated, circadian process that begins with ALAS1 in the matrix and is exquisitely timed by the solar cycle (peak expression ZT10 → peak heme ZT12 → peak HO-1/CO ZT18).

Rev-Erbα, the nuclear receptor that represses gluconeogenesis, requires heme as its endogenous ligand to bind NCOR and shut down PEPCK/G6Pase. Carbon monoxide (CO) from heme oxygenase-1 then inhibits CLOCK/BMAL1 DNA binding at night, completing the rhythmic brake on glucose production.

Dietary iron simply supplies raw material for this ancient light-controlled oscillator, it does not perturb periodicity, only amplitude. The moment you superimpose artificial light at night (ALAN) or blue-rich daytime light (melanopsin dysfunction), you destroy circadian ALAS1/HO-1 timing via retinal–SCN–hepatic axis collapse,

Prevent heme–Rev-Erbα repressor formation,
Block physiologic CO production,
Unleash unchecked CLOCK/BMAL1-driven gluconeogenesis and lipogenesis.

Result: hyperglycemia, **_fatty liver,_** and T2DM, even on a zero-carb, high-iron carnivore diet eaten under LEDs after sunset.

Ask Suzanne Humphries if this doesn't work. I dare you. She is way wiser than boy wonder Nick.

Neonatal blue-light phototherapy for jaundice (now known to increase childhood melanoma risk) is the perfect microcosm: we are literally programming infants with the exact light signal that deranges heme–CO–Rev-Erbα timing for life.

The carnivore community’s fear of “iron overload causing diabetes” is therefore backwards. Iron is not the problem, blue light and ALAN are the toxins that break the 2.4-billion-year-old contract between sunlight, heme, and the circadian clock built in the GOE.

Eat your ribeye at solar noon under real sunlight, and iron becomes the most potent circadian enhancer on the planet.

Eat it at 10 PM under 450 nm LEDs, and you’ve just re-enacted the metabolic catastrophe of a modern human with melanopsin dysfunction.

Light trumps food.
Every.
Single.
Time.

This is the nail in the coffin of nutrient-centric thinking.

Y'all should be thanking me for handing you the hammer of truth in this bullshit story.

# Thread: Does all electricity use generate radiation?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-all-electricity-use-generate-radiation.31934/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-25T04:13:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-all-electricity-use-generate-radiation.31934/post-363120>

Does all electricity use generate radiation?

Yes, it is true. All electricity use from our power grid generates radiation, specifically extremely low-frequency (ELF) electromagnetic fields (EMF) from the movement of electric current in power lines, wiring, and devices. This radiation, which includes both electric and magnetic fields, is a form of non-ionizing radiation and is present whenever current flows, from household appliances to high-tension power lines.

Sources of radiation

-   [Power lines](https://www.google.com/search?q=Power+lines&client=safari&sca_esv=9d19827db9edfb9f&sxsrf=AE3TifO-9nUQfBfJ_Rj9h5GaUklwL9xm0A%3A1764036398743&source=hp&ei=Lg8laZv8KqyPwbkPyOGo4Qc&iflsig=AOw8s4IAAAAAaSUdPiM2fip6IizMK7cluqrj_zLpOlbq&ved=2ahUKEwiAsfHim4yRAxXBUjABHarmGJ0QgK4QegQIAxAB&uact=5&oq=does+all+electricity+use+generate+radiation%3F&gs_lp=Egdnd3Mtd2l6Iixkb2VzIGFsbCBlbGVjdHJpY2l0eSB1c2UgZ2VuZXJhdGUgcmFkaWF0aW9uPzIIEAAYFhgKGB4yCxAAGIAEGIYDGIoFMgsQABiABBiGAxiKBTILEAAYgAQYhgMYigUyCxAAGIAEGIYDGIoFMgsQABiABBiGAxiKBTIFEAAY7wUyBRAAGO8FMgUQABjvBUiSqQFQAFi3ogFwAngAkAEAmAHQAaAB-&sclient=gws-wiz&mstk=AUtExfB_rBk8rtQkQeitqS0HvZc5ggGx5IS6_enfAM0T6IbwhHf-Wu5MB5KP6PPiDV7ObQjnrgrlPiOJEN7fplRYJ9Zqu1Q96kR4mbYtXNWQMO3znsL6W8HpNWsCTwev8tGa_Ho6Wvb6mafTsFtIbypisVaV3SRq7IsMCSJ3FtkKruHT-qU&csui=3) and wiring: Any electrical conductor carrying current, including overhead power lines and the wiring in your home, produces EMF.
-   Household appliances: Almost all electrical devices generate ELF-EMF when turned on, including refrigerators, vacuum cleaners, hair dryers, and computers.
-   Other electronic devices: Other products, such as televisions and some smart devices, also emit radiation.
-   Electrical current: The radiation is generated by the acceleration and deceleration of electrons as a current flows through a circuit.

Is all this radiation generated polarized?

Yes, virtually all electricity use generates electromagnetic radiation (EMR).
Moreover, this man-made radiation is polarized extremely low frequency (ELF) radiation.

Polarization of the Radiation

-   Man-made EMR is Polarized: Unlike most natural EMR (such as sunlight), which is unpolarized (oscillates in random orientations), the EMR from human-made electrical sources is polarized. Melanin is the only protein capable of handling polarized light.
-   Fixed Orientation: Man-made EMR is produced by electrons oscillating in a specific, forced direction along wires or within circuits. This gives the electric and magnetic fields a defined, consistent plane of oscillation (linear, circular, or elliptical polarization depending on the source geometry, e.g., 3-phase power lines produce circularly/elliptically polarized fields).
-   Biological Implications: Some scientists theorize that the polarized nature of man-made EMFs, rather than their intensity, might be a key factor in their potential biological effects, as this coordinated oscillation can force charged molecules within biological tissues to move in a specific, unnatural way. Health agencies such as the [World Health Organization (WHO)](https://www.who.int/news-room/questions-and-answers/item/radiation-electromagnetic-fields) and the [National Cancer Institute (NCI)](https://www.cancer.gov/about-cancer/causes-prevention/risk/radiation/electromagnetic-fields-fact-sheet)continue to study potential health effects, classifying ELF fields as "possibly carcinogenic to humans (group 2B)" based on some epidemiological studies suggesting an increased risk of childhood leukemia.

### knees4bukele — 2025-11-30T18:58:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-all-electricity-use-generate-radiation.31934/post-363398>

> [urko said:](/goto/post?id=363359)
>
> I remember one of the last Q&A I listened somebody asked you about a land he purchased in ES that he was going to install solar panels + batteries... You mentioned to put all 1000 yards far away from the place you live. I'm curious about the solar panels by themselves why are them so bad? I'm pretty aware about the inverters but with the Gauss meter we have [GQ EMF 390](https://lessemf.com/product/gq-emf-390-combination-meter/) and I have not noticed anything there I hope is not shitty meter and I have to purchase new one to detect that

The main problem with solar is the inversion from DC to AC. This creates high frequency transients (dirty electricity) that ride on the base AC frequency. Most electronics also invert from AC back to DC using switch mode power supplies, which create additional high frequency transients.

Keeping it DC only gets rid of the inverter problem and is much more efficient, as you no longer need to do two inversions to power your electronics.

I like doing small 12V DC solar systems as you can find a lot of electronics/appliances that run on 12V DC.
For example: My computer, modem, and wired ethernet router can all run directly off of a 12V DC battery with no step-up or step-down conversions needed. I also have a small fridge that runs on 12V DC as well. There's lots of options out there.

12V DC wiring is super simple to learn as well for the DIY type.

> [Anthony Smith said:](/goto/post?id=363422)

> The “DE” in most homes with a problem still measures less than 1.2V (1,200mV) < 1% of the 120V
> The elephant in the nnEMF room is the 60Hz (50Hz) electric field from the 120V (240V UK etc) in the walls, floor and ceiling radiating out 6 - 8 feet in all directions that the “DE” is 1%+/- part.

Just curious what measuring equipment you are using to get the 1.2V number?

Current flow increases at higher frequencies and the impedance of the skin also decreases when the frequency increases., so even though the DE might be a small percentage of the 120V 60hz/50hz electric field, there is still a large potential for negative effects.

At the end of the day, the best solution is to cut the AC power completely, as you've mentioned.

### BruceKing — 2025-11-27T14:48:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-it-might-be-going-down.31938/post-363254>

J. Christopher Giancarlo, former Chairman of the CFTC -
In a 2019 interview about the 2017 bubble, Giancarlo described how U.S. regulators viewed Bitcoin futures as a way to deflate the mania:
He told CoinDesk that the CFTC, Treasury, SEC, and NEC (Gary Cohn) “believed that the launch of bitcoin futures would have the impact of popping the bitcoin bubble. And it worked.” Institutional capital dominates. Large players can influence direction. Arbitrage forces spot markets to follow futures (whichever way they want to rig it). The U.S. establishes control over a decentralized asset.
They have done the same thing with silver for a very long time. The main difference is that you need silver to make stuff and it keeps getting "destroyed", BTC is only a store of value with a hard cap that keeps getting mined and not destroyed. I think this makes silver harder to manipulate in paper/synthetic markets because eventually you have to deliver a tangible asset that is getting costlier to recover from the earth. We are kind of seeing this right now in the silver market as December delivery month is one of the highest in history and the LBMA recently ran out of silver to deliver. Not financial advice and I don't have a crystal ball but hard assets that are needed by industry might be short term better. Still like BTC as a decentralized currency, though.

### BruceKing — 2025-11-28T22:39:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-it-might-be-going-down.31938/post-363335>

They don’t need to confiscate Bitcoin.
They **already** control Bitcoin the same way they control every asset that threatens the existing power structure: wrap it in a cash-settled futures market, drown it in synthetic supply, and dictate price without ever touching the underlying.

It’s the same script they executed on silver for 40+ years —
**but they made one fatal mistake this time: silver is not Bitcoin.**

Bitcoin doesn’t build a single physical object on this planet.
No factory stops running if Bitcoin disappears.
That’s why BTC’s price can be puppeteered indefinitely with paper.

Silver has no such weakness.

Silver is the highest-conductivity metal known to man — an _industrial keystone_ of the modern world. Solar panels, EVs, medical tech, optics, 5G hardware, satellites, water purification, AI server farms — all of them require **physical silver atoms**. Not “unallocated accounts.” Not “warehouse receipts.” Not “credits.”
Actual. Physical. Metal.

And here’s where the nuclear core goes critical:

### The physical silver shortage is NOT hypothetical.​

It’s happening right now — in real time.\*\*

-   **COMEX registered silver has collapsed** from over 150 million oz to around 70 million.
-   **LBMA vaults have lost nearly half their silver** in just a few years.
-   Industrial demand is at **all-time highs** and accelerating.
-   Global inventories are being **drained simultaneously** by industry, sovereign mints, and private stackers.
-   Ore grades have plunged 87% in the last century.
-   70% of new silver is a byproduct — meaning **the supply cannot scale even if the price explodes**.

This is a _mathematical impossibility_ for the suppression system.

COMEX trades **hundreds of ounces of paper silver for every one ounce in the vaults** —
a **fractional-reserve metals system** that only functions as long as nobody asks for the bars.

But the industrial sector _is_ asking.
They need silver to build actual stuff.
And they can’t run factories on a COMEX contract.

### **This is the exact failure mode that blew up the London Gold Pool in 1968.**​

Too many claims, not enough metal. Sound familiar?

JP Morgan literally paid **the largest manipulation fines in U.S. history** for rigging precious metals — but even they can’t conjure atoms out of thin air. The paper system is now being forced to answer to geology, and geology doesn’t negotiate. Remember, Mother Nature is a ruthless bitch.

Bitcoin can be suppressed forever because derivatives can outnumber coins endlessly.

Silver can’t because you can’t cheat physics.

And here’s the part that should terrify the PTB:

### Once industry competes with investors for physical, the entire LBMA/COMEX complex goes supercritical.​

Not reprice — _fail_.

When one link breaks — a missed delivery, a failed rollover, a refinery declaring force majeure — the paper market goes the way of Lehman: slowly at first, then all at once.

Not financial advice, but…

Silver has a _minimum_ two-year industrial supercycle baked in —
and the blast wave has already begun.

### BruceKing — 2025-12-06T18:54:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-it-might-be-going-down.31938/post-363639>

Have to backpedal a little on some of my previous views. After careful consideration that I might be wrong I need to clarify that there probably will not be any kind hyperinflation + complete banking collapse + global monetary reset. Realistically, this is highly unlikely. I think we will continue with the slow erosion of our purchasing power with accelerations and slowdowns. For hyperinflation we need to have: 1) A productivity collapse. Manufacturing is returning to the U.S. and the dollar is weakening incentivizing manufacturers to produce and sell products here and abroad (hyperinflation requires too much money chasing too few products) and export from the U.S. 2) Money supply expanding massively with velocity (got to have velocity to have hyperinflation). Boomers are retiring and spending less, fewer children from more recent generations who are already deep in debt, tends to slow spending. 3) The FED wants to survive so they are now more target liquidity oriented and more inflation sensitive (yes, I know lowering interest rates into inflation is not that inflation sensitive, but history shows they like to cycle this stuff with loose then tight monetary policy). 4) The government would never allow a massive banking collapse (voters would put politicians out on their ass when their retirement accounts are wiped out). Yes, the too big to fail banks will rip us off, again, privatize profits and socialize losses.
More later.

I latched onto the Rome analogy. In some ways Rome's slow decline punctuated by crisis is similar to the U.S. and in some ways it is not. We all know about currency debasement but many do not figure in the fact that the U.S. has vast energy reserves, tons of minerals, massive agriculture, gigantic forest, friendly neighbors (more or less), and ocean between us and hostile countries (greatest navy on earth), not to mention that under Trump we are re-industrializing our country which stabilizes currency and adds wages. Now, how much AI and robots are going to disenfranchise workers remains to be seen, but it should be balanced by more new manufacturing and increased productivity. There are forces pulling in both directions, so I can't really agree with Glen Beck's assessment, it just ends up being clickbait when you actually look at the details. Have to agree on the banking cartel getting what they want, but we kind of know what they want, to continue the game they are already playing. A major crisis really doesn't benefit anyone.

### BruceKing — 2025-12-19T18:39:06+0200

Interesting article on Strategy Inc. (Micro-strategy) MSTR. [https://www.investors.com/news/bitc...e=aflMarketWatch&mod=mw\_quote\_news\_topstories](https://www.investors.com/news/bitcoin-price-strategy-buying-mstr-stock-issuance/?src=A00619&refcode=aflMarketWatch&mod=mw_quote_news_topstories)
Basically it says they borrowed money and bought a lot of bitcoin in a bit of a scramble to avoid the "death spiral". Unfortunately, it didn't work very well as far as getting people on board and driving the price up. MSTR's almost 2 billion purchase in first two weeks of December was only only about 0.1% of bitcoin's current 1.76 trillion price cap. So, not that surprising that 0.1% doesn't have much effect on the price. MSTR is still a very profitable company and up on the day as I write this. And, their debt-to-equity ratio of around 0.14–0.16 at current bitcoin price is admirable. However, if bitcoin continues to drop this great leverage ratio is amplified hyperbolically and might make it harder for them to borrow and buy. I am looking at their cut off point in the price of bitcoin at around $75,000 right now. If it goes below that MSTR will be underwater and their ability to borrow and buy might be severely impaired. Of course, they are not the only player, but they are one of the big ones. All this to say, I think I will revise my buy in number for bitcoin at around $50,000 instead of $60,000. especially, if bitcoin drops below 75,000 (Not financial advice). HODL'ers can just hang on for the ride, but I am older and don't have time to wait out a big correction. Merry Christmas!

Tamoxifen affects melanin production by subtly increasing it and has been linked to both skin and hair pigmentation change.
